chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

vii 
COURSE DATA

1. Course Title. T-44C E-2D Intermediate Multi-Service Pilot Training System (MPTS) Flight 
Training Curriculum.

2. Course ID Number (CIN). Q-2A-6175.

3. Location. Naval Air Station, Corpus Christi, Texas 78419.

4. Course Status. Active.

5. Course Mission. The mission of E-2D Intermediate MPTS is to develop multi-engine flight 
skills while emphasizing instrument flying and crew coordination. At the successful completion 
of this phase of aviation training, the student will enter E-2D Advanced MPTS training.

6. Prerequisite Training. Successful completion of:

a. T-6B Joint Primary Pilot Training (JPPT ), (TW4: Q-2A-3166 or TW5: Q-2A-4166) or 
T-6B Primary Pilot Training, Naval Aviation Training Next (Avenger) Syllabus 
(TW4: Q-2A-8195 or TW5: Q-2A-9195) or,

b. T-6B Joint Primary Pilot Tr aining (JPPT), CNATRAINST 1542.166D.

7. Security Clearance Required. None.

8. Follow-on Training. As required by Chief of Naval Air Training (CNATRA) for each 
specific mission assignment.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE

9. Course Length. Overall time-to-train is calculated as per the CNATRAINST 
1550.6G. Training Days account directly or provide margin for factors including weather, 
personnel and equipment availability, briefing and preparation time, and historical delays. 
Production Weeks further account for weekends, holidays, safety stand-downs, and other 
expected 
nonworking days throughout the year.

Training Days Production Weeks

T-44C E-2D Intermediate MPTS 85.2 19.0

10. Class Capacity. Variable.

11. Instructor Requirements. As established by Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) planning 
factors.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

x 
16. Training Preparation Time. In addition to the hours formally planned for classes, simulators, 
and flights, significant additional time to prepare and study should be expected outside of 
scheduled training hours. This range will vary depending on the complexity of the material and 
individual student needs, and may be up to several hours per event. For simulator and flight 
events, specific brief and taxi times will be programmed into T-SHARP and accounted for on the 
flight schedule, per the following table:

ADDITIONAL FORMAL TRAINING TIME PER EVENT 
Training Area 
Brief/ 
Preflight/ 
Taxi 
Taxi/ 
Debrief Total 
Flight 2.25 1.0 3.25 
Simulator/CPT Dual Event 1.0 1.0 2.0 
Simulator/CPT Single Event 0.5 0.5 1.0

17. Physical Requirements. As specified in the Manual of the Medical Department 
(NAVMED P-117) and all applicable anthropometric standards.

18. Obligated Service. Refer to MILPERSMAN for Naval personnel.

19. Primary Instructional Methods. Lecture, computer aided instruction (CAI), self- and 
group-paced study, and in-flight instruction.

20. Preceding Curriculum Data. This curriculum replaces the E-2D curriculum in 
CNATRAINST 1542.175C.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
21. Student Performance Measurement and Application of Standards. The standards outlined in 
Chapter IX, Course Training Standards, are used to evaluate Student Naval Aviator (SNA) 
performance of individual items and maneuvers. Final judgment regarding the satisfactory 
performance of any flight maneuver rests with the Instructor Pilot who must assess the 
environmental and systems factors affecting the conditions under which the performance is 
measured and the SNA’s experience within the stage.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

xi 
GLOSSARY

1. Advancing X. Completed event within the normal syllabus flow. Excludes events with last 
characters in the range 84-89.

2. Aviation Training Form (ATF). A grade sheet documenting SNA performance for all 
categories of training regardless of media, phase, or stage.

3. Aviation Training Jacket (ATJ). The ATJ is the SNA’s training record. It contains ATFs, 
calendar card, grade reports, and all other associated training information. It is filed in student 
control and follows the SNA through all phases of training.

4. Aviation Training Summary (ATS). A tabular sheet listing the MIF and maneuver grades 
within a training stage.

5. Block of Training. A sequential series of lessons within a training stage sharing an identical 
MIF. The third character in the lesson designator identifies a block.

6. Check Flight (SXX90). A flight check in any stage of training.

7. Course of Training. The entire program of preflight, flight, simulation, academics, and 
officer development conducted in all media during the programmed training days.

8. Course Training Standard (CTS). A description of required behaviors and standards of 
performance for a specific maneuver. These standards are in Chapter IX.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
9. Courseware. The technical data, flight training instructions, audio, video, film, CAI, 
instructor guides, student study guides, and other training material developed to support and 
implement the syllabus of instruction.

10. Critical Item. Any maneuver coded with a plus sign (+). This symbol indicates the 
maneuver is required and must be accomplished to the specified standard in that block of 
training.

11. Commanding Officer Progress Check (CO-PC) (SXX89). A special check normally given 
by the CO or XO. The CO may designate, in writing, CO-PC duty to a qualified O-4 or above. 
This is only done if the CO or XO is unqualified or unavailable to instruct in the required stage. 
A satisfactory CO-PC returns the student to normal syllabus flow. An UNSAT CO-PC results in 
a TRB.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

xii 
12. Deliverables. A CNATRA 1542/1827 (Rev. 4-04) Training Review Board Summary Form, 
generated by the TRB, which summarizes a specific SNA’s progress in a given syllabus and 
provides detailed information on the application of MPTS training for that SNA. Deliverables 
indicate whether the quality and continuity of training provided was per CNATRAINST 
1542.175D and indicate the degree of influence by “human factors” on the SNA’s performance.

13. Demonstrate. Instructor performs the maneuver with precision and accompanying 
description. SNA is responsible for knowledge of the procedures prior to event brief and 
observes the maneuver.

14. Emergency Procedures (EP). Any degradation of aircraft systems or flight conditions 
requiring pilot action or intervention.

15. End of Block (EOB). Last event in block. In order to progress past EOB, the SNA must 
meet or exceed MIF on all critical items, and all optional items attempted, by the end of the 
block. Flight shall consist of a cross-section of critical items; however, all critical items do not 
have to be accomplished on the last flight in block as long as MIF had been previously met.

16. Extra Training (SXX87). Additional SNA training flights ordered by the Commanding 
Officer, or higher, in order to compensate for documented instructional deficiencies.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
17. Familiarization. The stage of training that includes both day and night familiarization.

18. Flight Training Instruction (FTI). A CNATRA-approved manual describing flight 
procedures and techniques for each training stage.

19. Hours per X (H/X). The average length for each event in a block, rounded to the nearest 
tenth of an hour.

20. Initial Progress Check (IPC) (SXX88). A special check given by the most experienced 
instructors that have a complete understanding of NATRACOM and PC processes and 
understand the gravity of their responsibility in helping maintain the standards of Naval 
Aviation. These instructors should carry the CO’s full confidence and shall be designated in 
writing by the CO. A satisfactory IPC returns the student to normal syllabus flow. An IPC can 
count as an Advancing “X” at the CO’s discretion. An UNSAT IPC results in a CO-PC. IPC 
events shall be coded as XX88, (e.g., OPS3188).

21. Introduce. Instructor coaches the SNA through the maneuver as necessary and/or may 
demonstrate the maneuver again. The SNA is responsible for knowledge of the procedures prior 
to the event brief and for performing the maneuver with coaching.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

xiii 
22. Lesson Designator. All syllabus events have a five-character lesson designator in the 
following format:

Char Meaning Remarks 
1st Stage G – Ground 
FAM – 
Familiarization 
I – Instrument 
F – Formation

2nd Media 0 or 1 – Ground 
Training/Flight 
Support 
2 – CPT 
3 – Simulator 
4 – Aircraft

3rd Block Sequential, indicat ing block within stage. 
4th 
 & 
5th 
Event/Check 
Identifier 
Sequential, indicating event within block, or other event types as 
shown below: 
84 – Adaptation 
85 – Practice Sim 
86 – Warmup 
87 – Extra Training 
88 – IPC 
89 – CO-PC 
90 – Check Flight/Exam

23. Maneuver Item File (MIF). A listing of required maneuvers and associated proficiency 
levels for each block of training.

24. Master Syllabus. Chapters I-VII list all training syllabus activities, prerequisites, and 
training flow for MPTS.

25. Naval Air Training Class Advisor Program Officer. An Instructor Pilot assigned to provide 
counseling and guidance to a specific student pilot or pilots throughout the applicable syllabus.

26. Off-Wing Flight. A Familiarization flight not flown with the SNA’s on-wing.

27. On-Wing. The SNA’s assigned instructor in the Familiarization stage per 
CNATRAINST 1500.4L.

28. Phase of Training. A major division in the course of training. MPTS consists of three 
phases: Primary, Intermediate, and Advanced.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
29. Pink ATF. A standard ATF that is printed on pink paper. The pink ATF is used to denote 
an UNSAT.

30. Practice. Instructor observes SNA with minimal coaching; may also demonstrate the 
maneuver if necessary. The SNA must perform maneuver with minimal coaching.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

xiv 
31. Progress Check Pilot. An Instructor Pilot authorized to administer Initial or Commanding 
Officer Progress Checks.

32. Ready Room UNSAT (RRU). An UNSAT grade given for inadequate knowledge of flight 
procedures, systems, discuss items, emergency procedures, or deficient preflight planning.

33. Special Syllabus Requirements (SSR). One-time, ungraded demonstration items. While 
SSRs are recommended for certain events in block, they may be accomplished at any time during 
the block.

34. Stage of Training. All training of a particular type (Ground, Familiarization, Instruments, 
Navigation, Formation, Tactical) within a phase. The first letter in the lesson designator 
identifies the stage of each lesson (Example: F4101 is in the Formation Stage).

35. Training Media. MPTS media include aircraft, simulator, CPTs, ground training, and CAI. 
The second character in the lesson identifier designates the training media.

36. Training Review Board (TRB). A fact-finding board generated by a failed CO-PC that 
considers the circumstances relevant to the SNA’s training, such as quality and continuity of 
training, outside influences, and extenuating circumstances. The TRB does not make attrition or 
retention recommendations.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
37. Warmup Event (SXX86). Additional events given to allow an SNA to regain a level of 
proficiency previously demonstrated which has diminished due to an extended break in training.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-1 
Chapter I

General Instructions

1. Syllabus Management

a. Distribution. Participating squadron personnel.

b. Interpretation. The syllabus is directive. Should circumstances create situations not 
covered within the scope of this syllabus, or specific course of action appears to conflict with other 
directives, consult CNATRA (N71).

c. Deviations. Document all deviations on the event’s ATF.

d. Changes. Recommended changes shall be submitted in accordance with CNATRAINST 
1550.6G.

e. Execution. SNAs will execute all the curriculum events.

f. Syllabus Description. Intermediate E-2 MPTS consists of intermediate multi-engine training 
for E-2 SNAs who will go to advanced jet training. This training is flown in the 
T-44C aircraft. Each phase is divided into stages. Stages are grouped by like flight training 
regimes: Familiarization, Instrument, and Formation. Each stage is subdivided into training 
blocks. The training blocks consist of a specified number of flights. Maneuver item files identify 
the acceptable level of performance that must be achieved at the completion of each training block.

2. Training Management

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
a. Syllabus Progression. Fly syllabus events within each stage sequentially. Do not start a 
block without all prerequisites. SNAs may be in different stages or blocks simultaneously. Where 
applicable, SNAs will be eligible for, and shall be prepared for, more than one syllabus event. 
SNAs must complete all events. The flowchart on page I-3 delineates the sequence of flying 
events and their ground training prerequisites. System training management is designed to 
facilitate two graded events (flight, simulator, or exam) per SNA per day.

b. Accelerated Progression

(1) TAP (Training Acceleration Program). SNAs with previous flight experience may 
warrant accelerated progression through the syllabus. TAP is a specifically tailored syllabus 
deviation, designed/approved by the Squadron CO, printed on a Supplementary ATF, placed in the 
student’s ATJ, specifying which syllabus events are waived based on the SNA’s previous flight 
experience. This policy shall not be used to meet squadron production goals; it is strictly for 
instances where previous flight experience makes completion of all events within a block of 
instruction unnecessary. All ATFs for the accelerated NFS will be clearly marked “Proficiency 
Adv” in the appropriate ATF dropdown box.” The squadron CO shall closely monitor the progress

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-2 
of TAP SNAs. If performance suffers due to participation in TAP, the SNA shall return to normal 
syllabus progression.

(2) Proficiency Advance. SNA with demons trated above average performance may warrant 
accelerated progression, known as “Proficiency Advance.” Squadron CO is encouraged to support 
a squadron program where a SNA/SNFO may be advanced to the next block of instruction when 
all required items for the current block meet or exceed performance prerequisites for the follow-on 
block of training:

(a) The squadron CO shall closely monito r the progress of Proficiency Advanced 
students. If performance suffers, the CO has options to assist the NFS with meeting performance 
expectations, (e.g., awarding ETs).

(b) All ATFs for the Proficiency Advanced ev ents will be marked “Proficiency Adv” in 
the appropriate ATF dropdown box.

(c) This policy shall not be used as a shortcut to meet squadron production goals; it is 
strictly for instances where demonstrated proficiency makes completion of all events within a 
block of instruction unnecessary.

c. Maneuver Continuity. SNAs must accomp lish previously introduced maneuvers frequently 
enough to ensure required proficiency is maintained.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
d. Hours/X (H/X). Instructor Pilots shall plan and execute missions to meet H/X as closely as 
practical. If actual event length varies from H/X by more than 0.3 hours, annotate reason(s) in the 
ATF’s general comments section. An SNA’s deficiency is not an acceptable reason to exceed H/X 
by more than 0.3 hours.

e. Special Syllabus Requirements. Unle ss otherwise noted, SIs may accomplish Special 
Syllabus Requirements (SSR) on any flight in the block. SSRs shall be completed in the specified 
block. Annotate completion of the SSR in the ITFs maneuver item content line and document the 
date on the SSR tab in T-SHARP. Assign NG/1 as the SSR maneuver grade.

f. Aviation Trai ning Jacket Reviews

(1) The Class Advisor shall conduct weekly jacket reviews per CNATRAINST 1500.4L. 
ATS forms are required to be reviewed before each flight or simulator event. ATJ reviews may be 
made as often as warranted by the individual SNA’s progress, subject to the following guidelines:

(2) All reviews shall be annotated on the CNATRA-GEN 1542/66, Jacket Review Divider, 
in the ATJ.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-4 
3. UNSAT Performance. (See also Progress Check, Chapter I, paragraph 9.c.(3).

a. Flights and Simulators

(1) Event Progression. Following an UNSAT event, if a PC is not required, that event shall 
be repeated until the NFS satisfactorily passes the event.

(2) NFS exhibits dangerous tendencies, or progress towards meeting EOB performance 
guidelines is insufficient. An overall event UNSAT is at the instructor’s discretion. It should be 
noted that an event may be graded UNSAT without any individual maneuvers graded U/2. If an 
overall UNSAT grade is awarded on any event other than normal syllabus flow events, the 
instructor shall clearly state the justification in the General Comments section of the ATF.

(3) Any UNSAT event, starting with failed academic exams in NIFE, through the final 
event prior to designation as a Naval Aviator or Naval Flight Officer, shall cumulatively be used in 
Progress Check triggers.

b. Ready Room UNSAT (RRU). A missed brie f does not constitute an RRU. Missed briefs 
shall be documented on a Supplementary ATF and counseling or discipline shall be administered as 
directed by the squadron CO. An RRU is defined as either of the following:

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
(1) An NFS is inadequately prepared for th e scheduled event. RRUs always trigger a 
Progress Check, so they shall always be documented on a pink version of the event’s ATF. The 
event will be marked as incomplete with a U/2 grade in the appropriate graded item column, 
(e.g., General Knowledge, Procedures, etc.). Upon successful completion of the Progress Check, 
the original RRU event shall be flown as a take two (or greater) to complete all remaining, or 
appropriate items, graded as a normal event.

(2) The NFS fails a non-academic ex amination (NATOPS quiz, etc.).

c. Academic. Failing two exams tri ggers an IPC or CO-PC as appropriate.

d. Remediation

(1) A ground evaluation emphasizing the deficient areas may clear an UNSAT check flight 
or end of block syllabus event caused solely by ground operations.

(2) End of block UNSAT syllabus events in the Instrument stage may be cleared in the 
simulator if these conditions are met:

(a) The cause of the UNSAT is specific to the maneuver.

(b) The simulator is suited to the failed maneuver.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-5 
 e. Restrictions. Until remediating the UNSAT:

(1) The SNA shall not fly solo.

(2) The SNA shall not accomplish any traini ng except academic classes, examinations, and 
ground training events, provided the UNSAT event was not a prerequisite.

4. Training Review Board

a. The TRAWING shall conduct a TRB on all NFSs recommended for attrition.

b. Refer to 1500.4L for additional information.

5. Instructor Continuity

a. SNAs shall fly Familiarization sy llabus events FAM3201, FAM4101-4 and FAM4201-2 
with their on-wing.

b. Any Familiarization Stan IP may substitute as on-wing in the event the SNA’s on-wing is 
not available and an on-wing change is not prudent.

6. Training Delays and Warmup Events Within Stage (SXX86)

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
a. A Warmup (WU) event is given to regain flight proficiency due to a training delay. 
Eligibility is based on the number of days since the last stage syllabus event, in either the aircraft 
or simulator. WU criteria are normally based on last event in stage because MPTS and NFOTS are 
built on increasing levels of skill attainment between blocks or stand-alone events. Every WU 
event shall ensure required skills for that stage are refreshed. WU events shall be coded as SXX86 
and include a justification in the general comments section of the ATF. For more detailed 
information on warmup training, refer to CNATRAINST 1500.4L

(1) WUs shall be scheduled and flown as the next event. If performance warrants a WU, it 
shall be re-coded as the last completed dual event. If performance is sufficient, or will be 
sufficient by EOB, any WU may be an advancing event at IP discretion. Standard event 
completion criteria apply.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
(2) Individual maneuver grades on WU events will not count toward calculation of NFS 
stage and phase grades. WU events are however eligible for overall event Unsatisfactory 
(UNSAT), if a significant incident not related to the stage discontinuity occurred. Note that even 
though stage and phase grades are not affected, UNSAT WU events do negatively affect the 
student’s PAS and NSS since the UNSAT does count toward the total number of UNSATs 
(see 1500.4L, Appendix G).

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-6 
 (3) Ready Room UNSATs (RRU) are overall event UNSAT and count toward PC triggers. See 
CNATRAINST 1500.4L, paragraph 608 for amplifying information on this situation.

(4) If the break in training occurs during the transition from simulator to aircraft, a WU 
shall be flown in the simulator and coded as the last completed simulator in stage. The WU may 
be flown in the aircraft with the TRAWING Commander’s approval.

(5) All WUs shall be dual for flight events or with an instructor in the simulator, except in 
the case of carrier qualifications, where WUs may be solo at the discretion of the respective LSO.

(6) For delays less than seven days, the specific WU criteria, promulgated in the MCG, shall 
apply.

(7) The instructor shall document on the ATF the reason and circumstances for awarding the 
WU event.

(8) Check events (SXX90) are considered part of the stage for which they are checking for WU 
purposes.

(9) Stage-specific EP simulators satisfy simu lator WU requirements for blocks within that 
stage.

(10) PC WU criteria differ from normal syllabus flow WU criteria; see paragraph 703.

(11) The following table is a quick reference regarding the use of WUs with respect to stage 
continuity, or breaks in training:

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-7 
*Break = (Current Julian Date) – (Julian Date of last simulator or flight event in state).

b. Training Delays and Warmups Between St ages. WU are intended for non-curriculum 
breaks in training. Each curriculum is designed to allow sufficient time for academics, simulators, 
and flights. First flights in stage following ground training are designed and graded with the delay 
factored in and normally do not require a WU.

(1) Between stages a WU is required if 14 to 30 days have elapsed since any curriculum 
unless otherwise specified in respective MCG.

Note. Mandatory WU does not apply for students going from I21 block back to FAM21 
block, (i.e., I2103 to FAM2104). The CI may grant an optional warm-up if deemed necessary.

(2) All WU events between stages shall be recorded on ATF and will include all required 
and relevant information to record and conduct and performance of that WU event.

c. Extended Training Delays. If the period between events is greater than 30 days, the 
squadron CO shall determine an appropriate WU training plan to regain NFS proficiency with the 
following guidance:

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-8 
 (1) A WU training plan should consist of a repr esentative cross-section of events completed 
prior to the break in training. The WU training plan shall be placed in the NSF's ATJ.

(2) The NFS should be thoroughly briefed a nd provided with a copy of the WU training 
plan prior to execution. If the WU training plan includes academic or simulator events, the 
TRAWING Ground Training Officer shall be furnished with a copy of the plan to ensure the 
training is properly scheduled. The CO may amend the WU training plan based on NFS 
performance during execution of that plan.

(3) Upon successful completion of the WU tr aining plan, the NFS shall resume normal 
syllabus flow.

(4) If the NFS has not regained proficiency sufficient to resume training following the WU 
training plan, additional WU training may be accomplished at the CO's discretion. If elected, a 
Supplementary ATF with an accompanying additional WU plan shall be added to the NFS's ATJ.

7. Additional Flights and Simulators

a. Extra Training Events (SXX87). A ll ETs shall be dual and coded as SXX87, 
(e.g., FAM4187). ET events include, but are not limited to:

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
(1) IPC or CO-PC ET Events. ETs offer the CO a powerful tool to improve NFS 
performance. ETs do however come with an opportunity cost of using scarce resources for 
individual remediation, where they would otherwise be used in normal training processes.

(2) The CO may authorize up to two ETs following an UNSAT event or up to two ETs 
preceding a Progress Check.

(3) International SNAs. Foreign-la nguage Extra Training (ET) events are not authorized 
unless coordinated beforehand. Coordination is required between the training squadron IMSO, 
TRAWING IMSO and NETSAFA, with notification to CNATRA (N713) of any approved 
foreign-language ETs. Approval of foreign-language ETs shall be documented on a 
Supplementary ATF.

(4) Additional Events to Meet Minimum Sylla bus Time. An event flown to meet minimum 
nighttime shall be flown as a FAM4587 and will meet the MIF for the block in which the ET is 
flown. All critical items need not be completed on this additional event.

b. Adaptation Events (SXX84). The Opera tions Officer may grant events required for 
adaptation to the flying environment when requested by the flight surgeon, (e.g., airsickness, 
eyeglasses, etc.).

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-9 
8. Ground Training and Briefing Requirements

a. Mission Preparation, Briefings, and Debriefings

(1) EOB Events. The IP shall carefully re view the Aviation Training Summary in planning 
the EOB event to ensure the profile includes opportunities to reach MIF on all critical items and 
optional items attempted in the block.

(2) Preparation. SNAs shall arrive for each flight with:

(a) Thorough knowledge of:

1. The flight’s Discuss Items, as listed in Chapters IV-VII.

2. Procedural knowledge of all it ems for the event’s training block.

(b) A flight profile tailore d to training requirements, weak areas, and continuity.

(c) The latest ATS for the stage.

(3) Briefing. Thoroughl y cover the mission’s:

(a) Event Discuss Items, as listed in Chapters IV-VII.

(b) Specific objectives.

(c) Techniques and required procedures for accomplishing those objectives.

(d) Planned profile and contingencies.

(4) Debriefing

(a) After each event, the instructor sha ll critique the SNA’s performance using cause 
and effect analysis, particularly with respect to the CTS.

(b) The mission’s complexity and SNA’s pr ogress will govern the time required for the 
debrief.

b. Emergency Procedures (EP) Briefing and Training

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
(1) EP training builds the SNA’s confidence in the aircraft. The IP shall conduct EP 
training on all dual aircraft events, either on the ground or in the aircraft. Correct procedural 
deficiencies through additional instruction and study assignments.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-10 
 (2) Incorporate EP training into simulator events when practical; however, instructional 
block objectives take precedence.

(3) Grade the SNA’s overall EP knowledge and performance under Emergency Procedures.

9. Mission Grading Procedures and Evaluation Policies

a. General Grading and Evaluation Policy. Maneuver item files listed in the MPTS are 
minimum stage or phase completion standards per maneuver.

b. Grading Procedures (A ircraft and Training Devices)

(1) Absolute Maneuver Grading. Use the fo llowing grading scale to document the SNA’s 
characteristic performance on maneuvers attempted during each dual event. This grading scale is 
an absolute grading scale. Judge the SNA’s proficiency only against the item’s course training 
standard. (See Student Performance Measurement and Application of Standards, page x, Course 
Data, paragraph 21.).

(a) Demonstrated or Optional (NG/1).

1. When the IP demonstrates the ma neuver and the SNA does not subsequently 
perform it during the event.

2. When the student performs a mane uver that is optional in the block.

3. To indicate accomplishing all SSRs for that event. Specify the completed SSR 
in the ATF’s maneuver item content line and document date of exposure on the SSR tab.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
(b) Unable (U/2 Level). Performance is uns afe or lacks sufficient knowledge, skill, or 
ability. Deviations greatly exceed CTS, significantly disrupting performance. Corrections 
significantly lag deviations or aggravate the deviation.

(c) Fair (F/3 Level). Performance is sa fe, but with limited proficiency. Deviations 
exceed CTS, detracting from performance. Corrections noticeably lag deviations, and may not be 
appropriate.

(d) Good (G/4 Level). Performance meet s or positively exceeds CTS. Deviations 
outside CTS are allowed, provided they are brief, minor, and do not affect safety of flight. 
Corrections must be appropriate and timely.

(e) Excellent (E/5 Level). Greatly surpa sses CTS. Performance is correct, efficient, 
and skillful. Deviations are very minor. Corrections, if required, are initiated by the SNA and are 
appropriate, smooth, and rapid.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-11 
 (2) Overall Event Grades. Overall event grades represent the SNA’s progression through 
MPTS. Grade events “Pass or” or “UNSAT.” Use the following definitions to characterize event 
grades.

(a) Pass

1. Prior to EOB. Progress is ad equate to meet standards by EOB.

2. EOB. The SNA’s performan ce meets or exceeds standards.

(b) UNSAT. The SNA exhibits dangerous tendencies, or progress towards meeting 
EOB standards is insufficient. UNSAT overall is at the instructor’s discretion. It should be noted 
that an event may be graded UNSAT without any individual maneuvers graded 2/Unable. 
Document all UNSAT events on a pick ATF.

(3) Awarding Overall Event Grades. The SNA’s overall grade is based on the SNA’s 
performance against the MIF for that event. The following rules govern overall event grading:

(a) EOB. Performance must meet MIF by EO B. As long as MIF was met in block at 
least two times, a NFS can progress past EOB if a maneuver does not meet MIF on EOB event.

(4) Maneuver Requirements. For each block:

(a) Mandatory Items. Items with a number and a plus (+) are mandatory and the SNA 
must meet the required proficiency by EOB. As long as MIF was met in block at least two times, a 
NFS can progress past EOB if a maneuver does not meet MIF on EOB event.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
(b) Optional Items. Items with a MIF of 1 are optional; however, if flown, can only be 
graded as a NG/1 or U/2.

(c) Not Demonstrated or Not Performed. Th e IP will not demonstrate, nor will the SNA 
perform: 
 1. Unnumbered items.

2. Items not in the stage.

3. Exceptions:

a. Weather-driven instrument approaches.

b. Prebriefed maneuvers for IP proficiency.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-12 
 (5) Incomplete Events. In general, IPs s hould consider an event complete if able to 
accomplish the requirements in paragraph (a) below. This is particularly true when weather 
precludes accomplishing certain maneuver items, but the IP is able to emphasize training on other 
maneuver items. Subsequent events in the block, when available, can reverse this emphasis, hence 
achieving overall training balance. If an SNA has had ample opportunity to learn a task and 
subsequently flies a short mission, do not incomplete the mission solely to provide unwarranted 
extra training.

(a) Assessment. Assess the event complete if:

1. A significant portion of the sortie was used for student training, and

2. Sufficient events remain in the block to redress the imbalance, and

3. The SNA passes the event.

4. Otherwise, assess the event incomplete.

(b) Completion Events

1. An event may both complete a previous event and count as an Advancing X.

2. For events flown exclusively to cl ear an incomplete, grades on maneuvers 
repeated from the incomplete event do not count towards the SNA’s score, except where the grade 
assigned for the repeated item is lower that the lowest grade previously assigned on that item from 
all previous attempts on that item.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
(c) Simulator Event Completion. Assess a simulator event complete if the SNA has 
received a full training period per the master curriculum guide.

c. Policies for Evaluation Flights and Ground Evaluations

(1) Authorized Evaluators. The squadron CO will designate check pilots for each stage.

(2) Check Flights (SXX90)

(a) Single Event Training Blocks. Check fl ights amount to single event training blocks.

1. Should fly a representative cro ss section of optional maneuvers.

2. Check pilots may allow SNAs to reattempt maneuvers.

3. The entire event should be devoted to assessing the SNA’s ability and readiness 
to progress to the next stage of training. All maneuvers indicated with a plus (+) are check flight 
critical and must be accomplished to MIF.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-13 
 4. The SNA should be able to demonstrat e required levels of proficiency without 
instructor assistance; however, instruction is allowed on check flights and SNAs may reattempt 
maneuvers at the check pilot’s discretion.

(b) Incomplete Check Flight. The ch eck flight shall be incomplete when:

1. Any (+) item was not flown, or

2. The check pilot was unable to sample su fficient examples of a given maneuver to 
assess the SNA’s overall performance, or

3. The flight is UNSAT.

Note: The subsequent flight need only include maneuvers required to complete the check if the 
previous flight was established as a “Pass”.

(c) Instrument or Navigati on Stage Check Failure. If the SNA fails an Instrument or 
Navigation stage check because of an UNSAT pattern or landing not directly related to the stage 
being evaluated, any subsequent ET event may be flown as a familiarization event.

(3) Progress Check

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
(a) The Progress Checks flown in the aircraft or simulator are holistic reviews of SNA’s 
proficiency, judgment, air sense, and overall ability to maneuver the aircraft safely and confidently. 
The intent of every Progress Check is to determine whether the SNA has the potential to reach the 
defined training standards of his or her current phase of training within the designated TTT, while 
demonstrating the potential to successfully complete advanced and FRS-level training process.

(b) Refer to 1500.4L for additional information.

10. Special Instructions and Restrictions

a. Flight Hour and Event Requirements and Restrictions

(1) Programmed Hours and Events. Progra mmed syllabus flight hours are listed on page 
ix. Event lengths, SXX86, 87, 88, and 89 events will cause variation. Accomplish all syllabus 
events.

(2) Minimum Night Hours. 4.0 hours.

(3) Maximum Daily SNA Activities (Aircraft, Simulator, or Academic). SNAs shall 
not exceed two graded activities during one duty day.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

I-14 
 (4) Minimum SNA Turn-Times. One hour is required between debriefing of an event 
and the brief for a follow-on or simulator event. This does not apply to out-and-in, 
cross-country, or safe-for-solo to hot-seat profiles; however, the instructor shall ensure 
adequate debrief and brief time is allocated.

(5) Crew Day. The period from the beginni ng of the SNA’s first event or official 
duty of the day until the completion of the last event of the day, including associated 
paperwork and debrief. Crew day shall not exceed 12 hours.

(6) Crew Rest. A minimum of 12 hours shall elapse between the conclusion of the NFS’s 
last scheduled event of the day (including associated debrief) and the NFS’s first scheduled event 
(including associated brief) of the following day. For scheduled non-graded events (classes, CAI, 
MIL, watch-standing duty days, this period may be shortened to eight hours. After six consecutive 
scheduled days, the NFS shall receive one day off. Official duty, squadron training, or standby 
scheduling do not qualify as a day off. No training may be accomplished on a duty day.

b. Aircraft and Simulator Interchangeability. Simulator events may be substituted in the 
aircraft when the simulator is unavailable for extended periods of time.

chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D

IV-4 
4. Discuss Items

FAM2101 
Seat and rudder pedal adjustment, fuel management panel, pilot instrument panel, engine 
instruments and switches, center instrument panel, annunciator panel analysis, right seat 
instrument panel, circuit breaker panels, control pedestal, overhead control panel, MFD, 
checklists: before start, engine start, after start, brake check (out of chocks), engine run-up, 
takeoff, after landing, and secure.

FAM2102 
Takeoff procedures, checklist management, landing procedures, checklists: climb, cruise, 
descent, approach, landing, start malfunctions, and MFD operations, CRM callouts, and 
pressurization system malfunctions.

FAM2103 
Loss of brakes, hot brakes, brake fire, mechanical stops in throttle quadrant, inadvertent 
condition lever to fuel cutoff, engine failure at altitude, anti-ice and deice systems autopilot 
disengagement, and ground emergencies, anti-ice and deice systems, and windshield heating 
failure.

FAM2104 
Vmca demo, dynamic engine cut, engine failure or fire during or after takeoff, fuel system 
malfunctions, smoke or fume emergencies, in-flight damage, bird strikes, wing or 
uncontrollable fire, ditching.

FAM2105 
Flap malfunctions, no-flap landings, gear malfunctions, unsafe gear and gear up landings, 
engine malfunctions, propeller malfunctions, electrical malfunctions, and air-starts.

2. Syllabus Notes

a. This block should concentrate on basic air work, high work maneuvers, landing patterns, 
and checklist management.

b. On-wing flights will be FAM4101-4.

c. On-wing SNAs shall taxi airc raft for all events in this block.

d. On a flight where a system is briefed, the SNA shall be given a simulated malfunction 
associated with that system.

e. Complete a minimum of two passes in landi ng pattern per event with IP as PF and SNA as 
PM.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements

FAM4101 
Oxygen mask familiarization and utilization.

4. Discuss Items

FAM4101 
Outside scan techniques, see and avoid, takeoff, crosswind takeoff and landing, aborted 
takeoff, landing pattern, full-stop landings, touch-and-go procedures, training-time-out, 
NATOPS brief, dual engine wave-offs, aircraft engine operating limits, engine start 
procedures, abnormal starts and malfunctions, and brake system and malfunctions.

FAM4102 
Porpoised landings, aircraft airframe operating limits, full-flap landings, engine fire on deck, 
MFD operation, stalls and spin recovery, no-flap landings, ditching power-on, and fuel 
system and malfunctions.

1. Prerequisites

a. FAM3201 (Emergen cy Familiarization).

b. FAM4104 (Familiarization).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. The purpose of this block is to con tinue basic air work while introducing additional 
emergency procedures, specifically SSE work.

b. CRM should be emphasized during all flights, especially during SSE training.

c. FAM4201-2 – 2.0 hours per event.

d. FAM4203-6 – 1.5 hours per event.

e. On-wing flights will be FAM4201-2.

f. Windmilling air-start sh all be accomplished once during the FAM42 block and annotated 
in the comments section of the ATF.

g. Starter-assisted air-sta rt shall be accomplished once during the FAM42 block and 
annotated in the comments section of the ATF.

h. SSE full-stop shall be accomplished and graded during this block following IP 
demonstration.

i. Complete a minimum of two passes in landi ng pattern per event with IP as PF and SNA as 
PM.

j. Where a system is briefed, the SNA shoul d be given a simulated malfunction associated 
with that system.

conditions.

CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025

V-4 
Blk # Media Title Events Hrs H/X 
I31 OFT Instrument Approaches 4 6.0 1.5

1. Prerequisites

a. FAM3401 (Night Familiarization).

b. I0103 (Flight Director Operation).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. Students shall bring a co mpleted DD-1801 for all RI events.

b. Autopilot shall not be used for approach es during the first two events in the block. 
Autopilot in the final two events shall be used and are limited to one fully coupled approach per 
event.

c. Simulator equipment-de pendent emphasis items are:

(1) I3101. PAR, ASR, and ILS approaches.

(2) I3102. VOR, NDB, and TACAN approaches.

(3) I3103. LOC and LOC-BC approaches.

(4) I3104. RNAV (GPS) approaches (shall accomplish one of each) – HILO approach, 
“T” approach, and radar vectors. Shall see one LNAV approach, one LNAV/VNAV approach, 
and non-approach GPS holding.

d. Events shall have a minimum of three appr oaches per event and include at least two full 
procedure turn approaches.

e. Normal two-engine approaches should be emphasized in this block, but may introduce 
minor malfunctions (no ESIS or SSE).

f. Each event shall include a minimum of one approach with the flight director and one 
approach without the flight director.

g. Holding should be accomplished and gr aded on at least three different events.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE

chunks.text:
h. All events shall include a missed appr oach; include at least two circling missed 
approaches in the block.

V-5 
 i. SNA in right seat shall be PM and gr aded accordingly, emphasizing CRM callouts and 
radio communications.

j. At least two non-precision approaches shall be conducted using the CDFA method.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements

I3102 
CDFA Practice Approach.

4. Discuss Items

I3101 
IAF and FAF procedures (6T’s, descent, and lead turns), CRM callouts and techniques, GCA 
approach, PAR, ASR, and ILS configuration point, ASR recommended altitudes, NAVAID 
setup (takeoff, en route, approach, and missed approach), cockpit procedures (en route versus 
multiple terminal approaches, task management), and point-to-point navigation.

I3102 
Holding, missed approach holding versus HILO procedures, VOR and TAC approach 
procedures, missed approach, circling procedures, circling missed approach (determining 
MAP and continuation beyond MAP), needle-only approach procedures, and continuous 
descent final approach (CDFA).

I3103 
NATOPS Instrument Flight Manual, SOPs, local instructions, NATOPS, LOC and LOC-BC 
approaches (configuration, presentation, MA, and FAF Identification by other than DME), 
ESIS procedures, reverse sensing, and TERPS.

I3104 
RNAV(GPS) approach types (LNAV, LNAV/VNAV, LPV, TAA, T’s, and HILO), feeder fix 
selection of IAF, RNAV configuration point, RAIM, GPS sensitivity modes, autopilot and 
flight director, lost communications (FIH and LOA) , and SSE approach procedures.

CNATRAINST 1542.175D

V-8 
Blk # Media Title Events Hrs H/X 
I41 T-44C Instrument Approaches 4 8.0 2.0

1. Prerequisites

a. FAM4502 (Night Familiarization).

b. I3104 (Instrument Approaches).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. IP shall make a note on the ATF if unable to accomplish emphasis items on appropriate 
flight. Weather, aircraft, and ATC equipment-dependent, emphasis items are:

(1) I4101. PAR, ASR, and ILS approaches.

(2) I4102. LOC and LOC-BC approaches.

(3) I4103. RNAV (GPS), VOR approaches.

(4) I4104. Discretion of the IP, but should in clude an SNA visual approach and an IP 
demonstration of SSE approach and wave-off.

b. Events should have a minimum of four a pproaches per event and include at least two 
procedure turn approaches. Normal two-engine approaches should be emphasized in this block, 
but may introduce minor malfunctions (no ESIS or SSE).

c. SNA shall see one LNAV and one LNAV/VNAV approach in the block.

d. Each event shall include a minimum of one approach with the flight director.

e. Holding should be accomplished and graded on at least two events, one of which should 
be GPS holding.

f. All events shall include a missed approach and should include at least two circling missed 
approaches in the block.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
g. Minimum of one approach per event with IP as PF and SNA as PM, emphasizing CRM 
callouts and radio communications. To allow SNAs additional radio communication practice, 
one approach during each event should be flown with the SNA handling radio communications 
as PF.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE

V-9 
 h. SNAs shall bring a DD-1801 flight plan or FAA flight plan to correspond with the 
appropriate flight plan discuss item for each event in this block. This flight plan will be filled 
out as a practice flight plan to demonstrate the applicable knowledge of the different types of 
DD-1801 and FAA flight plans. Additionally, during each event brief, SNAs shall fill out a 
DD-1801 flight plan for the actual event profile as required.

i. At least two non-precision approaches shall be conducted using the CDFA method.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements

I4101 
Coupled approach demo.

I4104 
IP demonstrate SSE approach to SSE missed approach.

4. Discuss Items

I4101 
Holding, GCA approach, PAR, ILS, and ASR configuration point, PAR versus ASR descent 
point, ASR recommended altitudes, NAVAID characteristics (ILS, LOC, LOC-BC (service 
volumes, operation principles, NATOPS procedures, and cockpit presentation)), ATC 
communication (clearance delivery, ground, tower, center, approach, tower, and ground), 
uncontrolled airfields (communications, canceling IFR, and procedures), holding in lieu of 
(HILO) PT approach, approach plate titles and notes, ATIS, AWOS, and ASOS, DD-175-1, 
and terminal area delay DD-1801.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
I4102 
Procedure turns (methods and types), instrument publications (DoD (NGA), FAA (NACO), 
Jeppesen, other), DPs (ODP, SID,vector, diverse, and uncontrolled field), VDP, CDFA, 
landing transition, low close-in obstacles, ILS, LOC, LOC-BC approach (configuration, 
presentation, MA, and FAF identification by other than DME), reverse sensing, and stopover 
DD-1801.

I4103 
Point-to-point navigation; TERPS required obstacle clearance (ROC) for initial, intermediate, 
final, and circling phases; NAVAID characteristics (VOR, TACAN, and NDB 
(service volumes, operation principles, NATOPS procedures, and cockpit presentation)); 
VOR, TAC, and NDB approach procedures; missed approach (determining MAP and 
continuation beyond MAP), circling procedures and circling missed approach; procedure track 
(arc and radial combination and teardrop); VDP; and RNAV (FMS, GPS, DME/DME, 
VOR/DME, and INS), and FAA flight plan.

approach procedures; SSE approach procedures; TERPS (FAA Order 8260.3B); Trouble T 
(OIS, low close-in obstacles, climb gradients, alternate routing, nonstandard takeoff weather 
minimums, and VCOA).

I32 OFT Emergency Instrument Approaches 4 6.0 1.5

1. Prerequisites. I4104 (Instrument Approaches).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. Students shall bring a co mpleted DD-1801 for all RI events.

b. Autopilot shall not be used for approach es during the first two events in the block. 
Autopilot in the final two events shall be used and are limited to one fully-coupled approach per 
event.

c. Simulator equipment-de pendent emphasis items are emergency procedures during:

(1) I3201. PAR, ASR, ILS approaches.

(2) I3202. LOC and LOC-BC approaches.

(3) I3203. VOR, NDB, and TACAN approaches.

(4) I3204. RNAV (GPS) approaches. Shall see one LNAV and one LNAV/VNAV 
approach.

d. Minimum of three approaches per event.

e. Each event shall include a minimum of one approach with the flight director.

f. Holding should be accomplished and graded on at least three different events, one of 
which shall be non-GPS.

g. All events shall include a missed approach; at least two circling missed approaches in the 
block.

h. SNA in right seat shall be PM and gr aded accordingly, emphasizing CRM callouts and 
radio communications.

i. At least two non-precision approaches shall be conducted using the CDFA method.

1. Prerequisites. I3204 (Emergency Instrument Approaches).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. Each flight should consist of a mix of approaches flown in the I41 block.

b. Events should have a minimum of four a pproaches per event and include at least two 
procedure turn approaches. Emergency procedures should be emphasized in this block.

c. Each event shall include a minimum of one approach with the flight director. SNA shall 
see one LNAV and LNAV/VNAV approach in the block.

d. Holding should be accomplished and graded on at least two events, one of which should 
be GPS holding.

e. All events shall include a missed approach and should include at least two circling missed 
approaches in the block.

f. Minimum of one approach per event w ith IP as PF and SNA as PM, emphasizing CRM 
callouts and radio communications. To allow SNAs additional radio communication practice, 
one approach during each event should be flown with the SNA handling radio communications 
as PF.

g. During each event brief, SNAs shall fill out a DD-1801 flight plan for the actual event 
profile as required.

h. At least two non-precision approaches shall be conducted using the CDFA method.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements. None.

SSE approach procedures (configuration, airspeeds and power in the descent, and descent 
rate), SSE circling approach and missed approach, emergency voice reports, propeller system 
and malfunctions, in-flight damage and bird strikes, ESIS approach, and simulated versus 
actual EP.

malfunctions.

I4203 
Procedure track (arc and radial combination and teardrop), VDP, CDFA, smoke and fume

performance information), fuel system and malfunctions, brake system and malfunctions,

a. This event will be an evaluation of IFR procedures, involving a representative cross 
section of maneuvers previously presented.

b. SNAs shall bring one DD-1801 flight plan per SNA. SNAs shall draft a flight plan that 
will execute the required maneuvers for the event.

c. Event shall have a minimum of three appr oaches and include at least one full procedure 
approach with exposure to all types of approaches.

d. Event shall include a minimum of one approach with the flight director.

e. Event shall be flown with military IP in right seat designated to give instrument check 
flights.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements. None.

4. Discuss Items. Operations limits quiz, IAF/FAF entry procedures (6 T’s, descent, and lead 
turns), pressurization system and malfunctions, electrical system, and CNAF-M 3710.7 weather 
filing criteria.

chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D CH-1
chunks.text:
CNATRAINST 1542.175D CH-1 
11 Mar 2026 
V-20 
Blk # Media Title Events Hrs H/X 
I43 T-44C Cross-Country 4 16.0 4.0

1. Prerequisites

a. I3390 (Mid-stage Instrument Check).

b. G0990 (IFR Final Exam).

c. G1090 (FRR Exam).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. Shall be conducted out of the local area concentrating on en route cross-country 
navigation with a minimum of two approaches conducted per event or a total of eight 
approaches. Flight director shall be used for one approach per event. Autopilot with flight 
director should be used during this block.

b. Numbered approaches shall expose the stude nt to all types of approaches. A visual 
approach shall be flown at least once during the block.

c. Should be flown above FL180 to emphasize high altitude en route navigation.

d. The CNATRAINST 1500.4L limit for the maximum SNA instructional flight hours in 
one day is removed for this block only. SNA shall not exceed the 12-hour workday.

e. I4301 and I4303 are right seat events for SNA.

f. Should fly a SID and a STAR during the block.

g. Ownship should be utilized on Fore Flight during cross-country operations.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements

I4301 
 Demonstrate weathe r radar procedures.

I4302 
 FMS Assisted Approach demo.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE

4. Discuss Items

I4301 
CRM Seven Skills, callouts, ATC communications (clearance delivery, ground, tower, center, 
approach, tower, and ground), pre-flight planning (nav bag, fuel planning and packet, weight 
and balance, NOTAMS (destination, alternates, and en route), CNAF-M 3710.7 weather filing 
criteria, DD-1801, DD-175-1, OPARS, AIRMETs, and SIGMETs), unfamiliar field 
operations, and MFD operation.

I4302 
DPs (ODP, SID, vector, diverse), checklist management, en route charts, airfield markings 
and lighting, STARs (filing, planning, and lost comms), en route weather facilities 
(FSS and Metro), airport diagrams and symbols, circling and circling missed approach (TAS 
considerations), securing and RON procedures.

I4303 
FAA flight plan, contacting FSS, performance data, anti-icing systems, RNP, lost 
communications (en route, VMC versus IMC), and course deviations for weather.

I4304 
Filing in-flight, SSE en route, FMS en route navigation, wake turbulence, arrival transition, 
and procedure turn (holding technique).

5. Block MIF

CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025 
V-23 
Blk # Media Title Events Hrs H/X 
I34 OFT Advanced Emergency Instrument 
Approaches 
2 3.0 1.5

1. Prerequisites. I4304 (Cross Country).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. This event is a scenario-based, comprehens ive review of instrument procedures, involving 
a representative cross section of IFR approaches, and will prepare the student for initial 
instrument rating. It should include degradation of aircraft systems, avionics automation, and IP, 
PM CRM. It must emphasize the following: CRM, PIC decision making, situational awareness, 
instrument proficiency, and multi-engine airmanship.

b. To allow SNAs additional radio communi cation practice, one approach during event 
should be flown with the SNA handling radio communications as PF.

c. The event should have a minimum of three approaches per event, including a minimum of 
two non-local. Numbered approaches shall expose the student to all types of approaches.

d. At a minimum, the following should be flown in the block: one approach with 
glideslope/glide-path guidance, one VOR or TAC or NDB approach, one approach with use of a 
flight director, and one approach without the use of a flight director.

e. CI will instruct event.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements. None.

4. Discuss Items

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
I3401 
Vmca, Vmcg, PIC decision making, WW versus convective SIGMET, weather considerations 
during emergencies, approach lighting, weight and balance form F, and other discussion items 
at the discretion of the IP.

I3402 
Memory items and deferred emergencies, NATOPS Procedures and situations not covered by 
NATOPS, changing flight plan in-flight, ATC priority handling, adaptability/flexibility during 
emergencies, and other discussion items at the discretion of the IP.

1. Prerequisites. I3204 (Emergency Instrument Approaches).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. I3501 will include various ai rcraft and instrument malfunctions. CI will instruct event.

b. I3502 will consist of a r eal world scenario commencing at crew brief or take-off. 
Emphasis will be placed on procedural knowledge, judgment, and crew resource management 
skills. PM SNA shall be graded on CRM, radio communications, and callouts. The scenario will 
be carried through to a logical conclusion. IP or CIS, CRM-I or CRM-F, shall evaluate SNA as 
per the OPNAVINST 1542.7E.

c. Upon successful completion I3502, CRM-I or CRM-F shall record flight evaluation on 
CRM Training and Evaluation Record for inclusion in SNA’s NATOPS jacket. IP or CIS shall 
include on ATF “Successful completion of initial CRM evaluation as per the 
COMNAVAIRFORINST 1542.7D.”

CNATRAINST 1542.175D 
10 Jul 2025 
V-28 
Blk # Media Title Events Hrs H/X 
I44 T-44C Advanced Emergency 
Instrument Approaches 
3 6.0 2.0

1. Prerequisites

a. I3402 (Advanced Emergenc y Instrument Approaches).

b. I3502 (Emergency In strument Approaches).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. This block is a scenario -based, comprehensive review of instrument procedures, involving 
a representative cross section of IFR approaches, and will prepare the student for initial 
instrument rating. Each event should include degradation of aircraft systems, avionics 
automation, IP and PM CRM. These events must emphasize the following: CRM, PIC decision 
making, situational awareness, instrument proficiency, and multi-engine airmanship.

b. SNAs shall contact the IP prior to the event fo r an assignment related to flight. If the IP is 
unavailable, contact the CDO for an assignment. SNAs shall bring one DD-1801 flight plan per 
SNA and one DD-175-1 per aircraft for their planned profile to every brief. SNAs shall draft a 
flight plan that will execute the required maneuvers for the event(s).

c. Minimum of one approach per event w ith IP as PF and SNA as PM, emphasizing CRM 
callouts and radio communications. To allow SNAs additional radio communication practice, 
one approach during each event should be flown with the SNA handling radio communications 
as PF.

chunks.title: T-44C E-2D INTERMEDIATE MULTI-SERVICE PILOT TRAINING SYSTEM (MPTS) MASTER CURRICULUM GUIDE
chunks.heading: CNATRAINST 1542.175D
chunks.text:
d. Should have a minimum of four appro aches per event, including a minimum of two non-
local. Numbered approaches may include ILS, LDA, LOC, LOC BC, RNAV, VOR, TAC, 
NDB, Visual, PAR, ASR, Needle-only, ESIS only, HI approaches, and circling.

e. At a minimum, the following should be flown in the block: one approach with 
glideslope/glide-path guidance, one VOR or TAC or NDB approach, one approach without the 
use of a flight director.

f. Any approach that does not meet MIF S HALL be remediated by flying that specific type 
of approach prior to end of block.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements. None.

I45 T-44C NATOPS Instrument Check Flight 1 2.0 2.0

1. Prerequisites. I4403 (Advanced Emergency Instrument Approaches).

2. Syllabus Notes

a. This event will be a comprehensive evaluation of IFR procedures, involving a 
representative cross section of instrument approaches and emergency procedures.

b. Complete a minimum of four approaches to include one precision and one non-precision 
approach. Numbered approaches may include ILS, LDA, LOC, LOC BC, RNAV, VOR, TAC, 
NDB, Visual, PAR/ASR, Needle-only, ESIS only, HI approaches, and circling. One approach 
shall be flown by the IP with the student acting as PM.

c. SNAs are required to bring a completed instrument rating request form to the brief.

d. IFR final must be within previous 60 days.

e. Event shall be flow n per the CNAF-M 3710.7 and the NATOPS Instrument Flight 
Manual. Successful completion shall lead to issuance of CNAF-M 3710.7 initial Standard 
Instrument Rating.

3. Special Syllabus Requirements. None.

4. Discuss Items. Instrument rating request form CNAF-M 3710.7, Trouble T 
(OIS, low close-in obstacles, climb gradients, alternate routing, nonstandard takeoff weather 
minimums, and VCOA), operations limits quiz, and additional discussion items at the discretion 
of the IP.

5. Block MIF
