chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
2.20
WINDSHIELD
WIPERS/ANTI-ICE
DEICE
SYSTEMS
2.20.1
W
indshield
W
ipers
T
wo
electrically
operated
windshield
wipers
are
provided
for
flight
and
ground
operations. A
rotary
switch
placarded
WINDSHIELD
WIPER
and
located
on
the
overhead
panel
(
Figure
2.1
1-1
)
controls
windshield
wiper
operation. Operation
of
the
wipers
on
dry
glass
will
result
in
damage
to
the
wipers
and
or
scratch
the
cockpit
windshields
beyond
limits. Function
positions
of
the
switch,
as
read
clockwise,
are
placarded
P
ARK,
SLOW
,
and
F
AST
. When
the
switch
is
held
in
the
spring-loaded
P
ARK
setting,
the
blades
will
return
to
their
normal
inoperative
position
on
the
glass. The
F
AST
and
SLOW
switch
positions
are
separate
operating
speed
settings
for
wiper
operation. The
windshield
wipers
are
powered
by
the
No. 2
feeder
bus,
through
and
protected
by
the
circuit
breaker
placarded
WSHLD
WIPER
located
in
the
WEA
THER
section
of
the
copilot
outboard
subpanel. 2.20.2
W
indshield
Anti-Ice
W
indshield
heat
for
both
the
pilot
and
copilot
windshields
is
controlled
by
a
toggle
switch
on
the
pilot
subpanel
placarded
WSHLD
ANTI-ICE
BOTH,
OFF
,
and
PILOT
. The
heat
control
circuit
for
the
pilot
windshield
is
protected
by
a
fuse
on
a
panel
mounted
on
the
forward
pressure
bulkhead. The
power
circuit
of
the
pilot
and
copilot
windshield
is
protected
by
two
circuit
breakers
located
on
the
extended
pedestal.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
A
controller
with
a
temperature
sensing
unit
maintains
95
°F
temperature
at
the
windshield
surface. An
electric
heater
lockout
is
incorporated
in
the
electrical
system
and
prevents
overload
of
the
electrical
system. This
assures
that
the
elements
of
the
electric
heater
will
be
blocked
from
functioning
when
windshield
heat,
propeller
deice,
or
engine
lip
boot
heat
is
in
use. •
Unreliable
operation
of
the
magnetic
compass
will
occur
during
use
of
windshield
anti-ice. •
During
use
of
windshield
anti-ice,
objects
viewed
through
the
windshield
may
appear
blurred. 2.20.3
Powerplant
Ice
Protection
Systems
Anti-ice
protection
for
each
engine
is
provided
by
two
systems,
the
mechanically
activated
engine
ice
vanes
and
the
electrical
lip
boot
heat. The
mechanical
system
consists
of
an
adjustable
engine
ice
vane
controlled
by
the
pilot,
using
push-pull
type
handles
located
below
the
left
subpanel. This
system
prevents
supercooled
water
droplets
and
snow
from
collecting
on
the
engine
screen. Protection
is
based
upon
the
principle
of
inertial
separation
and
is
accomplished
within
the
intake
air
duct
by
introducing
a
sudden
turn
to
the
airflow
before
it
reaches
the
engine
air
intake
screen
which
covers
the
inlet
in
the
compressor
section. Airstream
deflection
is
accomplished
by
an
engine
ice
vane
located
in
the
forward
nacelle
and
a
deflector
screen
located
in
the
mid-nacelle
area.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
Only
low-mass
air
will
make
contact
with
the
engine
screen
when
the
engine
ice
vane
is
extended. Because
water
and
snow
particles
are
heavier
than
air
,
they
will
continue
undeflected,
by
virtue
of
their
momentum,
to
be
dischar
ged
from
the
induction
system
through
an
open
bypass
duct
at
the
back
of
the
intake
scoop. The
aft
half
of
the
engine
ice
vane
is
hinged
to
allow
retraction
of
the
vane
until
icing
conditions
are
encountered. When
engine
ice
vanes
are
extended,
intake
airflow
is
changed
and
a
slight
decrease
in
engine
torque
will
occur
. The
ice
vanes
are
intended
to
be
used
in
the
fully
extended
position
or
full
retracted. The
electrical
anti-ice
system
is
comprised
of
an
electrothermal
boot
attached
to
the
air
intake
lip
of
each
engine
and
controlled
by
the
pilot
with
toggle-type
switches
on
the
left
subpanel. 2.20-1
1
SEPTEMBER
2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1
CUI

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
2.20.3.1
Engine
Ice
V
ane
Controls
T
wo
controls
placarded
PULL
FOR
ENGINE
ICE
PROTECTION,
LEFT
ENG,
RIGHT
ENG
and
located
below
the
left
subpanel
(
FO-1
)
allow
the
pilot
to
extend
the
engine
ice
vane
for
each
engine. When
using
the
ice
vanes,
monitor
the
position
of
the
ice
vane
controls
to
assure
they
remain
in
the
fully
extended
position. 2.20.3.2
Engine
Lip
Boot
Switches
T
wo
control
circuit
breaker
switches
placarded
ENG
LIP
BOOT
on
the
left
subpanel
control
the
electrothermal
element
in
the
lip
boot
attached
to
the
leading
edge
of
the
air
intake
for
each
engine. The
circuit
is
connected
through
the
landing
gear
squat
switch
and
is,
therefore,
operable
only
during
flight. The
lip
boot
heaters
are
powered
by
the
No. 1
and
No. 2
feeder
buses
and
are
protected
by
the
circuit
breakers
placarded
ENGINE
INLET
DEICE
located
on
the
pedestal
extension. 2.20.4
Fuel
System
Anti-Icing
An
oil-to-fuel
heat
exchanger
located
on
each
engine
accessory
case
operates
continuously
and
automatically
to
heat
the
fuel
to
prevent
freezing
of
any
water
in
the
fuel. No
controls
are
involved. One
external
fuel
vent
in
each
wing
serves
both
the
nacelle
and
wing
tanks
and
is
protected
against
icing
by
externally
attached
electric
heat
elements
that
are
controlled
and
protected
by
the
circuit
breaker
function
of
two
switch
breakers
on
the
left
subpanel
and
placarded
FUEL
VENTS,
LEFT
,
RIGHT
.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
2.20.4.1
Fuel
Control
Heat
Each
fuel
control
unit
pneumatic
line
is
protected
against
ice
by
electrically
heated
jackets,
controlled
by
two
circuit
breaker
toggle
switches
on
the
left
subpanel,
placarded
FUEL
CONTROL,
LEFT
,
RIGHT
. The
pneumatic
fuel
line
heaters
are
powered
by
the
No. 1
and
No. 2
subpanel
buses. Operation
with
the
FUEL
CONTROL
HEA
T
(ON)
will
damage
the
electrically
heated
anti-ice
jacket,
unless
the
engine
is
running. 2.20.5
Propeller
Electrothermal
Deicer
System
Electrothermal
deicing
boots
are
cemented
to
each
propeller
blade
to
remove
ice
from
the
propellers. Each
thermal
boot
consists
of
one
outboard
and
one
inboard
heating
element
and
receives
electrical
power
through
the
deicer
timer
,
slip
ring,
and
brush
block. Current
consumption
is
monitored
by
a
propeller
ammeter
on
the
left
(pilots)
subpanel
(
FO-1
). The
propeller
deice
system
is
powered
by
the
No. 1
feeder
bus
through
the
No. 1
bus
circuit
breakers. Circuit
protection
is
provided
through
the
circuit
breaker
function
of
the
switch
placarded
PROP
and
located
in
the
HEA
T
section
of
the
pilot
inboard
subpanel. Operating
the
propeller
deice
system
with
the
propellers
stationary
will
cause
arcing
on
the
deice
slip
ring.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
2.20.5.1
Propeller
Deice
Switches
Operation
of
the
propeller
deicing
system
is
controlled
by
the
propeller
heat
switch
and
placarded
PROP
on
the
pilot
subpanel
that
controls
an
inboard
and
outboard
heating
element
in
each
propeller
boot. The
timer
cycles
power
to
the
heating
elements. There
are
two
types
of
timers:
the
older
mechanical
type
and
the
newer
electronic
type. Regardless
of
starting
point,
both
types
of
timers
follow
a
sequencing
pattern
as
follows:
RH
propeller
,
outboard
segments
(all
three
blades)
RH
propeller
,
inboard
segments
(all
three
blades)
1
SEPTEMBER
2023
2.20-2
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1
CUI

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
LH
propeller
,
outboard
segments
(all
three
blades)
LH
propeller
,
inboard
segments
(all
three
blades). Each
of
the
above
segments
in
heated
34
±6
seconds,
normally
requiring
in
excess
of
2
minutes
to
complete
a
four
-segment
cycle. For
ground
test
purposes,
a
minimum
of
2
minutes
is
required
to
test
the
applied
amperage
of
all
four
segments. W
ith
the
older
mechanical
timer
,
the
sequencing
would
advance
to
the
next
segment
from
where
it
was
heating
when
last
utilized. Also,
the
mechanical
timer
-type
would
show
a
fluctuation
in
prop-deice
amperage
every
34
±6
seconds
as
the
timer
moved
from
one
segment
to
the
next. For
the
newer
electronic
timer
types,
the
timer
always
cycles
to
the
RH
propeller
outboard
segment
when
shut
of
f
and
commences
its
next
cycle
of
operation
from
that
point
when
reactivated. W
ith
this
type
of
timer
,
no
fluctuation
in
amperage
can
be
noted
between
segments. Regardless
of
the
type
of
timer
utilized,
each
propeller
blade
boot
draws
approximately
5
to
6
amps
of
current
and
thus
with
three
blades
drawing
power
concurrently
,
the
amperage
should
read
between
14
to
18
amps. Should
one
blade
boot
segment
be
inoperative,
a
drop
of
5
to
6
amps
should
be
anticipated
and,
likewise,
a
drop
of
10
to
12
amps
below
normal
readings
would
be
indicative
of
malfunction
on
two
propeller
blades. Refer
to
Part
V
for
abnormal
operations.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
2.20.6
Surface
Deicer
System
Ice
accumulation
is
removed
from
each
outboard
wing
leading
edge,
both
horizontal
stabilizers,
and
the
vertical
stabilizer
by
the
flexing
of
five
deicer
boots
that
are
pneumatically
actuated
(
Figures
2.20-1
and
2.20-2
). Engine
bleed
air
from
the
engine
compressor
is
used
to
supply
air
pressure
to
inflate
the
deicer
boots
and
to
supply
vacuum
through
the
distribution
valve
for
boot
hold-down
during
flight. A
pressure
regulator
protects
the
system
from
overinflation. When
the
system
is
not
in
operation,
a
distributor
valve
applies
vacuum
to
the
boots
for
hold-down. When
a
solenoid
in
the
distributor
valve
is
ener
gized
by
the
pneumatic
deicer
timer
or
when
the
surface
DEICE
CYCLE
switch
is
positioned
to
either
SINGLE
or
MANUAL,
a
servo
valve
changes
the
inlet
to
the
boots
from
vacuum
to
pressure
that
allows
the
boots
to
inflate. When
the
solenoid
valve
is
deener
gized,
the
airflow
through
the
valve
is
cut
of
f. The
air
then
dischar
ges
out
of
the
boots
through
an
integral
check
valve
until
the
pressure
reaches
approximately
1-inch
Hg,
at
which
time
the
boots
are
ported
to
vacuum
and
the
remaining
air
is
evacuated. The
boots
are
again
held
down
by
vacuum. Either
engine
is
capable
of
providing
suf
ficient
bleed
air
for
all
requirements
of
the
surface
deicer
system.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: WINDSHIELD
chunks.text:
Check
valves
in
the
bleed
air
lines
prevent
backflow
through
the
system
during
single-engine
operation. Bleed
air
passes
through
an
18-psi
regulator
and
then
enters
the
deicer
and
vacuum
systems. V
acuum
pressure
is
created
by
the
distribution
valve
and
is
proportional
to
pneumatic
pressure
by
the
deicer
pressure
regulator
valve. Regulated
pressure
is
indicated
on
a
gauge
placarded
PNEUMA
TIC
PRESS
and
located
on
the
right
side
panel. •
Stall
speeds
will
significantly
increase
with
the
wing
deice
boots
inflated. •
Cycling
the
system
rapidly
in
icing
conditions
may
cause
the
ice
to
accumulate
outside
the
contour
of
the
inflated
boots,
leading
to
the
inability
to
remove
ice
pneumatically
. Aircraft
performance
will
be
significantly
degraded
with
collected
ice. 2.20-3
1
SEPTEMBER
2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1
CUI

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
2.20.6.1
Surface
Deice
Switch
Normal
operation
of
the
surface
deice
systems
is
accomplished
by
use
of
a
three-position
switch
located
on
the
left
subpanel
(
FO-1
)
placarded
DEICE
CYCLE,
SINGLE,
OFF
,
and
MANUAL. When
the
switch
is
moved
to
the
SINGLE
position,
the
deicer
boots
automatically
inflated
for
7
to
8
seconds,
then
deflate
and
return
to
the
deflated
position. When
switched
to
the
MANUAL
position,
all
boots
of
the
system
inflate
and
stay
inflated
while
the
switch
is
in
this
mode. When
the
switch
is
released
from
the
MANUAL
position,
the
boots
in
that
system
deflate
and
the
toggle
returns
to
the
center
OFF
position. The
MANUAL
position
is
for
use
in
case
of
timer
failure. In
either
switch
position,
the
boots
cannot
be
overinflated. The
surface
deice
control
system
is
powered
by
the
subpanel
bus
No. 2
and
is
protected
by
a
circuit
breaker
placarded
SURF
ACE
DE-ICE
located
in
the
WEA
THER
section
of
the
copilot
outboard
subpanel. 2.20.7
Pitot
and
Stall
Heat
Systems
The
pitot
tubes
and
the
lift
detector
have
electrical
heating
elements
to
prevent
icing. On
the
ground,
electrical
power
to
the
stall
heating
elements
pass
from
the
No. 2
feeder
bus
through
a
current
limiter
(acting
as
a
resistor)
which
lowers
the
voltage
from
28
Vdc
to
14
Vdc
(low
heat).

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
At
lift-of
f,
a
squat
switch
on
the
right
main
landing
gear
causes
the
current
limiter
to
be
bypassed
and
makes
28
Vdc
(high
heat)
available
to
the
system. The
pitot
tube
heating
elements
are
powered
from
both
the
No. 1
and
No. 2
feeder
buses,
respectively
. 2.20.7.1
Pitot
and
Stall
Heat
Switches
The
heating
elements
are
controlled
by
circuit
switch
breakers
located
on
the
pilot's
sub-panel
(
FO-1
)
in
the
HEA
T
section
placarded
PIT
OT
LEFT
-RIGHT
and
ST
ALL
respectively
. When
a
switch
is
in
the
up
position,
the
heating
elements
of
that
particular
system
are
ener
gized. The
down
position
shuts
the
heating
elements
of
f. In
the
event
of
electrical
overload,
the
circuit
breaker
element
will
disconnect
the
heating
circuit,
tripping
the
toggle
switch
to
the
down
position. 1
SEPTEMBER
2023
2.20-6

NA

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: PERSONNEL
chunks.text:
2.21
PERSONNEL
EQUIPMENT
2.21.1
Seats
Individually
adjustable
seats
(
Figure
2.21-1
)
are
provided
for
the
pilot,
copilot,
and
student/passengers. The
cockpit
seats
are
adjustable
fore
and
aft
with
the
inboard
handle
on
each
pilot
seat
and
vertically
with
the
outboard
handle. The
inboard
armrests
will
fold
up
to
make
access
to
the
seats
easier
for
the
crew
. Handholds
on
either
side
of
the
overhead
panel
and
fold-away
protective
pedestal
step
are
provided
for
pilot
and
copilot
entry
into
the
cockpit
(
Figure
1.2-3
). Each
cockpit
seat
is
equipped
with
a
lap-type
seatbelt
and
a
shoulder
harness
connected
to
an
inertial
reel. Normally
,
three
seats
(
Figure
1.2-2
)
are
installed
on
full
length
tracks
in
the
cabin
area. These
seats
may
be
removed
for
light
car
go
or
additional
seats
may
be
added
for
passengers
if
desired. The
three
seats
normally
installed
are
adjustable
fore
and
aft
with
adjustable
headrests
and
seat
backs
that
may
be
reclined
for
individual
comfort. The
cabin
seat
backs
must
be
in
fully
upright
position
for
takeof
f
and
landing
for
maximum
crash
protection. Each
of
the
seats
in
the
cabin
area
is
equipped
with
lap-type
seatbelt
and
a
container
beneath
the
seat
for
storage
of
life
preservers. 2.21.1.1
Seatbelts/Shoulder
Harness
Each
of
the
cabin
seats
and
the
pilot
and
copilot
seats
are
equipped
with
lap-type
seatbelts
(
Figure
2.21-1
).

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: PERSONNEL
chunks.text:
The
pilot
and
copilot
seats
are
equipped
with
shoulder
harnesses
that
incorporate
an
automatic
inertial
reel
to
allow
movement
required
to
operate
the
controls. The
inertia
reels
will
lock
automatically
under
a
2
to
3g
deceleration. No
inertial
reel
lock/unlock
controls
are
required. 2.21.2
Headsets
Headsets
should
be
utilized
by
the
pilot,
copilot,
and
observer
. The
electronic
noise
cancellation
headset
connectors,
Figure
20.4-7
,
for
the
pilot
and
copilot
are
located
on
the
outboard
portion
of
the
instrument
panel
and
the
observer's
connector
is
located
below
the
oxygen
mask
plug-in
on
the
right
sidewall
just
forward
of
the
first
cabin
window
. 2.21-1
1
SEPTEMBER
2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: RESCUE
chunks.text:
2.22
RESCUE
EQUIPMENT
2.22.1
Life
Preservers
An
A
V
-8
life
preserver
is
provided
with
each
seat.
The
pilot
compartment
preservers
are
stored
in
the
pockets
on
the
seatbacks.
The
cockpit
observer
and
passenger
life
preservers
are
stored
beneath
the
respective
seats.
The
handle
on
the
life
preservers
are
placarded
LIFE
VEST
PULL.
2.22.2
Liferaft
A
type
II
seven-man
liferaft
(
Figure
1.2-2
)
is
located
on
the
seat
tracks
opposite
the
main
entrance
door
.
The
liferaft
assembly
is
secured
to
the
seat
tracks
by
a
three-piece
strap
assembly
that
anchors
to
the
seat
tracks
with
four
tiedown
fittings.
The
proper
location
for
the
tiedown
fitting
is
identified
with
red
insignia
paint
on
the
seat
tracks.
The
equipment
container
contains
a
mirror
,
whistle,
compass,
casualty
blanket,
sponge,
bagged
water
,
rations,
sunscreen,
knife,
sea
dye
marker
,
a
survival
radio,
day/night
flares
and
a
strobe
light.
2.22-1
1
SEPTEMBER
2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1
CUI

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CARGO
chunks.text:
2.23
CARGO
PROVISIONS
The
cabin
area
can
be
quickly
converted
to
a
combination
passenger/car
go,
air
ambulance,
or
all
configurations.
Refer
to
Part
VIII
for
additional
information.
2.23.1
T
iedown
Provisions
Four
tiedown
straps
rated
at
2,000
pounds
each
are
provided
for
securing
light
car
go.
The
straps
are
approximately
10
feet
in
length
and
have
a
brownline
buckle
incorporated
to
adjust
strap
length.
The
tiedown
straps
are
anchored
to
the
seat
tracks
with
quick
removable
tiedown
fittings.
A
webbing
is
also
provided
to
secure
baggage
in
the
far
aft
compartment.
The
tiedown
straps
and
webbing
for
securing
the
baggage
area
are
located
with
the
aircraft
loose
tools.
2.23-1
1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: EMERGENCY
chunks.text:
2.24
EMERGENCY
EQUIPMENT
2.24.1
Hand
Fire
Extinguishers
T
wo
2.5
lb
Halon
121
1
hand-type
fire
extinguishers
are
installed
in
the
aircraft. One
is
stored
beneath
the
copilot
seat;
another
is
stored
on
the
left-hand
seat
riser
just
forward
of
the
airstair
door
. Repeated
or
prolonged
exposure
to
and/or
inhalation
of
high
concentrations
of
extinguishing
agent
or
its
decomposition
products
should
be
avoided. The
liquid
may
cause
frostbite
if
allowed
to
contact
the
skin. 2.24.2
First-Aid
Kit
A
first-aid
kit
is
stored
on
the
forward
side
of
the
stub
partition
in
the
aft
cabin. The
kit
is
removed
by
pulling
the
red
hinge
pin. 2.24.3
Emergency
Locator
T
ransmitter
The
Artex
C406N
series
EL
T
is
a
single
output
EL
T
that
incorporates
latitude/longitude
information
from
the
aircraft
navigation
system. All
3
emer
gency
frequencies
(121.5/243.0
and
406.028
MHz)
utilize
the
same
RF
output,
which
requires
only
1
coax
cable
to
connect
to
the
single
input
antenna. The
EL
T
automatically
activates
during
a
crash
and
transmits
the
standard
swept
tone
on
121.5
and
243.0
MHz. Every
50
seconds
for
520
milliseconds
(long
message
protocol)
the
406.028
MHz
transmitter
turns
on
and
transmits
an
encoded
digital
message
to
the
Cospas/Sarsat
satellite
system. The
EL
T
is
located
in
the
lower
right
rear
section
of
the
fuselage
aft
of
the
pressure
bulkhead.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: EMERGENCY
chunks.text:
The
ON/ARM/RESET
remote
switch
is
located
above
the
V/UHF
radio
and
to
the
right
of
the
FGP
. Y
ou
cannot
disarm
or
disable
the
unit
from
the
cockpit. Cockpit
operation
is
limited
to
deactivating
the
EL
T
after
it
has
been
activated,
or
manually
activating
the
EL
T
. 2.24-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: MISCELLANEOUS
chunks.text:
2.25
MISCELLANEOUS
EQUIPMENT
2.25.1
Lavatory
A
forward
facing,
nonflushing
lavatory
(
Figure
1.2-2
)
is
located
just
aft
of
the
entrance
door
.
The
lavatory
may
be
screened
from
the
cabin
area
by
a
privacy
curtain.
A
storage
area
beneath
the
seat
area
provides
space
for
toilet
items.
2.25.2
Relief
T
ube
A
relief
tube
assembly
consisting
of
a
plastic
horn,
a
normally
closed
valve
handle,
and
flexible
tubing
to
the
overboard
drain
is
provided
on
the
left
sidewall
in
the
lavatory
area.
Prior
to
use,
the
flexible
tube
must
be
fully
extended
and
the
valve
handle
depressed.
2.25.3
Sun
V
isors
T
wo
sun
visors
are
provided
for
the
pilot
and
copilot.
Each
visor
is
manually
adjustable
and
detachable.
2.25.4
Curtains
A
foldback
curtain
is
located
aft
to
provide
privacy
in
the
toilet
area.
This
curtain
is
fixed
to
the
left
sidewall
and
when
drawn
in
fastened
to
the
stub
partition.
Additionally
,
each
of
the
cabin
windows
is
provided
with
a
vertical
sliding
shade
to
provide
blockage
of
light
in
the
cabin
area.
2.25-1
1

2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: DIGIT
chunks.text:
2.26
DIGIT
AL
ENGINE
INDICA
T
ORS
The
aircraft
is
equipped
with
digital
microprocessor
-based
engine
indicators. Each
indicator
is
electrically
powered
by
its
respective
dc
electrical
bus
and
protected
with
a
maximum
5
amp
circuit
breaker
located
on
the
right
sidewall
circuit
breaker
panel. Each
indicator
is
backlit
and
dimmable
using
the
engine
indicator
lighting
rheostat. Each
indicator
displays
the
engine
parameter
based
upon
a
signal
from
the
engine
transmitters. Note
If
flying
on
auxiliary
battery
only
,
aircraft
engine
instruments
will
be
inoperative. For
single
instruments
the
display
is
indicated
by
a
rotating
pointer
showing
power
trends
and
limitations
against
a
fixed
scale
plate. Better
resolution
is
also
provided
via
a
digital
display
. 2.26.1
Digital
Engine
Indicators
Self
T
est
Upon
initial
power
-up,
each
indicator
performs
a
self-test. During
this
test
and
prior
to
assuming
normal
operation:
1. The
digital
display
initially
displays
“OK”,
followed
by
“--------”,
then
when
the
self
test
is
complete
the
actual
engine
indication. 2. The
status
LED
illuminates
red
then
green
for
two
cycles,
then
extinguishes. 3. The
pointer
is
driven
from
of
f
scale
to
the
full
scale
position,
followed
by
the
of
f
scale
zero
position,
then
displays
the
actual
indication.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: DIGIT
chunks.text:
2.26.2
Digital
Engine
Indicators
Normal
Operation
During
normal
operation
each
indicator
is
conducting
a
continuous
self-test
on
the
indicator
and
the
transmitter
’
s
signal. The
result
of
this
self-test
is
displayed
using
the
status
LED
(
Figure
2.26-1
). Figure
2.26-1. Status
LED
T
able
ST
A
TUS
LED
ILLUMINA
TION
DESCRIPTION
None
Instrument
is
functioning
normally
Flashing
Red
(2x
per
second)
Instrument
failed
self-test
Flashing
Red
(4x
per
second)
•
with
the
digital
display
dashed
•
with

display

a

and

.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VICING
chunks.text:
3.2
SER
VICING
THE
FUEL
SYSTEM
The
complete
aircraft
fuel
system
consists
of
two
separate
engine
fuel
systems
connected
by
a
crossfeed
system. Each
engine
fuel
system
has
a
61-gallon
(414
pounds)
nacelle
tank
and
four
interconnected
wing
tanks
that
have
capacities
of
40
gallons
(271.6
pounds),
25
gallons
(170
pounds),
23
gallons
(156
pounds),
and
44
gallons
(299
pounds). The
total
tank
capacity
for
each
engine
system
is
193
gallons
(1,310.5
pounds)
of
which
192
gallons
(1,303.7
pounds)
are
usable. The
complete
aircraft
fuel
system
contains
387.6
gallons
(2,631.8
pounds)
of
which
384
gallons
(2,607.4
pounds)
are
usable. Fuel
weights
are
based
on
JP-5,
standard
day
(6.79
pounds). Note
•
Service
fuel
tanks
after
each
flight
to
keep
the
bladder
-type
tanks
from
drying
out. •
At
least
30
minutes
after
servicing
the
tanks,
open
the
sump
drains
and
fuel
strainer
drains
and
allow
a
small
amount
of
fuel
to
drain
from
each
of
these
points. 3.2.1
T
ype
of
Fuel
The
primary
fuels
for
the
T
-44C
are
JP-4,
JP-5,
JP-8,
F-24,
Jet
A,
Jet
A1
and
Jet
B. Consult
the
fuel
reference
charts
(
Figure
3.2-1
). Commercial
jet
fuels
do
not
contain
anti-ice/fungicide
(PF
A55MB,
MIL-I-27686,
or
equivalent). When
commercial
fuels
are
procured,
fuel
thickening
may
occur
when
temperatures
are
less
than
-40
°F
and
long-term
fungus
control
may
be
degraded.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VICING
chunks.text:
When
adding
commercial
jet
fuel
in
quantities
greater
than
104
gallons
(approximately
700
pounds)
in
each
wing,
20
fluid
ounces
of
anti-ice/fungicide
additive
(PRIST)
should
be
added
to
each
wing
when
required. PRIST
should
be
added
gradually
during
filling
to
permit
increased
blending
in
the
fuel. •
JP-8+100
shall
not
be
used
in
the
T
-44C. This
fuel
will
clog
the
fuel
filter
. •
PRIST
injected
into
the
fuel
from
aerosol
cans
as
it
is
pumped
into
the
aircraft
is
not
recommended. It
does
not
mix
well
with
the
fuel,
has
a
tendency
to
settle
to
the
bottom
of
tanks,
and
may
damage
fuel
system
seals
and
fuel
tank
materials. Note
If
PRIST
is
injected
via
aerosol
cans,
special
attention
should
be
paid
to
low
point
drain
samples
for
signs
of
deteriorating
fuel
cells,
i.e. fuel
cell
coatings,
bladder
material,
or
corrosion-born
material. Injection
of
aerosol
PRIST
increases
the
risk
of
deterioration. Request
refueler
assistance
for
proper
fuel/additive
blending
during
the
aircraft
refueling. The
following
procedure
will
be
utilized
when
blending
anti-icing
additive,
complying
with
MIL-I-27686,
as
the
aircraft
is
being
refueled
through
wing
fillers. 1. When
using
HI-FLO
PRIST
blender
(model
PHF-204),
remove
the
cap
retaining
tube
and
clip
assembly
. 2. Attach
pistol
grip
on
collar
. 3. Press
tube
into
button. 4. Clip
tube
end
to
fuel
nozzle. 5.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VICING
chunks.text:
Pull
trigger
firmly
to
assure
a
full
flow
capability
and
lock
in
place. 6. Start
flow
of
additive
when
refueling
begins. (Refueling
should
be
at
50
gal/min
minimum
and
60
gal/min
maximum.)
A
rate
of
less
than
30
gal/min
may
be
utilized
when
topping
of
f
tanks. 3.2-1

Fuel
definitions:
Primary
fuel
—
A

—

or

is
available

an

need
exists
(such

weather

evacuation
or
urgent
military

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
Pilot
approval
shall
be
obtained
before
servicing
and
the
aircraft
shall
be
conspicuously
placarded
with
the
emergency
fuel
grade
and
maintenance
departments
shall
be
informed
when
serviced
with
any
emergency
fuel. For
additional
information
on
aviation
fuels,
consult
the
following:
1. NA
V
AIR
00-80T
-109,
Aircraft
Refueling
NA
T
OPS
Manual. 2. MIL-HDBK-844B
(AS),
Refueling
Handbook
for
Navy/Marine
Corps
Aircraft. Ensure
that
the
additive
is
directed
into
the
flowing
fuel
stream
and
that
additive
flow
is
started
after
fuel
flow
starts
and
is
stopped
before
fuel
flow
stops. Do
not
allow
concentrated
additive
to
contact
the
coated
interior
of
fuel
cells
or
aircraft
painted
surfaces. Use
no
less
than
20
fluid
ounces
of
additive
per
260
gallons
of
fuel
added
or
more
than
20
fluid
ounces
of
additive
per
104
gallons
of
fuel
added. In
the
event
aviation
kerosene
is
not
available,
aviation
gasoline
grades
80/87,
100/130,
100LL,
or
1
15/145
are
approved
emer

viation

adverse
ef
fect
on

engines. The
lowest
octane
(A
VGAS)
available
(lowest
lead
content)
should
be
used. 1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Fuel
chunks.text:
3.2.2
Fuel
Handling
Procedures
While
handling
fuel,
remember
the
aircraft
uses
F-24
as
its
principal
fuel. A
viation
gasoline
is
also
an
emer
gency
fuel. When
aviation
gasoline
is
used
in
a
turbine
engine,
extreme
caution
should
be
used
when
around
the
combustion
chamber
and
exhaust
area
to
avoid
cuts
or
abrasions. The
exhaust
deposits
contain
lead
oxide
that
will
cause
lead
poisoning. Proper
procedures
for
handling
fuel
cannot
be
overstressed. Clean,
fresh
fuel
must
be
used
and
the
entrance
of
water
into
the
fuel
storage
or
aircraft
fuel
system
must
be
avoided. When
conditions
permit,
the
aircraft
should
be
positioned
so
that
the
wind
will
carry
the
fuel
vapors
away
from
all
possible
sources
of
ignition. The
fueling
vehicle
shall
be
positioned
to
maintain
a
minimum
distance
of
10
feet
from
any
part
of
the
aircraft,
while
maintaining
a
minimum
distance
of
20
feet
between
the
fueling
vehicle
and
the
fuel
filler
point. Note
•
The
use
of
aviation
gasoline
as
an
emer
gency
fuel
is
permitted
for
a
period
of
not
more
than
150
hours
during
time
between
engine
overhaul. It
may
be
used
in
any
quantity
with
aviation
kerosene
fuel. Each
engine
uses
approximately
45
gallons
of
fuel
per
hour
. T
o
determine
the
number
of
hours
that
should
be
char
ged
against
the
150-hour
TBO
period,
divide
the
number
of
gallons
of
gasoline
in
each
side
by
45
gallons.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Fuel
chunks.text:
Example:
If
135
gallons
of
gasoline
are
pumped
into
one
side,
divide
by
45
and
the
total
time
is
3
hours. The
engine
should
have
3
hours
char
ged
against
the
150-hour
TBO
period. The
lowest
octane
aviation
gasoline
available
is
recommended
to
avoid
buildup
of
deposits
on
the
turbine
blades. •
Do
not
allow
operation
of
any
electrical
tools,
such
as
drills
or
buf
fers,
in
or
near
the
aircraft
during
fueling. 1. Shut
of
f
all
electrical
equipment
on
the
aircraft,
including
radar
equipment. The
battery
switch
may
be
left
on,
but
it
must
not
be
moved
during
the
fueling
operation. 2. Refuel
aircraft
as
soon
as
possible
after
landing. 3. Keep
fuel
servicing
nozzles
free
of
snow
,
water
,
and
mud
at
all
times. 4. Always
fill
nacelle
tank
before
wing
tanks. Note
Should
the
wing
tanks
be
filled
first
when
the
associated
nacelle
tank
quantity
is
less
than
three-eighths
filled,
gravity
feeding
to
the
nacelle
tank
would
leave
the
wing
tanks
less
than
full. 5. Carefully
remove
snow
,
water
,
and
ice
from
the
aircraft
fuel
filler
cap
area
before
removing
the
fuel
filler
cap. Remove
only
one
aircraft
tank
filler
cap
at
any
one
time
and
replace
each
one
immediately
after
the
servicing
operation
is
completed. 6. W
ipe
all
frost
from
fuel
filler
necks
before
servicing. 7. In
so
far
as
possible,
leave
only
fully
serviced
aircraft
outside. 8.

ash

. 13. Handle

gized
airborne

equipment
or

gized

radar
equipment
installations. 15. W
ear
only
nonsparking

near

or
fueling
equipment
since
shoes
with

soles
or
metal
heel
plates
can
be
a
source
of
sparks. 3.2.2.1
Fuel
Handling
Precautions
—
Extreme
W
eather
Conditions
When
fueling
is
conducted
on
ice
or
on
sandy
or
desert
terrain
or
when
a
satisfactory
ground
cannot
be
secured,
additional
precautions
must
be
taken
to
avoid
the
buildup
of
static
electricity
. Since
draining
the
static
char
ge
is
not
possible
without
an
adequate
ground,
reliance
must
be
placed
on
equalizing
the
potentials
in
order
to
prevent
a
dangerous
sparking
dischar
ge
as
in
the
following
bonding
procedure:
1. Connect
bonding
cable
from
aircraft
to
fuel
servicing
unit. A
conductive-type
fuel
hose
is
not
a
satisfactory
method
of
bonding. 2.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Fuel
chunks.text:
Connect
bonding
cable
from
fuel
nozzle
to
aircraft
before
fuel
tank
cover
is
opened. 3. When
disconnecting,
reverse
the
order
of
Steps
1
and
2
. 3.2.3
Gravity
Fueling
Refer
to
Figure
3.2-2
. 1. Attach
bonding
cables
to
aircraft. 2. Attach
bonding
cable
from
hose
nozzle
to
ground. 3. Open
applicable
fuel
tank
filler
cap. 4. Fill
fuel
tank
with
fuel
using
slow
fuel
dischar
ge
rate. Do
not
insert
fuel
nozzle
completely
into
fuel
cell
because
of
possible
damage
to
bottom
of
fuel
cell. 5. Secure
applicable
fuel
tank
filler
cap. Ensure
latch
tab
on
cap
is
pointed
aft. If
latch
tab
isn't
fully
secure,
or
if
tab
isn't
pointing
aft,
air
load
after
takeof
f
could
result
in
fuel
siphoning
or
the
fuel
cap
departing
the
aircraft. 6. Disconnect
bonding
cables
from
aircraft. 3.2.4
Draining
Moisture
From
Fuel
System
Open
each
fuel
drain
daily
to
drain
of
f
any
water
or
other
contamination
collected
in
the
low
places. Along
with
the
drain
on
the
firewall-mounted
fuel
filter
,
there
are
four
other
drains
(
Figure
7.2-2
);
the
nacelle
tank
fuel
pump
drain,

section
tank
transfer
pump
drain,
wheelwell
drain,
and
the
inboard
end
of
the
wing
leading
edge
tank
drain. The
fuel
pump
and
tank
drains
are
accessible
from
the
underside
of
the
aircraft
while
the
fuel
filter
drain
is
reached
inside
the
top
cowl
door
. 1

ype

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VICING
chunks.text:
3.3
SER
VICING
THE
OIL
SYSTEM
Servicing
the
engine
oil
system
primarily
involves
maintaining
the
engine
oil
at
the
proper
level. The
oil
tank
is
provided
with
an
oil
filler
neck
and
quantity
dipstick
cap
that
protrude
through
the
accessory
gearcase
at
the
1
1-o'clock
position. The
dipstick
is
marked
in
U.S. quarts
and
indicates
the
amount
of
oil
required
to
fill
the
tank. Access
to
the
dipstick
cap
is
gained
by
opening
the
engine
cowl. Oil
tank
capacity
is
2.3
U.S. gallons
with
5
quarts
measured
on
the
dipstick
for
adding
purposes. When
a
dry
engine
is
first
serviced
it
will
require
5
quarts
in
addition
to
tank
capacity
to
fill
the
lines
and
cooler
,
giving
a
total
system
capacity
of
14
quarts. If
oil
quantity
indicates
more
than
3
quarts
low
,
motor
starter
for
15
seconds
and
recheck. If
quantity
is
still
low
,
add
oil. Do
not
overfill. Do
not
mix
dif
ferent
brands
or
types
of
oil
when
adding
oil
between
changes. Dif
ferent
brands
or
types
of
oil
may
be
incompatible
because
of
the
dif
ferences
in
chemical
structure. Spilled
oil
should
be
removed
immediately
to
prevent
possible
tire
contamination
or
damage. 1. Open
upper
right
cowling
to
gain
access
to
the
oil
filler
cap. 2. Remove
oil
filler
cap. 3. Replenish
oil
to
within
2
quarts
below
MAX
(HOT
or
COLD
as
applicable)
mark
on
dipstick. 4. Check
oil
filler
cap
for
damaged
performed
packing,
general
condition. 5.

fluid.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VICING
chunks.text:
3.5
SER
VICING
OXYGEN
SYSTEM
The
oxygen
system
is
serviced
(
Figure
3.5-1
)
by
a
filler
valve
accessible
by
removing
the
access
plate
on
the
right
side
of
the
aft
fuselage. The
system
has
two
pressure
gauges,
one
on
the
right
side
panel
in
the
pilot
compartment
for
in-flight
use
and
one
adjacent
to
the
filler
valve
for
checking
system
pressure
during
filling. 3.5.1
Oxygen
System
Safety
Precaution
1. A
void
any
operation
that
would
create
sparks
and
keep
all
burning
cigarettes
or
fire
away
from
the
vicinity
of
the
aircraft
when
the
outlets
are
in
use. 2. Inspect
the
filler
connection
for
cleanliness
before
attaching
it
to
the
filler
valve. 3. Make
sure
that
hands,
tools,
and
clothing
are
clean,
particularly
of
grease
or
oil
stains,
for
these
contaminants
will
ignite
upon
contact
with
pure
oxygen. 4. As
a
further
precaution
against
fire,
open
and
close
all
oxygen
valves
slowly
during
filling. 3.5.2
Filling
the
System
Fill
the
oxygen
system
slowly
by
adjusting
the
rechar
ging
rate
with
the
pressure
regulating
valve
on
the
servicing
cart. Fill
the
cylinder
to
a
pressure
of
1,850
±50
psi
at
a
temperature
of
70
°F
. This
pressure
may
be
increased
an
additional
3.5
psi
for
each
degree
of
increase
in
temperature;
similarly
,
for
each
degree
of
drop
in
temperature,
reduce
the
pressure
for
the
cylinder
by
3.5
psi.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VICING
chunks.text:
The
oxygen
system,
after
filling,
will
need
to
cool
and
stabilize
for
a
short
period
before
an
accurate
reading
on
the
gauges
can
be
obtained. When
the
system
is
properly
char
ged,
disconnect
the
filler
hose
from
the
filler
valve
and
replace
the
protective
cap
on
the
filler
valve. 3.5-1
1

2023

chunks.text:
3.6
SER
VICING
THE
FIRE
EXTINGUISHER
SYSTEM
A
fire
extinguisher
supply
cylinder
is
mounted
on
brackets
at
the
aft
side
of
the
main
spar
in
each
wheelwell
of
the
main
landing
gear
.
Access
to
the
fire
extinguisher
cylinder
is
through
the
wheelwells
of
the
main
landing
gear
.
3.6.1
Cylinder
Pressure
Each
cylinder
is
char
ged
with
2-1/2
pounds
of
bromotrifluoromethane
and
pressurized
with
.08
pound
of
dry
nitrogen
to
450
psi
at
70
°F
.
Check
the
pressure
gauge
on
each
cylinder
prior
to
flight
to
ascertain
that
the
cylinders
are
char
ged
to
within
the
pressure
limits
for
the
ambient
temperatures
as
noted
in
the
chart
below
.

POINTS

chunks.heading: VICING
chunks.text:
3.8
SER
VICING
TIRES
3.8.1
T
ire
Sizes
The
T
-44C
is
equipped
with
8.50
X
10,
8
ply-rated,
tubeless
tires
on
the
main
landing
gear
wheels
and
a
6.50
X
10,
6
ply-rated,
tubeless
tire
on
the
nose.
T
ires
that
have
picked
up
a
fuel
or
oil
film
should
be
washed
down
as
soon
as
possible
with
a
deter
gent
solution
to
prevent
contamination
of
the
rubber
.
3.8.1.1
Inflation
Pressures
Maintaining
proper
tire
inflation
will
help
to
avoid
damage
from
landing
shock
and
contact
with
sharp
stones
and
ruts
and
will
minimize
tread
wear
.
When
inflating
the
tires,
inspect
them
for
cuts,
cracks,
breaks,
and
tread
wear
.
The
main
tires
should
be
inflated
between
54
and
60
psi
and
the
nose
tire
between
50
and
55.
For
soft
field
takeof
fs,
30
to
32
psi
will
work
in
an
emer
gency
situation.
3.8-1
1

2023
CUI
NA

01-T44AAC-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: EXTERNAL
chunks.text:
3.9
EXTERNAL
POWER
APPLICA
TION
3.9.1
Safety
Precautions
•
Before
connecting
the
power
cables
from
the
external
power
source
to
the
aircraft,
the
following
considerations
should
be
observed:
—
Ensure
that
the
GPU
is
not
touching
the
aircraft
at
any
point. Should
they
come
in
contact
while
the
GPU
is
operating,
arcing
and
aircraft
damage
could
occur
. This
is
due
to
voltage
drop
in
the
cables,
which
results
in
the
two
ground
systems
having
dif
ferent
voltage
potentials. —
Failure
to
turn
of
f
all
external
power
while
connecting
or
removing
the
power
cable
from
the
external
power
supply
receptacle
could
result
in
aircraft
damage. The
aircraft
electrical
system
is
protected
against
damage
from
an
external
power
source
with
reversed
polarity
by
a
relay
and
diodes
in
the
external
power
circuit. Observe
the
following
precautions
when
using
an
external
power
source. 1. Use
only
a
ground
power
source
that
is
negatively
grounded. If
the
polarity
of
the
power
source
is
unknown,
determine
the
polarity
with
a
voltmeter
before
connecting
the
unit
to
the
aircraft. Failure
to
ensure
the
polarity
of
the
external
power
source
is
the
same
as
that
of
the
aircraft
before
it
is
connected
could
result
in
aircraft
damage. 2. Before
connecting
an
external
power
unit,
ensure
that
a
battery
capable
of
18
volts
minimum
is
installed
in
the
aircraft
and
that
the
battery
switch
is
ON.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: EXTERNAL
chunks.text:
All
other
electrical
and
avionics
equipment
should
be
turned
OFF
to
prevent
damage
from
transient
voltage
spikes. T
o
ensure
avionics
equipment
is
secured,
A
VIONICS
NO. 1,
NO. 2,
NO. 3,
and
Essential
circuit
breakers
on
the
control
pedestal
shall
be
pulled. 3. If
the
unit
does
not
have
a
standard
AN
plug,
check
the
polarity
and
connect
the
positive
lead
from
the
external
power
unit
to
the
center
post
and
the
negative
lead
to
the
front
post
of
the
aircraft's
external
power
receptacle. The
small
pin
of
the
receptacle
must
be
supplied
with
+24
Vdc
to
close
the
external
power
relay
that
provides
protection
against
damage
by
reversing
polarity
. 3.9.2
V
oltage
Requirements
T
o
supply
power
for
ground
checks
or
to
assist
in
starting,
a
ground
power
source
shall
not
exceed
28
Vdc
and
must
be
capable
of
delivering
a
continuous
load
of
300
amperes
and
up
to
1,000
amperes
maximum
for
0.1
second
if
required. 3.9.3
Receptacle
Location
The
external
power
receptacle
is
located
just
outboard
of
the
nacelle
in
the
right
center
section. The
receptacle
is
designed
for
a
standard
AN
type
plug. 3.9-1
1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: OWING
chunks.text:
3.10
T
OWING
THE
AIRCRAFT
Note
When
maneuvering
the
aircraft
in
congested
areas,
allow
several
inches
for
clearance
of
hangar
doors
or
other
obstructions. Refer
to
Figure
3.10-1
. The
tow
bar
connects
to
the
upper
torque
knee
fitting
of
the
nosestrut. Use
a
tow
bar
when
moving
it
by
hand
or
for
towing
the
aircraft
with
a
tug. 3.10.1
T
owing
Procedures
Although
the
tug
will
control
the
steering
of
the
aircraft,
a
qualified
person
must
be
positioned
in
the
pilot
seat
to
operate
the
brakes
in
case
of
emer
gency
. Always
ascertain
that
the
rudder
controls
lock
is
removed
before
towing
the
aircraft. Never
exceed
the
turning
limits
marked
on
the
nosegear
strut
during
ground
handling. The
nosegear
steering
stop
block
is
designed
to
withstand
the
loads
normally
imposed
through
steering
from
the
cockpit
and
not
to
prevent
turn
limitations
from
being
exceeded
during
towing. •
Use
of
a
universal
tow
bar
that
connects
to
the
nosewheel
axle
may
seriously
damage
the
cotter
pin
installed
in
the
nosewheel
castle
nut
and
result
in
the
nosewheel
becoming
separated
from
the
aircraft. After
universal
tow
bar
has
been
used,
the
cotter
pin
must
be
inspected
for
damage. •
Serious
damage
can
occur
to
the
steering
linkage
if
the
aircraft
is
towed
with
the
rudder
control
lock
installed. •
It
is
possible
to
overcome
the
stop
during
ground
handling,
causing
damage
to
the
steering
linkage
and
nosestrut.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: TIEDOWN/SECURING
chunks.text:
3.1
1
TIEDOWN/SECURING
THE
AIRCRAFT
The
proper
steps
for
securing
the
aircraft
(
Figure
3.1
1-1
)
must
be
based
on
the
time
the
aircraft
will
be
left
unattended,
the
aircraft
weight,
the
expected
wind
direction
and
velocity
,
and
the
anticipated
availability
of
ground
and
aircrews
for
mooring
and/or
evacuation. When
practical,
head
the
aircraft
into
the
wind,
especially
if
strong
winds
are
forecast
or
if
it
will
be
necessary
to
leave
the
aircraft
overnight. Set
the
parking
brake
and
chock
the
wheels
securely
. Following
engine
shutdown,
position
and
engage
the
controls
lock
(
Figure
2.12-1
). 3.1
1.1
T
iedown
Locations
Mooring
points
are
provided
beneath
the
wings,
tail,
and
on
each
main
landing
gear
. 3.1
1.1.1
Propeller
Restraint,
Dust
Covers,
Wheel
Chocks
Propeller
restraints
and
wheel
chocks
shall
be
used
whenever
the
aircraft
is
moored. At
pilot
discretion,
dust
covers
may
be
used
for
the
pilot
masts,
engine
intake
and
exhaust,
and
the
heat
exchanger
leading
edge
intake. 3.1
1.2
Mooring
Procedures
1. Use
mooring
cables
of
1/4-inch
aircraft
cable
and
clamp
(clip-wire
rope),
chain
or
rope
3/8-inch
or
over
. Length
of
the
cable
or
rope
will
be
dependent
upon
existing
circumstances. Allow
suf
ficient
slack
in
ropes,
chains,
or
cable
to
compensate
for
tightening
action
because
of
moisture
absorption
of
rope
or
thermal
contraction
of
cable
or
chain.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: TIEDOWN/SECURING
chunks.text:
Using
slip
knots
to
secure
aircraft
to
mooring
stakes
may
result
in
improper
tie-down
restraint/tensioning. 2. One-piece
wheel
chocks
or
wood
blocks
may
be
used
to
chock
the
main
landing
gear
wheels. They
must
be
equipped
with
rope
or
wood
cleats
to
retain
them
against
the
wheels
(
Figure
3.1
1-1
). Note
In
ice
or
snow
conditions,
collapsible
ice
grip
wheel
chocks
should
be
used. However
,
sandbags
may
be
used
if
collapsible
chocks
are
not
available
or
if
parking
or
mooring
the
aircraft
on
steel
mats. 3.1
1.2.1
High
W
ind
Conditions
If
an
aircraft
is
to
remain
securely
moored
during
high
velocity
winds,
it
is
necessary
to
use
the
proper
size
and
type
of
wheel
chock. Since
the
factor
of
weight
is
significant
in
determining
adequate
mooring
provisions,
the
approximate
weight
must
be
known
if
the
aircraft
is
to
be
properly
secured. During
emer
gencies,
knowledge
of
this
information
is
very
useful
in
selecting
the
aircraft
that
should
be
tied
down
first
since
heavy
aircraft
will
better
stand
high
winds
than
an
empty
aircraft. Structural
damage
can
occur
from
high
velocity
winds;
therefore,
if
at
all
possible,
the
aircraft
should
be
moved
to
a
safe
weather
area
when
winds
above
75
knots
are
expected. 1. After
aircraft
is
properly
located,
place
nosewheel
in
centered
position.

or

,

45°

is

. Locate

at
slightly
more

. Make

with

or

3,000-pound

so

to

all
slack. (Use
a
3/4-inch
or

ger

if

or

is
not

pounds. 6.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CHAPTER
chunks.text:
CHAPTER
4
Operating
Limitations
4.1
INTRODUCTION
This
chapter
includes
all
important
limits
and
restrictions
that
shall
be
observed
during
ground
and
flight
operations.
The
operating
limitations
set
forth
are
the
direct
results
of
design
analysis,
test,
and
operating
experiences.
Compliance
with
these
limits
will
allow
the
pilot
to
safely
perform
the
assigned
missions
and
to
derive
maximum
utility
from
the
aircraft.
Limits
concerning
maneuvers,
weight,
and
center
-of-gravity
limitations
are
also
covered.
4.1.1
Exceeding
Operational
Limits
Any
time
an
operational
limit
is
exceeded,
each
occurrence
must
be
properly
recorded
on
the
appropriate
maintenance
forms.
Each
entry
shall
state
what
limit
or
limits
were
exceeded,
range,
time
above
limits,
and
any
additional
data
that
would
aid
maintenance
personnel
in
the
inspection
that
may
be
required.
4.1-1

chunks.text:
4.2
SYSTEM
LIMIT
A
TIONS
4.2.1
Instrument
Markings
Instruments
which
display
operating
limitations
are
illustrated
in
Figure
4.2-1
. The
operating
limitations
are
color
coded
on
the
instrument
faces. Color
coding
of
each
instrument
is
explained
in
the
illustration. The
instrument
illustration
also
denotes,
in
bold
type,
the
fuel
grade
upon
which
limits
are
based. Red
markings
on
the
dial
faces
indicate
the
limit
above
or
below
which
continued
operation
is
likely
to
cause
damage
or
shorten
life. The
green
markings
indicated
the
safe
or
normal
range
of
operation. The
yellow
markings
indicate
the
range
when
special
attention
should
be
given
to
the
operation
covered
by
the
instrument. Operation
is
permissible
in
the
yellow
range,
but
should
be
avoided. Note
The
indicator
dial
and
pointer
is
the
primary
indication
of
each
indicator
. As
such
and
due
to
possible
internal
unit
of
measure
limits,
minor
dif
ferences
between
the
dial
and
digital
indication
may
be
seen. 4.2.2
Propeller
Limitations
Propeller
limitations
consist
of
rpm
limits. The
normal
propeller
operating
range
(green
arc)
extends
form
1,800
to
2,200
rpm
with
a
red
line
at
2,200
rpm. 4.2.3
Starter
Limitations
The
starters
are
limited
to
an
operating
period
of
40
seconds
on,
60
seconds
of
f
for
two
cycles;
then
40
seconds
on,
30
minutes
of
f
on
the
third.

Management
Airframe
and

fuel

may

if

below

specified
freeze

o

chunks.heading: ENGINE
chunks.text:
4.3
ENGINE
LIMIT
A
TIONS
The
conditions
which
set
limits
for
operation
of
the
PT6A-34B
engines
are
monitored
by
instruments. An
individual
instrument
will
show
the
torque
applied
to
the
shaft
which
drives
the
propeller
. Other
indicators
display
interstage
turbine
temperature
gas
generator
speed
(N
1
),
and
propeller
speed
(N
P
). A
dual
indications
instrument
displays
both
oil
pressure
and
oil
temperature. Figure
4.3-1
shows
all
operating
conditions
and
limits
for
the
engine. 4.3.1
Overtemperature
Limitations
During
engine
start,
the
temperature
and
time
limits
listed
in
the
engine
operating
limitation
chart
(
Figure
4.3-1
)
must
be
observed. When
these
limits
are
exceeded,
the
engine
shall
be
shut
down
and
the
flight
terminated. Note
Report
amount
and
duration
of
start
ITT
temperature
exceeding
925
°C
to
maintenance. Include
with
the
report
any
secondary
indication
observed. Whenever
the
limiting
temperatures
(other
than
for
start)
of
the
engine
operating
limitation
chart
(
Figure
4.3-1
)
are
exceeded
and
cannot
be
brought
into
limits
by
retarding
the
power
levers,
secure
the
engine
and
land
as
soon
as
possible. 4.3.2
Overspeed
Limitations
Whenever
the
prescribed
engine
overspeed
limit
or
engine
rpm
operating
limit
is
exceeded,
the
incident
must
be
reported
as
an
engine
discrepancy
in
the
appropriate
maintenance
forms.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: ENGINE
chunks.text:
It
is
particularly
important
to
record
the
maximum
percent
of
rpm
registered
by
the
tachometer
and
the
duration
of
overspeed. 4.3.3
Power
Definitions
The
following
definitions
will
describe
the
engine
power
ratings
listed
in
the
engine
operating
limitation
chart
(
Figure
4.3-1
). 1. Maximum
allowable
power
—
The
maximum
power
available
from
the
engines
for
takeof
f. T
ime
limited
to
5
minutes. 2. Maximum
continuous
power
—
The
maximum
power
available
from
the
engines
not
limited
by
time. Use
of
this
rating
is
intended
for
emer
gency
use
at
the
discretion
of
the
pilot. 3. Cruise
climb
—
The
maximum
power
approved
for
normal
climb. 4. Cruise
—
The
power
required
from
the
engines
to
maintain
level
flight
at
a
desired
altitude
while
traveling
at
an
airspeed
within
the
normal
operating
range
approved
for
the
aircraft. Cruise
power
will
vary
with
altitude
and
airspeed. 4.3.4
Minimum
Oil
T
emperature
Required
for
Flight
Engine
oil
is
used
to
heat
the
fuel
on
entering
the
fuel
control. Since
no
temperature
measurement
is
available
for
the
fuel
at
this
point,
it
must
be
assumed
to
be
the
same
as
the
OA
T
. Figure
4.3-2
is
supplied
to
use
as
a
guide
in
preflight
planning,
based
on
known
or
forecast
operating
conditions,
to
allow
the
pilot
to
become
aware
of
operating
temperatures
where
icing
at
the
fuel
control
could
occur
.

the

should

ype

Ambient

emperature
Limitations
The

shall

when

ambient

above

the

is

aircraft
is
referred
to
as
the
pilot's
side
and
the
right
side
is
referred
to
as
the
copilot's
side. These
definitions
have
no
bearing
on
actual
aircraft
qualifications
or
crew
positions. •
UC-12B,
Beech
King
Air
series
aircraft,
and/or
approved
simulator
pilot
hours
may
be
substituted
for
up
to,
but
not
to
exceed,
50
percent
of
flight
time
requirements
for
pilot
designations. 5.5.1
Aircraft
Commander
This
designation
is
granted
to
pilots
who
have
demonstrated
a
thorough
knowledge
of
the
T
-44C
and
its
subsystems
and
have
shown
mature
judgment
and
superior
airmanship. An
aircraft
commander
may
act
as
pilot
in
command
on
all
missions
for
which
he
meets
the
minimum
currency
requirements. For
designation
as
an
aircraft
commander
,
a
pilot
must
have
obtained
a
minimum
of
700
total
pilot
hours
of
which
200
hours
must
have
been
in
type
(fixed-wing). Additionally
,
the
pilot
must
have
obtained
a
minimum
of
100
hours
in
model
and
be
NA
T
OPS
qualified. The
hours
in
model
may
be
reduced
by
the
approving
authority
under
the
following
conditions:
1.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: FLIGHTCREW
chunks.text:
The
hours
in
model
may
be
reduced
to
50
hours
for
pilots
who
have
a
minimum
of
1,000
hours
in
fixed-wing
multi-engine
propeller
-driven
aircraft. 2. Upon
successfully
completing
a
CNA
TRA
T
-44C
flight
instructor
course,
the
hours
in
model
may
be
reduced
to
25
hours. 5.5.2
Second
Pilot
This
designation
is
granted
to
those
pilots
who
have
demonstrated
an
advanced
level
of
skill
in
the
employment
of
the
T
-44C. Except
for
those
missions
that
specifically
require
an
aircraft
commander
(CNAF
M–3710.7
refers),
a
second
pilot
may
act
as
pilot
in
command
on
all
missions
for
which
he
meets
the
minimum
currency
requirements. For
designation
as
second
pilot,
the
pilot
must
have
obtained
500
hours
total
pilot
time
of
which
200
must
have
been
in
type
(fixed-wing). Additionally
,
the
pilot
must
have
obtained
20
hours
in
model
and
be
NA
T
OPS
qualified. 5.5.3
Instructor
Pilot
This
designation
is
granted
to
those
pilots
who
are
assigned
to
conduct
pilot
training
flights
as
specified
by
CNAF
M–3710.7
(series). 5.5.4
Pilot
Under
Instruction
This
classification
applies
to
designated
aviators
under
going
transition
training
prior
to
completing
the
minimum
requirements
for
a
third
pilot. 5.5.5
Copilot
The
copilot
classification
applies
to
a
NA
T
OPS-rated
pilot,
student
naval
aviator
,
instructor
under
training,
pilot
under
instruction,
or
NFO.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: FLIGHTCREW
chunks.text:
For
designation
as
a
copilot,
a
military
member
must
have
obtained
a
minimum
of
12
total
fixed-wing
hours
and
a
minimum
of
12
hours
in
model. The
NFO
is
expected
to
have
the
same
knowledge
of
aircraft
systems
and
normal/emer
gency
procedures
that
is
required
of
a
T
-44
designated
aviator
copilot,
except
the
NFO
will
not
be
qualified
to
physically
control
the
aircraft. This
classification
may
also
apply
to
any
designated
military
member
authorized
by
the
commanding
of
ficer
. The
copilot
shall
assist
the
pilot
in
command
in
the
safe
and
successful
accomplishment
of
the
assigned
flight. 5.5-1

Student
Military
A
viator
This
designation

pilots,

service,

or

T
-44C
curriculum
in

Naval
Air
T
raining
Command.
5.5.7
Observer
This
classification
applies
to
any
student

aviator
who
occupies
a
cabin
seat
for

sole
purpose
of
visual
lookout
on

flights.
When
authorized
by

,
this
classification
also
applies
to
any
naval
aviator
or
naval
flight
of
ficer
utilized
as
an
observer
.
An
observer
serving
as
an
additional
lookout
should
accompany
each
flying
event
to
the
maximum
extent
feasible.
5.5.8
Passenger
The
classification
applies
to
all
persons
flying
in
the
aircraft
for
any
reason
other
than
to
control
the
aircraft
in
flight
or
to
operate
any
of
its
installed
equipment.

chunks.text:
5.6
FLIGHTCREW
QUALIFICA
TIONS
AND
CURRENCY
REQUIREMENTS
The
minimum
requirements
for
flightcrew
designation
in
the
T
-44C
shall
be
as
set
forth
by
CNAF
M–3710.7
(series)
and
amplified
by
local
directives. All
pilot
designation
shall
normally
remain
current
for
as
long
as
he
regularly
flies
the
aircraft
and
performs
the
mission
required
of
the
command
unless
revoked
by
the
commanding
of
ficer
. Pilots,
who
during
any
90-day
period
have
not
made
at
least
two
takeof
fs
and
landings
and
logged
5
hours
flight
time
in
model,
shall
be
considered
no
longer
qualified. Such
pilots
may
requalify
in
the
T
-44C
without
progressing
through
lower
designations. Requalification
shall
consist
of
at
least
one
refresher
flight
and
an
evaluation
flight
with
a
designated
NA
T
OPS
instructor
. 5.6.1
Functional
Checkflight
At
a
minimum,
the
flightcrew
shall
consist
of
a
designated
maintenance
checkpilot
and
a
copilot. During
checkflights
where
equipment
and
systems
must
be
checked
in
the
air
,
the
flightcrew
shall
consist
of
such
additional
crewmembers
as
the
commanding
of
ficer
determines
necessary
. 5.6.2
T
ransport
Mission
An
aircraft
commander
and
second
pilot/copilot
(except
NFOs)
shall
be
required
for
all
passenger
and
car
go
missions. Individual
NFOs
may
seek
waiver
of
this
requirement
through
CNA
TRA.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CHAPTER
chunks.text:
CHAPTER
6
Flight
Preparation
6.1
GENERAL
These
procedures
are
designed
to
aid
the
operating
crew
in
the
conduct
of
an
ef
ficient
and
orderly
flight. 6.1.1
Crew
Duty
T
ime
Crew
duty
time
begins
when
a
crew
reports
to
a
designated
place
of
duty
to
begin
preparation
for
a
mission. For
normal
missions,
this
will
be
1
hour
before
scheduled
departure,
1-1/2
hours
when
a
VIP
is
to
be
carried. However
,
in
the
event
a
pilot/crewmember
is
assigned
other
duties
commencing
before
his
reporting
for
preparation
for
a
flight,
his
crew
duty
time
will
be
computed
from
the
time
he
initially
reports
for
such
assigned
duties. Passenger
time
immediately
before
acting
as
a
crewmember
will
count
as
crew
duty
time. Crew
duty
time
ends
when
the
engines
are
cut
at
the
end
of
a
mission
or
series
of
missions. Maximum
crew
duty
time
is
14
hours
with
a
functioning
autopilot
and
12
hours
without
a
functioning
autopilot. 6.1.2
Crew
Rest
Crew
rest
is
in
accordance
with
CNAF
M–3710.7
(series). 6.1.3
En
Route
Crew
Rest
Crew
rest
shall
be
a
minimum
of
12
hours
and
shall
commence
when
the
aircraft
engines
are
shut
down. This
should
permit
the
crew
to
have
8
hours
of
uninterrupted
rest
plus
time
for
postflight,
transportation,
and
meals. The
aircraft
commander
may
declare
a
10-hour
crew
rest
period
to
expedite
the
aircraft
return
to
home
base.

commanders
will
delay
a
mission
to

crew
rest
whenever
this
action
is
in
the
interest
of
flight
safety
. 6.1-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: MISSION
chunks.text:
6.2
MISSION
PLANNING
Mission
planning
is
the
responsibility
of
the
pilot
in
command
or
flight
leader
and
will
cover
all
matters
pertinent
to
the
mission
to
be
flown.
Every
task
which
can
be
completed
prior
to
takeof
f
will
contribute
to
the
ease
and
success
with
which
the
actual
flight
is
conducted.
6.2.1
W
eather
A
complete
weather
briefing
and
an
understanding
of
the
weather
picture
is
a
prerequisite
of
successful
mission
planning.
En
route
weather
will
dictate
the
probable
altitude
assignments
and
whether
or
not
those
altitudes
assigned
will
be
acceptable.
Destination
weather
will
determine
expected
delays
in
letdown
and
landing
procedures
and
a
good
weather
alternate
or
alternates
are
a
must
to
save
the
day
in
unforeseen
circumstances.
All
of
this
weather
picture
af
fects
fuel
planning
and
flight
procedures.
6.2.2
Planning
Data
Data
and
information
required
for
determining
the
operational
parameters
and
the
mission
requirements
are
found
in
Part
XI
.
6.2.3
W
eight
and
Balance
Refer
to
Chapter
4
for
aircraft
operating
limitations.
For
loading
information,
refer
to
Part
VIII
.
6.2.4
Fuel
Planning
A
fuel
planning
log
shall
be
prepared
and
cross-checked
with
actual
fuel
consumption
on
every
cross-country
flight.
6.2-1
1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: BRIEFING
chunks.text:
6.3
BRIEFING
The
success
of
a
flight
is
a
direct
measure
of
the
preflight
preparation,
planning,
and
briefing.
The
briefing
syllabus
should
include,
but
is
not
limited
to,
the
following
guidelines.
6.3.1
Communications
1.
Radio
procedures
and
identification.
2.
Frequencies.
3.
CRM.
6.3.2
W
eather/NOT
AMS/BASH
1.
Local
observation.
2.
En
route/destination
forecast.
3.
Alternate
forecast.
6.3.3
Flight
Planning
1.
Climb-out/Departure.
2.
Mission/Fuel
planning.
3.
Recovery
.
4.
Electronic
publications.
6.3.4
Emergencies
1.
Aborts.
2.
Divert
fields.
3.
Radio
failure.
4.
Downed
pilot.
5.
System
failures.
6.
Spin
recovery/windshear/forced
landing/ditch.
7.
Emer
gency
egress.
The
pilot
in
command
will
inspect
all
flight
members
for
the
proper
flight
equipment.
6.3.5
Observer
Duties
1.
Look
out
for
traf
fic.
2.
Confirm
landing
gear
down
and
locked.
3.
Monitor
radios.
4.
Check
flaps
up
on
touch
and
go.
5.
Count
the
number
of
landings.
6.
Passenger
briefing.
6.3-1
1
SEPTEMBER
2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: DEBRIEFING
chunks.text:
6.4
DEBRIEFING
Each
flight
shall
be
followed
with
a
thorough
debriefing
by
the
flight
leader
or
pilot
in
command
as
soon
as
is
practical.
All
areas
of
flight
should
be
covered,
paying
particular
attention
to
those
areas
where
dif
ficulty
was
encountered.
The
debriefing
may
quite
possibly
be
the
most
beneficial
nonflying
portion
of
the
flight
because
it
is
here
that
errors
committed
are
reviewed,
the
proper
techniques
discussed,
and
these
associated
with
past
performance.
6.4-1
1

2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CHAPTER
chunks.text:
CHAPTER
7
Shore-Based
Procedures
7.1
LINE
OPERA
TIONS
7.1.1
Scheduling
The
commanding
of
ficer
or
his
designated
representative
is
responsible
for
the
promulgation
of
the
flight
schedule.
The
flight
schedule
is
an
order
of
the
commanding
of
ficer
.
It
shall
be
followed
rigidly
and
variations
require
approval
of
the
commanding
of
ficer
or
his
designated
representative.
Shipboard
scheduling
is
not
applicable
to
this
aircraft.
7.1.2
Aircraft
Acceptance
The
discrepancy
records
of
at
least
the
last
10
flights
will
be
made
available
to
the
pilot
for
his
examination.
The
pilot
in
command
will
ensure
that
a
preflight
inspection
has
been
conducted
and
that
all
servicing
entries
have
been
made.
When
satisfied
that
all
required
information
has
been
stated,
the
pilot
in
command
will
sign
for
the
aircraft.
7.1-1
1

CUI
NA

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: PREFLIGHT
chunks.text:
7.2
PREFLIGHT
INSPECTION
The
pilot
in
command
will
ensure
that
a
proper
preflight
inspection
is
accomplished. A
satisfactory
preflight
will
consist
of
both
the
interior
and
exterior
inspection. As
the
aircraft
is
approached,
observe
wheel
chocks
in
place,
tiedowns
removed,
and
that
no
obvious
aircraft
discrepancies
exist. The
path
to
be
followed
during
the
exterior
inspection
is
presented
in
Figure
7.2-1
;
the
drains
and
vents
are
shown
in
Figure
7.2-2
,
antenna
locations
are
shown
in
Figure
7.2-3
. The
following
items
shall
be
checked
prior
to
commencing
the
preflight
inspection:
1. Battery
—
OFF
,
check
voltage. Note
Minimum
battery
voltage
is
20
volts
for
GPU
start,
22
volts
for
battery
start. 2. Landing
gear
handle
—
DOWN
(check
safety
switch
in
locked
position). 7.2.1
Interior
Inspection
7.2.1.1
Passenger/Cargo
Compartment
1. Aft
compartment
—
Check
All
Loose
Items
Stowed. 2. Emer
gency
equipment
—
Check
as
Required. a. Aft
fire
extinguisher
. b. Raft. c. First
aid
kit. 3. Emer
gency
exit
—
Latched,
Flush
W
ith
Interior
. 4. Passenger
Seats
—
Check
All
Seats
Upright,
and
ensure
each
seat
contains
a

7.2.3
Ground
Power
Unit
(GPU)
Procedures
The
ground
power
source
(three
prong)
used
for
aircraft
ground
checks,
battery
char
ging,
or
engine
starting
must
not
exceed
28
Vdc
and
have
the
capability
of
delivering
a
continuous
output
of
300
amp
maximum
with
a
peak
output
of
up
to
1,000
amp
for
0.1
second
if
required. Refer
to
Paragraph
3.9
,
EXTERNAL
POWER
APPLICA
TION,
for
additional
precautions. Note
•
When
a
GPU
start
is
planned,
perform
fuel
panel
check
prior
to
connecting
GPU. If
battery
voltage
is
insuf
ficient
to
perform
the
fuel
panel
check,
char
ge
the
battery
using
external
power
,
then
disconnect
the
GPU
and
perform
the
fuel
panel
check. •
The
left
engine
will
be
started
first
using
the
GPU
because
the
receptacle
is
located
on
the
underside
of
the
right
wing
outboard
of
the
engine
nacelle. •
Minimum
battery
voltage
for

connection
is
18
volts. If
less
than
18
volts,
replace
the
battery
. Minimum
battery
voltage
for
a
GPU

20
volts. If
voltage

less

20

battery
or

ge

ger

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CHECKLIST
chunks.text:
7.3
CHECKLIST
METHODOLOGY
The
checklists
that
follows
in
this
chapter
from
Before
Start
Checklist
through
Secure
Checklist
are
challenge
and
reply
checklists.
Normally
,
the
pilot
monitoring
will
read
the
checklist
and
thus
provide
the
challenge.
After
each
challenge
is
initiated,
the
appropriate
action
should
be
completed
and
a
response
provided
by
the
crewmember(s)
indicated.
Responder
abbreviations
are:
PF
—
pilot
flying,
PM
—
pilot
monitoring,
OBS
—
observer
,
LS
—
left
seat,
or
RS
—
right
seat.
Left
seat
(LS)
and
right
seat
(RS)
responsibilities
are
specific
to
the
respective
seat
position.
Observers
are
to
ensure
appropriate
action
is
completed
by
all
passengers
prior
to
initiating
a
checklist
response.
The
pilot
monitoring
will
read
the
challenge
and
response
to
emer
gency
checklists
in
this
manual.
The
pilot
flying
will
direct
action
to
be
taken
with
the
appropriate
crewmember
performing
the
action
and
repeating
the
response.
Checklists
that
do
not
have
a
responder(s)
indicated
do
not
fall
within
the
challenge
and
reply
category
,
but
may
be
utilized
in
a
similar
manner
.
7.3-1
1

Start
When

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: AXIING
chunks.text:
7.7
T
AXIING
Normal
taxi
operations
are
started
from
a
slow
straight-ahead
roll
using
engine
power
as
required
to
initiate
movement.
T
axi
speed
can
be
ef
fectively
controlled
by
the
use
of
power
application
in
the
beta
range
which
varies
propeller
pitch.
Normally
,
augmentation
of
light
braking
is
also
necessary
to
ef
fectively
control
taxi
speed.
The
best
power
lever
position
for
taxi
is
generally
at
the
boundary
of
beta
and
reverse
where
an
increase
in
engine
rpm
and
engine
noise
is
first
detected.
Symmetric
reversing
should
be
checked
at
some
point
during
the
initial
taxi.
Normal
turns
may
be
made
with
the
steerable
nosewheel
or
by
the
use
of
asymmetric
power
or
a
combination
of
both.
T
urns
may
be
tightened
by
using
full
rudder
and
inside
brake
as
necessary
.
T
urns
should
not
be
started
with
brakes
alone,
nor
should
the
aircraft
be
pivoted
sharply
on
one
main
landing
gear
.
Observe
appropriate
turn
instruments
for
proper
response.
Minimize
use
of
reverse
at
slow
taxi
speeds.
Use
of
reverse
range
in
surface
areas
containing
loose
sand
or
small
stones
may
cause
propeller
blade
erosion.
Note
Lar
ger
airports
may
require
transponders
for
taxi.
Consideration
should
be
given
to
check
appropriate
publications
for
transponder
requirements
prior
to
taxiing.
7.7-1
1

chunks.heading: SPOT/QUARTERDECK
chunks.text:
7.8
HOT
SPOT/QUARTERDECK
OPERA
TIONS
When
embarking
or
debarking
personnel
at
a
hot
spot
or
quarterdeck,
the
left
engine
must
be
feathered
and
the
aircraft
must
be
depressurized.
Care
must
be
taken
not
to
leave
an
engine
feathered
for
a
prolonged
period.
When
the
propeller
is
feathered
on
the
ground,
engine
exhaust
is
not
adequately
forced
aft
by
propeller
wash
which
eventually
causes
the
side(s)
of
the
aircraft
fuselage
to
overheat.
Regardless
of
whether
the
left
prop
is
feathered
or
secured,
it
may
be
impossible
to
close
the
main
cabin
door
without
shutting
down
both
engines.
A
few
T
-44s
do
not
have
a
cabin
door
seal
solenoid
installed
which
dumps
air
pressure
to
the
seal
when
on
the
ground.
For
these
aircraft,
both
engines
must
be
secured
in
order
to
close
the
main
cabin
door
and
a
full
Secure
Checklist
is
required.
7.8-1
1
SEPTEMBER
2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1

chunks.heading: FUEL
chunks.text:
7.13
FUEL
MANAGEMENT
During
normal
operation,
the
fuel
level
in
each
nacelle
tank
is
automatically
maintained
by
gravity
and
an
electrically
operated
transfer
pump.
No
provision
is
made
for
fuel
transfer
from
one
wing
to
the
other
.
However
,
should
a
fuel
imbalance
occur
,
fuel
load
may
be
balanced
by
feeding
both
engines
from
the
same
wing.
When
crossfeeding,
if
feeding
tank
is
run
dry
,
both
engines
will
flame
out.
Fuel
quantity
indications
may
be
erroneous
and
should
not
be
utilized
as
the
sole
indication
of
fuel
quantity
.

wo-Engine
Crossfeed

—

ransfer

—

O.

respective

7.14

Descent
from
cruising
altitude
may
be
made
at
any
airspeed
and
power
combination
within
the
aircraft
operating
envelope.
Maximum
rate
of
descent
may
be
obtained
in
either
the
clean
or
dirty
configuration.
However
,
the
descent
gradient
of
a
dirty
descent
is
considerably
greater
than
that
of
a
clean
descent
at
a
given
power
setting.
Note
Approximately
75
percent
N
1
(single
engine
85
percent
N
1
)
is
required
to
maintain
the
pressurization
schedule
during
descent.
The
Descent
Checklist
must
be
completed
anytime
the
Cruise
Checklist
has
been
completed.
Commence
the
Descent
Checklist
upon
descending
through
FL
180
or
upon
initial
descent
from
cruise
altitude
if
lower
.

chunks.heading: LANDING
chunks.text:
7.17
LANDING
The
Landing
Checklist
shall
be
completed
prior
to
every
landing. 1. Flaps
—
As
required
(PF
,
PM). 2. Landing
gear
—
Down/Locked
(PF
,
PM). 3. Lights
—
Set
(LS). Overriding
the
landing
gear
warning
horn
in
the
traf
fic
pattern
or
during
final
segment
of
an
instrument
approach
could
result
in
a
landing
gear
-up
landing. •
Engagement
of
the
yaw
damp
during
takeof
f
or
landing
may
result
in
severe
directional
control
problems. •
Before
landing,
ensure
the
prop
levers
are
placed
to
the
full
forward
(HIGH
RPM)
position
to
avoid
inadvertent
damage
to
the
prop
linkage. Note
Under
certain
meteorological
conditions,
landing
lights
may
induce
vertigo. 7.17.1
Normal
Landing
Normal
landings
are
descending,
decelerating
approaches
to
obtain
the
optimum
angle
of
attack
prior
to
entering
the
flare
(see
Figures
7.17-1
and
7.17-2
). Adjust
power
to
idle
at
touchdown. Upon
ground
contact,
lower
the
nosewheel
to
the
runway
. Maintain
directional
control
with
rudder
and
apply
braking
and
prop
reversing
(props
full
forward)
as
required
by
runway
length
remaining. Refer
to
Part
XI
for
landing
distances. Part
XI
landing
distance
data
is
based
on
no
reverse,
maximum
braking,
and
condition
levers
at
LOW
IDLE. Landing
distance
will
increase
by
approximately
900
feet
with
no
reverse,
maximum
braking,
and
condition
levers
at
HIGH
IDLE.

chunks.text:
Note
Landing
distance
will
decrease
by
approximately
700
feet
with
reverse
employed,
maximum
braking,
and
condition
levers
set
at
HIGH
IDLE. 7.17.2
T
ouch-and-Go
Landing
After
touchdown,
retract
the
flaps,
retrim
elevators,
and
takeof
f
in
a
normal
manner
. W
ith
safe
altitude,
turn
to
the
downwind
leg
of
the
traf
fic
pattern. 7.17-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: LANDING
chunks.text:
•
Mechanical
stops
incorporated
in
the
throttle
quadrant
prevent
rapid
forward
movement
of
the
power
levers
from
the
beta
range
to
the
flight
range. Downward
pressure
must
be
applied
to
the
power
levers
to
permit
movement
into
the
flight
range
and
allow
power
to
be
added. •
Diligence
shall
be
used
to
ensure
the
landing
gear
handle
is
not
mistakenly
moved
by
the
pilot
in
the
right
seat
while
reconfiguring
the
aircraft
during
touch-and-go
operations. •
Failure
to
properly
reset
flaps
and/or
trim
will
result
in
abnormal
control
surface
pressures
when
seeking
to
obtain
a
normal
flying
attitude
upon
rotation. The
aircraft
can
be
safely
flown
out
of
this
hazardous
situation,
but
extreme
care
must
be
taken
to
apply
adequate
control
forces
to
achieve
the
proper
flying
attitude. The
flaps
should
not
be
raised
until
reaching
300
feet
AGL,
or
two
positive
rates
of
climb
indications
established. •
During
runway
operation,
insuf
ficient
weight
on
the
right
main
landing
gear
squat
switch
may
cause
the
landing
gear
handle
downlock
J-hook
to
unlatch
and
allow
the
landing
gear
handle
to
be
inadvertently
raised
while
the
aircraft
is
on
the
ground. 7.17.3
Crosswind
Landing
No
unusual
technique
is
required
in
a
crosswind
landing. Use
the
wing
low
method. Allow
for
drift
while
turning
on
final
approach
so
that
the
approach
leg
will
not
be
overshot.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: LANDING
chunks.text:
Establish
drift
correction
as
soon
as
drift
is
detected. Use
ailerons
and
top
rudder
as
necessary
to
counteract
drift
during
flare
and
touchdown. Crosswind
correction
(ailerons)
should
be
maintained
during
the
ground
roll. 7.17.4
Night
Landing
Night
landing
procedures
are
the
same
as
used
during
a
day
landing. Instrument
and
interior
light
intensity
should
be
kept
as
low
as
possible
to
aid
night
vision. A
void
using
landing
and/or
strobe
lights
in
thick
haze,
smoke,
or
fog
as
reflected
light
from
the
particles
in
the
air
may
reduce
visibility
. 1

7.18
W
A
VEOFF/MISSED
APPROACH
Pilots
should
initiate
their
own
waveof
f
anytime
a
safe
landing
is
not
possible.
The
decision
should
be
made
as
soon
as
possible
during
the
approach
and
prior
to
flare
to
provide
a
safe
mar
gin
of
airspeed
and
altitude.
Circumstances
requiring
a
waveof
f
may
not
permit
time
for
referral
to
the
checklist
and
shall
be
committed
to
memory
.
Complete
the
following
procedures
as
applicable:
*1.
Power
—
As
required,
Establish
Positive
Rate
of
Climb
(V
X
minimum).
Rapid
advancement
of
the
power
levers
may
result
in
prop
sur
ging
and
asymmetric
power
application
that
could
result
in
directional
control
problems.
*2.
Flaps
—
APPROACH
(unless
already
UP).
*3.
Landing
Gear
—
UP
.
Raising
the
landing
gear
prior
to
establishing
a
positive
rate
of
climb
could
result
in
ground
impact
with
the
gear
raised.
*4.
Flaps
—
UP
.
*5.
Props
—
1,900
rpm.
Note
Reducing
prop
rpm
to
1,900
reduces
drag,
enhancing
climb
performance.

chunks.heading: POSTFLIGHT
chunks.text:
7.21
POSTFLIGHT
A
satisfactory
postflight
will
consist
of
a
general
overall
check
of
the
aircraft
interior
and
exterior
for
discrepancies,
missing
panels,
in-flight
damage,
and
fuel,
oil,
or
hydraulic
leaks.
Check
the
accelerometer
for
overstress
of
the
airframe
and
ensure
the
aircraft
is
secured
as
required.
Excessive
tire
sidewall
damage,
brake
damage,
popped
rivets
around
wheelwell,
or
buckled
outer
aircraft
skin
are
indicative
of
a
hard
landing.
A
hard
landing
inspection
shall
be
conducted.
7.21-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CHAPTER
chunks.text:
CHAPTER
9
Special
Procedures
9.1
FORMA
TION
AND
T
ACTICS
The
following
discussion
of
formation
fundamentals
will
provide
a
foundation
of
formation
flying
that
will
meet
most
T
-44C
mission
requirements. The
signal
for
a
change
in
formation
may
be
made
by
radio. Each
aviator
in
the
flight
shall
be
thoroughly
briefed. The
elements
of
a
formation
are
a
section
of
two
aircraft
and
a
division
consisting
of
three
or
four
aircraft
(two
sections). T
wo
or
more
divisions
form
a
flight. 9.1.1
Parade
Formation
Parade
formation
requires
aircraft
to
fly
a
fixed
bearing
in
close
proximity
. The
recommended
parade
position
is
a
stable
position
on
the
45°
bearing
with
approximately
20
feet
of
step
down,
nose-to-tail
separation
approximately
15
feet
and
with
approximately
10
feet
of
lateral
separation
between
wing's
nose
and
lead's
wingtip. 9.1.2
Formation
T
akeoff/Landing
After
the
lead
begins
the
takeof
f
roll,
the
wingman
delays
takeof
f
roll
a
suf
ficient
amount
of
time
to
allow
adequate
separation
between
aircraft. Except
in
emer
gency
situations,
section
landings
shall
not
be
conducted. 9.1.3
Running
Rendezvous
Lead
will
depart
maintaining
a
prebriefed
airspeed
while
the
wingman
uses
power
,
airspeed,
and
radius
of
turn
as
applicable
to
join
up
as
briefed. 9.1.4
Parade
T
urns
In
parade
turns,
the
wingman
matches
lead's
rate
of
roll.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CHAPTER
chunks.text:
For
turns
into
the
wingman,
rotation
is
about
the
lead's
longitudinal
axis. For
turns
away
from
the
wingman,
the
wingman
normally
rotates
about
his
own
longitudinal
axis. 9.1.5
Crossunders
Crossunders
are
performed
to
move
the
wingman
from
the
parade
position
on
one
side
of
lead
to
the
parade
position
of
the
opposite
side. After
appropriate
signaling,
the
wingman
increases
stepdown. While
maintaining
adequate
nose
to
tail
separation,
cross
under
lead
to
a
point
directly
beneath
the
new
parade
position
and
decrease
stepdown
to
rejoin
the
parade
position. 9.1.6
Lead
Changes
The
lead
change
is
normally
accomplished
with
the
wingman
starting
in
the
starboard
parade
position. Once
the
appropriate
lead
change
signal
has
been
given
and
acknowledged,
the
original
lead
becomes
wingman
and
has
the
responsibility
of
maintaining
adequate
separation. The
responsibility
of
the
new
lead
is
to
maintain
steady
reference
attitude,
keep
flight
clear
of
conflicting
traf
fic
and/or
clouds,
and
assume
navigation
responsibilities. 9.1.7
Night
Formation
Formation
flights
shall
not
be
conducted
at
night. 9.1-1
1
SEPTEMBER

chunks.heading: CHAPTER
chunks.text:
CHAPTER
10
Functional
Checkflight
Procedures
10.1
GENERAL
10.1.1
Checkpilots
Pilots
shall
be
fully
qualified
pilots
in
accordance
with
this
manual
and
the
current
edition
of
CNAF
M–3710.7
(series). 10.1.2
Crewmembers
Crewmembers
of
checkflights
shall
be
qualified
to
conduct
such
tests
as
may
be
required. 10.1.3
Safety
Safety
shall
be
the
governing
factor
on
all
post
maintenance
checkflights. The
following
general
rules
shall
apply:
1. The
flight
shall
be
covered
by
the
requirements
of
this
manual
and
CNAF
M–3710.7
(series). 2. Radio
communications
shall
be
maintained
with
the
control
tower
or
another
appropriate
agency
at
all
times. 3. Checkflights
should
be
flown
in
a
designated
area
and
those
portions
of
the
checkflight
that
are
considered
critical
shall
be
conducted
in
the
vicinity
of
a
suitable
landing
area. 10.1.4
Forms
Functional
checkflight
checklists
are
published
separately
. The
results
of
the
checks
shall
be
entered
on
the
checklists
with
appropriate
remarks,
if
necessary
,
and
returned
to
Quality
Assurance
at
the
completion
of
the
checkflight. 10.1.5
Checkflights
Checkflights
are
required
to
determine
whether
the
air
frame,
powerplant,
accessories,
and
items
of
equipment
are
functioning
in
accordance
with
predetermined
requirements
while
subjected
to
the
intended
operating
environment.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000

chunks.text:

shall
be
flown
as

OPNA
VINST

(series)
or
such
other
times
as

be
directed
by
the
commanding
of
ficer
or
other
competent
authority

REQUIRING
CHECKFLIGHT

fuel

section

After

or

of
a
propeller
or

governor
,

steps:

to
4,

1,

akeof

1,

At

10,
12,
and
Landing
1.

appropriate
box
on

1
to

to

C.
When

or

flight
surfaces
or

have

or

and

or

could

an

minimum

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: PROCEDURES
chunks.text:
10.3
PROCEDURES
10.3.1
Briefing
Prior
to
the
checkflight,
Quality
Assurance
shall
brief
pilots
on
the
maintenance
performed
on
the
aircraft,
pertinent
aircraft
history
,
and
the
checkflight
requirements
for
the
particular
flight.
The
briefing
should
include
the
expected
results,
troubleshooting
recommendations,
and,
when
appropriate,
corrective
or
emer
gency
action
to
be
taken
if
required.
10.3.2
Functional
Checkflight
NA
T
OPS
procedures
shall
apply
during
the
entire
checkflight
unless
specific
deviation
is
required
by
the
functional
check
to
record
data
or
ensure
proper
operation
within
the
approved
aircraft
envelope.
The
following
items
provide
a
detailed
description
of
the
functional
checks,
sequenced
in
the
order
in
which
they
should
be
performed.
In
order
to
complete
the
checks
in
the
most
ef
ficient
and
logical
order
,
a
flight
profile
has
been
established
for
each
checkflight
condition
and
identified
by
the
letter
corresponding
to
the
purpose
for
which
the
checkflight
is
being
flown.
The
applicable
letter
identifying
the
profile
prefixes
each
check,
both
in
the
following
text
and
in
the
functional
flight
checklist.
Checkflight
personnel
shall
familiarize
themselves
with
these
requirements
prior
to
the
flight.
10.3-1
1

10.4
PREST
ART
B

to

Inspection. a. Normal
exterior
inspection
shall
be

normal
preflight. b. Considering
the
nature

the

appropriate
access
panel/cowling
doors
should
be

and
the
areas
that
were
worked
on
should
be
scrutinized
closely
for
proper
assembly
,
leaks,
correct
rigging,
tools,
rags,
and
other
foreign
objects. c. If
a
downing
discrepancy
is
discovered
during
preflight,
the
complete
exterior
inspection
should
be
completed
and
ALL
discrepancies
recorded
on
the
checklist
and
Aircraft
Discrepancy
Form. A
B
C
3. Interior
Inspection. a. Normal
interior
inspection
shall
be
conducted
with
more
attention
to

than
the
normal
preflight. b. Check
cabin
entrance
door
. Door
must
latch
and

easily
. When
closed,
red
lines,
indicating
closed,
should
line
up
with
pointers
and

lock
arm
should

in
position
around
the
plunger
. c. Check
operation
of
door
lowering
cylinder
—
door
must
lower
slowly
and
smoothly
. A
B
C
4. Flight
Station
Inspection. a. Check
operation
of
switches,
rheostats,
cockpit
and
instrument
lights. Check
for
missing
hardware,
aircraft
and

limit
decals,
and
compass
cards. b. Check
rudder
pedals,
seat,
and
restraint
harness
adjustments. c. Check
pilot
and
copilot
clocks
for
normal
operation. d.

CHAPTER

Flight
Characteristics
1
1.1
GENERAL
CHARACTERISTICS
The
characteristics
described
in
this
chapter
are
based
on
actual
flight
test
data.
The
general
flight
characteristics
of
the
aircraft
are
satisfactory
for
normal
maneuvering
in
all
flight
configurations
throughout
the
design
speed
and
altitude
range.
When
correctly
trimmed,
the
aircraft
tends
to
remain
in
straight-and-level
flight.
Extending
and
retracting
the
landing
gear
and
wing
flaps
or
changing
power
settings
cause
changes
in
control
pressure
which
can
be
removed
by
retrimming.
1

CONTROLS
Aileron,
elevator
,

and
trim
tab
controls
function
ef
fectively
throughout
slow
,
cruising
and
high
speed
flight.
Controls
become
more
sensitive
as
speed
increases;
likewise,
control
forces
become
progressively
greater
.
Elevator
control
feel
at
low
airspeed
is
enhanced
by
the
use
of
antiservo
trim
tabs,
which
also
serve
to
increase
ef
fectiveness
at
extreme
positions.

CLIMB
CHARACTERISTICS
After
normal
takeof
f
and
completion
of
the
Climb
Checklist,
accelerate
to
the
appropriate
en
route
climb
speed.
Refer
to
Part
XI
to
determine
aircraft
rate
of
climb
and
gradient.
Control
forces
resulting
from
climb
attitudes
and
power
settings
may
be
completely
trimmed
out.
The
approach
to
a
stall
during
climb
is
characterized
by
excellent
longitudinal,
lateral,
and
directional
control
ef
fectiveness.
Ample
stall
warning
in

form
of
aerodynamic
buf
feting
is
given
when
a
stall
approaches.

FLIGHT

speed
or
the

which
abrupt

travel

exceeding

load
factor
of

aircraft
is
153
KIAS.
Figure
4.4-2
is
a
plot
of
acceleration
versus
airspeed
and
shows
the
speeds
at
which
maneuvers
are
restricted
relative

load
limit
factors.

an
aircraft
maximum

pounds.

flight

pressures.
