chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VIGA
chunks.text:
20.3
NA
VIGA
TION
The
navigation
systems
provide
the
pilot
and
copilot
with
various
sources
of
aircraft
relative
position. Information
can
be
entered
and
displayed
for
precise
navigation
to
or
from
a
destination. The
main
component
of
the
navigation
system
is
an
EFIS
that
is
used
to
display
an
integrated
presentation
utilizing
inputs
from
various
sources. The
navigation
systems
also
include
the
FMS,
RSS,
T
ACAN,
ADS,
AHRS,
RA,
and
GPS. The
RSS
controls
the
Mode-S
T
ransponder
,
ADF
,
VOR/GS/LOC,
and
DME
for
navigation. 20.3.1
Electronic
Flight
Instrument
System
(EFIS)
20.3.1.1
Description
The
EFIS
is
made
up
of
a
pilot
side
and
copilot
side
system. Each
side
is
functionally
and
physically
isolated
from
each
other
and
is
capable
of
functioning
as
a
complete,
independent
system. The
display
portion
of
the
system
is
made
up
of
two
Primary
Flight
Displays
(PFDs)
that
show
the
basic
flight
instruments
in
the
upper
half
of
the
display
and
navigation
information
in
the
bottom
half. Control
of
the
system
is
handled
primarily
by
the
two
Display
Control
Panels
(DCPs)
located
just
to
the
inside
of
the
PFDs. Buttons
on
the
PFDs
also
enable
the
flight
crew
to
select/change
navigation
information
on
the
display
. System
start-up
is
automatic
once
power
is
applied. 20.3.1.1.1
Display
Control
Panel
(DCP)
The
DCP
provides
the
controls
of
the
PFD.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VIGA
chunks.text:
The
DCP
selects
barometric
pressure
correction,
V
-speed
reference
settings,
navigation
source
selection,
bearing
source
selection,
weather
radar
control,
and
display
range
selection
(see
Figure
20.3-1
). The
DCPs
are
protected
by
2-ampere
circuit
breakers,
labeled
DCP
1
and
DCP
2
located
on
the
copilot
outboard
subpanel. They
receive
power
from
the
No. 1
and
No. 2
A
vionics
Bus
respectively
. 1. T
ilt
Range
Knobs
—
The
outer
knob
controls
the
vertical
tilt
of
the
radar
and
can
be
used
to
adjust
the
tilt
angle
between
(-15
to
+15
degrees). The
selected
angle
is
displayed
on
the
PFDs
along
with
the
letter
“T”. The
inner
knob
can
be
used
to
adjust
the
range
on
the
PFD
between
2.5
and
600
nm. Note
If
the
RDR
prompt
next
to
Line
Select
Key
(LSK)
R2
is
selected,
the
PFD
will
be
restricted
to
a
maximum
range
of
300
NM. 2. Menu
Advance
Knobs
—
T
urn
the
MENU
ADV
knob
to
highlight
the
preset
primary
NA
V
source. a. MENU
SET
Button
—
Push
the
MENU
SET
button
to
select
the
preset
primary
navigation
source. 3. Barometric
Correction
Knob
—
The
barometric
correction
knob
can
be
used
to
adjust
the
barometric
pressure
setting
which
is
displayed
on
the
PFD. 4. Push
Standard
Barometric
Pressure
Button
—
Push
the
PUSH
STD
button
to
select
standard
barometric
pressure. 5.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: VIGA
chunks.text:
References
Button
—
Push
the
REFS
button
to
show
the
airspeed
V
-speed
references,
minimum
radio
altitude,
and
minimum
baro
altitude
setting
on
the
PFD. a. The
airspeed
VT
,
V2,
VR,
and
V1
references
show
on
the
left
side
of
the
PFD. b. RA
MIN
(minimum
radio
altitude),
BARO
MIN
(minimum
baro
altitude),
and
VREF
references
show
on
the
right
side
of
the
PFD. c. Push
the
associated
line
select
key
on
the
PFD
and
the
selected
reference
is
boxed. d. T
urn
the
MENU
SET
knob
on
the
DCP
to
set
the
reference
value. e. Push
the
REFS
button
again
to
remove
the
REFS
menu. 6. NA
V/BRG
Menu
Button
—
Push
the
NA
V/BRG
button
to
show
the
NA
V
SOURCE
and
BRG
SOURCE
menus
on
the
PFD. The
NA
V
SOURCE
menu
shows
the
possible
active
navigation
sources
on
the
left
side
of
the
PFD. The
possible
sources
are
FMS
1,
VOR
1/LOC
1,
and
VOR
2/LOC
2
adjacent
to
the
top
left-side
line
select
key
. Push
the
line
select
key
to
move
the
selection
box
to
the
desired
navigation
source. 20.3-1

chunks.text:
Figure
20.3-1.
Display
Control
Panel
(DCP)
The
BRG
SOURCE
menu
shows
the
single-bar
bearing
pointer
source
and
the
double-bar
bearing
pointer
source
on
the
right
side
of
the
PFD.
Repeatedly
push
the
associated
line
select
key
to
cycle
through
the
possible
bearing
pointer
selections.
Possible
bearing
sources
are
OFF
,
FMS,
VOR,
and
ADF
.
Push
the
NA
V/BRG
button
again
to
remove
the
NA
V/BRG
menu.
7.
Radar
Button
—
Push
the
RADAR
button
to
show
the
weather
radar
menus
on
the
PFD.
A
box
shows
around
the
current
RADAR
operational
mode.
T
urn
the
MENU
ADV
knob
to
move
the
box
to
the
item
the
pilot
wants
to
make
active.
Push
the
PUSH
MENU
SET
button
on
the
DCP
to
make
the
selected
item
become
active.
The
box
can
also
be
moved
using
the
line
select
keys
on
the
PFD.
The
left
side
RADAR
menu
shows
the
STBY
,
TEST
,
MAP
,
WX,
WX+T
,
and
TURB
radar
modes
and
ST
AB
(stabilization).
a.
Move
the
box
to
the
STBY
mode
to
select
the
weather
radar
standby
mode.
The
radar
is
powered
but
does
not
transmit
or
scan
is
STBY
mode.
b.
Move
the
box
to
the
TEST
mode
to
show
the
test
display
pattern.

chunks.text:
c. The
MAP
mode
selects
the
ground
mapping
mode. d. The
WX
mode
selects
the
weather
radar
mode. e. The
WX+T
mode
shows
the
weather
radar
with
turbulence
detection. f. Select
the
TURB
mode
to
show
only
the
turbulent
weather
. The
TURB
only
mode
has
a
10
second
time-out
and
returns
to
the
WX+TURB
mode. g. The
stabilization
function
can
be
toggled
ON
or
OFF
by
pushing
the
ST
AB
line
select
key
. h. The
right
side
RADAR
menu
shows
GAIN,
SEC
(sector)
SCAN,
and
T
ARGET
(tar
get
alert). i. The
current
GAIN
setting
shows
in
a
box
below
the
GAIN
legend. T
urn
the
MENU
ADV
knob
to
set
the
gain
at
NORM,
±1,
±2,
or
±3. j. The
sector
scan
function
can
be
toggled
ON
or
OFF
by
pushing
the
SEC
SCAN
line
select
key
. k. The
tar
get
alert
function
can
be
toggled
ARM
or

by
pushing
the
T
ARGET
line
select
key
. 8. Ground
Clutter
Suppression
(GCS)
Button
—
Push
the
GCS
button
to
select
or
deselect
ground
clutter
suppression. When
selected,
the
Receiver/T
ransmitter/Antenna
suppresses
the
intensity
of
ground
returns
(clutter)
in
WX
and
WX+T
modes
to
help
identify
precipitation
tar
gets. GCS
annunciates
with
the
radar
mode
on
the
PFD. 9. Push
Auto
T
ilt
Button
—
Press
the
PUSH
AUT
O
button
to
select
or
deselect
automatic
tilt
control. The
letter
A
annunciates
with
the
tilt
angle
to
indicate
auto
tilt
is
selected.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
The
autotilt
function
compensates
for
airplane
attitude
and
range
changes,
and
adjusts
the
tilt
angle
to
maintain
the
selected
reference-to-ground. 20.3.1.1.2
Primary
Flight
Display
(PFD)
The
PFD
provides
the
functions
of
the
attitude
indicator
,
heading
indicator
,
airspeed
indicator
,
altimeter
,
and
vertical
speed
indicator
onto
a
single
electronic
display
. It
also
has
the
capability
to
display
overlays
from
T
AS
and
the
weather
radar
. The
display
shown
in
Figure
20.3-2
presents
the
initialization
display
immediately
after
power
is
applied. Power
on
default
brightness
is
75
percent
brightness. Each
section
of
the
display
is
divided
to
give
the
pilot
information
on
magnetic
heading,
altitude,
speed,
and
attitude. Other
displays
can
also
be
selected
via
the
use
of
the
left
and
right
line
select
keys. T
o
preclude
the
PFDs
from
dropping
of
f
line
during
low
voltage
situations
(airstart
of
the
engines),
each
PFD
is
connected
to
a
DC
to
DC
convertor
that
maintains
a
constant
28
Vdc. These
convertors
receive
power
from
the
No. 1
and
No. 2
A
vionics
Buses
respectively
and
are
protected
by
15-ampere
circuit
breakers. The
breakers
are
labeled
DC
CONVTR1
and
DC
CONVTR2
and
are
located
on
the
center
pedestal
circuit
breaker
panel. The
PFDs
are
also
protected
by
individual
circuit
breakers
labeled
PFD
1
and
PFD
2
which
are
located
on
the
copilot
outboard
subpanel.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
T
o
completely
remove
power
from
the
PFD,
both
the
DC
CONVTR
and
PFD
breakers
must
be
pulled. 1. Line
Select
Keys
(LSK)—
The
PFD
has
four
LSKs
on
each
side
of
the
display
. The
NA
T
OPS
numbering
system
for
these
keys
will
be
from
top
to
bottom
and
left/right. Hence,
the
top
left
line
select
key
will
be
LSKs
L1
and
the
bottom
right
line
select
key
will
be
LSKs
R4. The
LSKs
that
are
active
will
have
a
side-facing
caret
(<
or
>)
that
point
from
the
associated
legend
to
the
LSKs. Some
menus
may
place
legends
within
the
proximity
of
various
LSKs
that
do
not
have
a
function. The
absence
of
a
side-facing
caret
indicates
the
LSKs
have
no
function. 2. Horizontal
Situation
Indicator
(HSI)
Display
—
The
HSI
display
shows
navigation
data. This
portion
of
the
PFD
shows
a
compass
arc,
airplane
heading,
active
course,
selected
bearing(s),
selected
heading,
temperature,
drift
angle,
and
system
message
annunciation. 3. Airspeed
Indicator
(ASI)
—
The
ASI
display
shows
airspeed
data. This
portion
of
the
PFD
shows
current
Indicated
Airspeed
(IAS),
current
Mach,
several
IAS
markers
(bugs),
an
IAS
trend
vector
,
and
low/high
speed
cues. 20.3-3
1

chunks.text:
4. Attitude
Direction
Indicator
(ADI)
Display
—
The
ADI
display
shows
primary
flight
data. This
portion
of
the
PFD
shows
airplane
attitude,
flight
director
steering
commands,
flight
control
status/mode
annunciations,
vertical/lateral
deviation,
and
marker
beacon
annunciations. 5. Altitude
Indicator
(ALI)
—
The
ALI
display
shows
altitude
and
vertical
speed
data. This
portion
of
the
PFD
shows
barometric
corrected
altitude,
pre-selected
altitude,
radio
altitude,
minimum
descent/reporting
altitude,
decision
height,
and
current
vertical
speed. 6. BR
T/DIM
TRIM
Switch
—
The
BR
T/DIM
TRIM
switch
works
in
conjunction
with
the
pilot's
display
rheostat
on
the
overhead
control
panel
to
adjust
the
PFD
display
brightness. Push
the
top
or
bottom
of
the
rocker
switch
to
adjust
display
brightness. a. Attitude
Direction
Indicator
(ADI)
Figure
20.3-3
and
the
follow-on
text
provide
detailed
information
concerning
the
ADI
portion
of
the
PFD. It
includes
airplane
attitude,
flight
director
steering
commands,
flight
control
status/mode
annunciations,
vertical/lateral
deviation,
and
marker
beacon
annunciations. 1. Aircraft
Symbol
—
A
stationary
chevron
marker
used
as
a
reference
for
viewing
Flight
Director
(FD)
steering
commands
and
pitch/roll
attitudes. 2.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: (ADI)
chunks.text:
FD
Steering
Command
Bars
—
Integrated
V
-bars
show
(on
the
coupled
side)
when
the
autopilot
is
engaged
or
when
an
onside
flight
director
mode
is
selected
on
the
Flight
Guidance
Panel
(FGP). The
V
-bars
turn
from
the
apex
of
the
aircraft
symbol
to
show
computed
pitch
and
roll
flight
director
steering
commands. 3. W
ing
T
ips
—
A
short
yellow
line
on
each
end
of
the
display
represents
the
wing
tips
of
the
aircraft. 4. Marker
Beacon
Annunciation
—
Marker
beacon
status
is
annunciated
by
a
boxed
“OM”
(outer
marker),
boxed
“MM”
(middle
marker),
or
by
an
empty
box
(inner
marker). 5. Roll
Scale
—
A
stationary
roll
scale
consists
of
index
and
triangle
marks
along
an
arc
centered
at
the
aircraft
symbol. Scale
marks
are
at
10,
20,
30,
45
and
60°
left/right
roll
angles. 6. Mistrim
Annunciators
—
Elevator
and
aileron
mistrim
is
annunciated
by
the
letters
E
and
A
in
a
box
with
an
arrow
indicating
the
direction
of
the
mistrim. The
annunciators
are
removed
if
the
control
surface
forces
are
relieved. 7. Flight
Guidance
System
(FGS)
Mode
Annunciation
—
FGS
modes
annunciate
at
the
top
of
the
display
. Lateral
modes
show
on
the
left
side
and
vertical
modes
show
on
the
right
side. Active
modes
show
in
green
and
annunciate
above
the
armed
modes. Armed
modes
show
in
white. A
yellow
horizontal
line
appears
through
a
mode
annunciation
if
the
data
source
(VOR,
etc.)
for
that
mode
is
invalid. 8.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: (ADI)
chunks.text:
Roll
Pointer
—
T
urns
around
the
roll
scale
to
show
the
amount
of
roll
attitude. 9. Autopilot
Engaged
Annunciation
—
When
the
autopilot
is
engaged,
“AP”
annunciates
in
green
with
a

arrow
pointing

button
is

to
cancel. 10. Slip/Skid
Indicator
—
A
white
trapezoid
symbol
that
turns
with
the
roll
pointer
,
but
moves
laterally
from
the
pointer
to
show
aircraft
slip/skid
movement. This
indicator
is
driven
by
lateral
body
accelerations
[sensed
in
the
Attitude
Heading
Computer
(AHC)],
and
replaces
a
traditional
glass-tube
inclinometer
. 1
1. V
ertical
Deviation
Display
—
Can
show
glideslope
or
VNA
V
deviation. a. Glideslope
(1)
Deviation
display
automatically
appears
when
a
LOC
is
the
active
NA
V
source
and
a

frequency
is
tuned. When
making
a
back
course
approach,
this
display
is
replaced
with
a
BACK
CRS
annunciation. If
deviation
data
becomes
invalid,
this
display
is
replaced
with
a
red
GS
annunciation. 20.3-5

urn

Certain
systems

upgrade,

independent
components
(AHRS
1/2,
ADC
1/2,

1/2). These
identical
but
independent
components
continually
cross-talk
to
each
other
to

that
similar
information
they

is
within
a
prescribed

a
dif
ference
in

information

that

in

. 1. Heading
Comparator
—
The
heading
comparator
(yellow
boxed
HDG)

when
the
dif
ference
between
the
pilot's
and

heading
exceeds
6

comparator

for
five

and
then
is
steady
. 2. PFD

Comparator

indicated
airspeed

(yellow
boxed

when
the
dif
ference
between
the
pilot's
and
copilot's
airspeed
exceeds
10
knots. The
indicated
airspeed
comparator
flashes
for
five
seconds
and
then
is
steady
. 4. Aileron,
Elevator
Mistrim
—
A
yellow
boxed
A
or
E
is
annunciated
when
the
autopilot
indicates
a
moderate
aileron
or
elevator
mistrimmed
condition.

ference

data

,

Flag

ith

ith

o

Sources
—

waypoint. The

instantaneous

rate

steep

select

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: (FMS)
chunks.text:
20.3.2
Flight
Management
System
(FMS)
20.3.2.1
Description
The
FMS
is
designed
to
give
the
pilot
centralized
control
of
the
navigation
sensors
and
computer
based
flight
planning. The
system
enables
the
pilot
to
enter
waypoints
into
the
flight
plan
and
create
additional
flight
plan
legs
(100
max.). A
stored
waypoint
and
a
pilot-defined
waypoint
may
be
entered. For
routes
that
are
frequently
traveled,
a
route
database
can
store
and
recall
the
routes
in
a
pilot-defined
database
(100
max.). The
system
blends
inputs
from
both
VORs,
DME,
and
a
GPS
to
compute
present
position
for
display
on
the
CDU. A
remote
tuning
capability
(see
Figure
20.3-13
)
enables
the
FMS
to
tune
the
VHF
COMM,
VHF
NA
V
. A
TC
and
DME. The
FMS
computer
is
located
in
the
Integrated
A
vionics
Processor
System. 20.3.2.1.1
Control
Display
Unit
(CDU)
The
CDU
provides
integrated
control
of
the
FMS,
a
VHF
COMM
receiver
,
and
navigation
radio
subsystems. The
CDU
is
a
color
LCD
display
with
an
integrated
keyboard. Five
colors,
cyan,
yellow
,
magenta,
white,
and
green,
are
used
to
show
text. The
display
area
supports
12
display
lines
of
24
characters
plus
a
title
line,
scratchpad,
and
message
line. The
keyboard
provides
16
function
keys
plus
a
full
alphanumeric
keypad. In
addition,
the
CDU
has
6
line
select
keys
located
in
the
bezel
on
each
side
of
the
display
(see
Figure
20.3-14
).

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: (FMS)
chunks.text:
T
ext
shows
in
either
small
or
lar
ge
size
font. Prompts
show
on
data
lines
as
small
square
boxes. A
prompt
indicates
data
must
be
entered
to
perform
the
function
related
to
the
prompt's
description
on
the
label
line. If
no
data
is
entered
for
a
specific
prompt,
the
FMS
may
not
be
able
to
perform
the
related
function
or
will
have
degraded
performance. Dashes
show
on
data
lines
where
data
can
be
entered. All
operations
for
entering
a
flight
plan,
modifying
an
existing
flight
plan,
or
entering
data
for
other
FMS
operating
functions
are
done
through
the
use
of
a
scratchpad
entry
system. Entry
for
the
scratchpad
is
by
using
the
alphanumeric
keyboard
or
by
pushing
a
line
select
key
to
copy
data
shown
on
the
display
to
the
scratchpad. From
the
scratchpad,
data
is
transferred
by
pushing
the
appropriate
line
select
key
to
transfer
the
scratchpad
content
to
the
selected
location. The
CDU
is
located
in
the
forward
right
portion
of
the
center
pedestal. The
CDU
is
protected
by
a
3-ampere
circuit
breaker
,
labeled
CDU
located
on
the
copilot
outboard
subpanel. The
CDU
receives
power
from
A
vionics
Bus
No. 2. 1. ALPHANUMERIC
KEYS
—
The
alpha
keys
are
used
to
enter
the
26
letters
of
the
alphabet,
a
space,
and
a
slash
(/). The
numeric
keys
are
used
to
enter
numbers
0
to
9,
a
decimal
point
(.)
and
+/-. 2. FUNCTION
KEYS
—
Provides
quick
access
to
frequently
used
CDU
pages. a.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: (FMS)
chunks.text:
MESSAGE
KEY
(MSG)
—
The
MSG
key
is
used
to
display
the
message
page. The
MESSAGES
page
shows
new
and
old
FMS
messages. Push
the
MSG
key
once
to
display
the
MESSAGES
page
and
push
again
to
get
back
to
the
previous
CDU
display
page. b. DIRECT
KEY
(DIR)
—
The
DIR
key
selects
the
Direct-T
o
function
of
the
FMS. c. FLIGHT
PLAN
KEY
(FPLN)
—
The
FPLN
key
displays
the
FMS
Flight
Plan
pages. d. LEGS
KEYS
(LEGS)
—
The
LEGS
key
displays
the
FMS
LEGS
pages. e. DEP
AR
TURE
ARRIV
AL
KEY
(DEP
ARR)
—
The
DEP
ARR
key
displays
one
of
three
pages
(DEP
AR
T
,
ARRIV
AL,
DEP/ARR
INDEX)
depending
upon
the
phase
of
flight. f. PERFORMANCE
KEY
(PERF)
—
The
PERF
key
selects
the
Performance
function
of

LINE

to;

page

A

(system

or

ge
(operator
generated). When

data

a
selected
mode,

mode
text
is
small
(inactive)
or
lar
ge
(active). 6. SCRA
TCHP
AD
LINE
-
The

line
key

shows
on

so

visually
verified
before
being
transferred
to
a
selected
data
field. 7.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: <ENABLED>
chunks.text:
The
operator
can
create
an
along-track
waypoint
by
specifying
a
radial
from
the
reference
waypoint,
a
distance
from
the
reference
waypoint,
or
a
location
abeam
the
reference
waypoint. Each
method
is
described
below:
1. Radial
—
A
radial
fix
is
the
point
at
which
a
specific
radial
from
the
reference
waypoint
intersects
the
flight
plan. The
radial
fix
shows
on
the
PFD
PPOS
display
as
a
small
green
circle
around
the
fix
reference. A
dashed
green
line
also
shows
leading
from
the
green
circle
to
a
small
white
circle
on
the
course
line
at
the
fix
point. T
o
establish
a
radial
fix,
enter
a
three
digit
bearing
value
into
the
scratchpad
and
transfer
it
to
the
RAD
CROSS
data
line. The
FMS
computes
DIST
,
ETE,
and
required
FUEL
to
get
to
the
fix. 2. Distance
—
A
distance
fix
is
the
point
at
which
a
specific
distance
from
the
reference
waypoint
intersects
the
flight
plan. A
distance
fix
shows
as
a
lar
ge
green
circle
(or
partial
circle)
centered
over
the
fix
reference. The
radius
of
the
circle
is
equal
to
the
specified
distance
from
the
fix
reference,
and
small
white
circle
shows
at
the
point
where
the
lar
ge
circle
intersects
the
course
line. T
o
establish
a
distance
fix,
enter
the
desired
distance
into
the
scratchpad
and
transfer
it
to
the
DIS
CROSS
line
select
key
.

The
FMS
computes
DIST
,

FUEL
to
get
to
the
closest
intersection
of
the
distance
circle
with
the
flight
plan
track
line. 3. Abeam
—
An
abeam
fix
is
the
point
on
the
flight
plan
where
the
reference
waypoint
is
perpendicular
to
the
flight
plan
leg. The
abeam

shows

green

around

reference. A
dashed
green
line
also
shows
leading
from
the
green
circle
to
a
small
white
circle
on
the
course
line
at
the
fix
point. T
o
establish
an
abeam
fix,
push
the
ABEAM
REF
line
select
key
. The
FMS
computes
DIST
,
ETE,
and
required
FUEL
to
get
to
the
abeam
fix. If
a
fix
cannot
be
computed
from
the
entered
data,
a
NO
INTERCEPTION
message
is
displayed
on
the
scratchpad. Use
the
CLR
DEL
key
to
clear
a
computed
fix
or
to
clear
the
FIX
INFO
page. Push
the
CLR
DEL
key
to
indicate
DELETE
on
the
scratchpad
line. Push
the
RAD
CROSS
line
select
key
to
delete
all
fix
information
except
the
REF
waypoint
and

data. Push
the
REF
line
select
key
to
delete
all
fix
information
from
this
page. A
second
Fix
Page
is
available. i. Hold

20.3-23

from

Index

line
select
key
. If

the
present
position
of
the
aircraft. 1. IDX
Function
Key
—
Select. 2. HOLD
—
Select. 3. Holding
Point
—
Enter
. T
ype
the
desired
holding
point
into
the
scratchpad
and
transfer
it
to
the
HOLD
A
T
line. PPOS
can
also
be
selected. 4. Holding
parameters
—
Enter
. Select
INBD
CRS/RADIAL,
TURN
DIRECTION,
LEG
TIME,
LEG
DIST
as
required. 20.3.3
Global
Positioning
System
(GPS)
20.3.3.1
Description
The
GPS
sensor
provides
accurate
position
information. The
receiver
is
located
in
the
Nose
A
vionics
Bay
,
bottom
shelf. The
power
for
the
Global
Positioning
System
is
from
A
vionics
Bus
No. 2. The
circuit
breaker
is
3
amperes
and
is
located
on
the
Copilot
outboard
subpanel
labeled
GPS. 20.3.3.2
Limitations
1. During
a
period
of
dead
reckoning,
navigation
shall
not
be
predicated
on
the
GPS
as
a
means
of
operation
in
the
U.S. National
Airspace
System. Following
a
period
of
dead
reckoning,
the
aircraft
position
should
be
verified
by
visually
sighting
a
ground
reference
point
and/or
by
using
a
valid
Navaid
or
other
navigation
equipment. 2. IFR
GPS/GPS
Overlay
Non
Precision
Approach
approval
is
limited
to
published
approaches
within
the
U.S. National
Airspace
System.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Limitations
chunks.text:
Approaches
to
airports
in
other
airspace
are
not
approved
unless
authorized
by
the
appropriate
sovereign
authority
. a. If
an
approach
procedure
is
programmed
on
the
Active
FPLN
and
a
new
approach
is
desired
at
a
dif
ferent
airport,
the
original
approach
should
be
ERASED
before
selecting
the
new
approach
to
insure
proper
waypoint
sequencing
on
the
Active
FPLN. The
waypoint
sequencing
should
always
be
checked
any
time
new
waypoints
are
added
to
the
Active
FPLN. W
aypoint
sequencing
should
be
verified
on
the
Direct
T
o
pages. b. Due
to
system
configuration,
the
radar/map
will
not
display
waypoints
beyond
the
ARC
beginning
point
when
a
DME
ARC
approach
is
programmed
on
the
Active
FPLN. W
aypoints
will
not
reappear
until
the
aircraft
has
passed
the
ARC
end
point
inbound
to
the
F
AF
. c. W
aypoints
depicted
on
approach
plates
between
the
F
AF
and
the
MAP
are
not
in
the
Jeppesen
approach
database,
hence,
are
neither
output
nor
displayed
by
the
GPS. The
GPS
provides
non-coupled
reference
vertical
guidance
during
the
non-precision
approach. This
vertical
guidance
is
computed
as
a
straight
line
descent
between
the
Final
Approach
Fix
(F
AF)
and
the
Missed
Approach
Point
(MAP)
altitudes
as
coded
in
the
Jeppesen
approach
database. In
some
cases,
this
computed
straight-line
descent
does
not
agree
with
the
altitudes
published
for
the
interim
waypoints.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Limitations
chunks.text:
For
Non-Precision
Approach
Procedures
which
include
a
step-down
fix(es)
between
the
F
AF
and
MAP
,
fly
the
approach
using
the
pilot's
altimeter
and
the
interim
waypoint
altitudes
shown
on
the
published
approach
page. d. Due
to
the
nature
of
certain
leg
types
required
to
fly
missed
approach
procedures,
the
aircraft
must
be
flown
manually
or
by
use
of
the
FMS
Heading
mode
to
insure
that
certain
portions
of
the
missed
approach
procedure
are
flown
properly
. 3. When
the
system
sequences
from
en
route
to
terminal
CDI
sensitivity
,
the
aircraft
may
bank
slightly
if
the
cross
track
error
is
greater
than
.05
nm
because
of
increased
roll
steering
gain. 1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Limitations
chunks.text:
4. Due
to
the
system
logic,
the
aircraft
may
momentarily
roll
wings
level
before
turning
to
the
T
O
waypoint
when
the
aircraft
is
in
a
turn
and
a
Direct-T
o
function
is
initiated. 20.3.3.3
Normal
Procedures
The
GPS
operates
as
a
sensor
system
and
is
controlled
by
the
FMS
CDU. 20.3.3.3.1
GPS
Information
The
GPS
page
is
used
to
show
the
current
GPS
position
and
related
GPS
information. This
information
is
for
display
only
and
cannot
be
edited. 1. IDX
function
key
—
Select. 2. GPS
POS
—
Select. 20.3.4
Air
Data
System
(ADS)
20.3.4.1
Description
The
ADS
is
a
dual
system
that
senses
and
processes
data
derived
from
the
air
mass
around
the
aircraft. The
two
ADCs
connect
to
the
pitot/static
air
input
ports
and
to
a
temperature
sensor
. The
ADC
processes
the
air
data
inputs
and
provides
the
air
data
parameters
to
the
PFDs,
attitude
heading
computers,
T
AS,
and
the
IAPS
Input/Output
Concentrators
(
Figure
20.3-51
). The
ADCs
are
located
in
the
Nose
A
vionics
Bay
,
middle
shelf. ADC
1
and
ADC
2
are
powered
by
A
vionics
Bus
1
and
2
respectively
. They
are
protected
by
2-ampere
circuit
breakers
located
on
the
Copilot
outboard
subpanel
and
labeled
ADC
1
and
ADC
2. 20.3.4.2
Limitations
None. 20.3.4.3
Normal
Procedures
The
ADS
operates
as
a
sensor
system
and
the
extracted
information
is
displayed
on
the
FMS
CDU
and
PFDs.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Limitations
chunks.text:
An
operator
selectable
switch
is
located
on
the
pilot
and
copilot
instrument
panels
to
change
reference
sources. The
following
paragraph
describes
the
switch
function. 20.3.4.3.1
Air
Data
Computer
(ADC)
Reversion
Switch
The
ADC
reversion
switch,
Figure
20.3-52
,
is
a
three-position
switch
labeled
1,
NORM,
and
2. The
NORM
position
selects
ADC
1
data
for
the
pilot
side
computations
and
ADC
2
data
for
the
copilot
side
computations. If
ADC
1
fails,
the
switch
can
be
positioned
in
the
2
position
to
use
data
from
ADC
2
for
both
pilot
and
copilot
computations. An
ADC
2
failure
would
require
the
switch
be
placed
in
the
1
position. The
PFD
will
annunciate
a
yellow-boxed
ADC
1
or
ADC
2
at
the
top
left
side,
depending
upon
the
switch
position. 20.3-57
1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: (AHRS)
chunks.text:
20.3.5
Attitude
Heading
Reference
System
(AHRS)
20.3.5.1
Description
The
AHRS
is
a
dual
strap-down
reference
system
that
senses
the
aircraft
attitude,
heading,
and
3-axis
rate/accelerations. Both
systems
contain
an
attitude
heading
computer
,
external
compensation
unit,
and
flux
detector
unit. The
Attitude
Heading
Computer
(AHC)
replaces
a
conventional
vertical
gyro,
directional
gyro,
three
rate
gyros,
and
three
linear
accelerometers. The
AHC
utilizes
a
quartz-based
inertial
measurement
unit
that
measures
angular
rates
and
linear
accelerations
about
the
body
axis
of
the
aircraft. The
AHC
provides
attitude
and
heading
parameters
to
the
PFDs,
the
flight
guidance
computers,
and
the
IAPS
Input/Output
Concentrators
(
Figure
20.3-53
). The
AHRS
1
is
powered
by
the
A
vionics
Bus
No. 1
and
AHRS
2
is
powered
by
the
A
vionics
Bus
No. 2. Both
have
a
battery
bus
input
to
sustain
the
system
in
short
outages. The
AHRS
computers
are
mounted
on
the
floor
of
the
aircraft
cabin
behind
the
pilot's
seat. The
circuit
breakers
for
the
AHRS
are
on
the
Copilot
outboard
subpanel
and
labeled
AHRS
1
and
AHRS
2. The
External
Compensation
Unit
(ECU)
stores
the
compensation
data
in
nonvolatile
memory
. The
compensation
data
corrects
the
hard-iron
errors
caused
by
the
aircraft
altering
the
earth's
magnetic
field
and
Flux
Detector
Unit
(FDU)
misalignment
(heading)
errors.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: (AHRS)
chunks.text:
The
FDUs
are
mounted
in
the
left
and
right
wingtips,
and
provide
the
magnetic
flux
measurements
to
the
AHC. The
AHC
computes
the
aircraft
heading
from
the
magnetic
flux
measurements. 20.3.5.2
Limitations
None. 20.3.5.3
Normal
Procedures
The
AHS
operates
as
a
sensor
system
and
the
extracted
information
is
displayed
on
the
FMS
CDU
and
PFDs. The
following
paragraphs
describe
the
function
of
various
AHRS
switches
located
on
the
reversionary
panel. 20.3.5.3.1
Attitude
Heading
Reference
System
(AHRS)
Reversion
Switch
The
AHRS
reversion
switch,
Figure
20.3-54
,
is
a
three-position
switch
labeled:
1,
NORM,
and
2. The
NORM
position
selects
AHC
1
data
for
the
pilot
side
computations
and
AHC
2
data
for
the
copilot
side
computations. If
AHC
1
fails,
the
switch
can
be
positioned
in
the
2
position
to
use
data
from
AHC
2
for
both
pilot
and
copilot
computations. An
AHC
2
failure
would
require
the
switch
be
placed
in
the
1
position. The
PFD
will
annunciate
a
yellow-boxed
AHC
1
or
AHC
2
at
the
top
left
side,
depending
upon
the
switch
position. 20.3-59
1

Figure

AHRS
Rev
Switch
20.3.5.3.2
DG
Switches
Both
pilot
and
copilot
have
a
two-position
DG
switch,
Figure
20.3-55
,
to
control
the
onside
attitude
heading
computer
.
In
the
NORM
mode,
heading
computations
are
slaved
to
the
magnetic
heading
sensed
by
the
FDU.
Switching
to
the
FREE
mode
will
disable
the
magnetic
heading
input
and
select
DG
gyro
free
mode.
In
this
mode,
the
SLEW
switch
is
enabled.
Figure
20.3-55.
DG
Switch
20.3.5.3.3
SLEW
Switches
Both
pilot
and
copilot
have
a
two-position
SLEW
switch,
Figure
20.3-56
,
to
control
the
onside
attitude
heading
computer
gyro
when
the
corresponding
DG
switch
is
selected
in
the
FREE
mode.
The
SLEW
switch
will
change
the
heading
+
or
-
from
the
current
reading.
20.3-61
1

chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
Figure
20.3-56. Slew
Switch
20.3.6
Radio
Altimeter
(RA)
20.3.6.1
Description
The
RA
transceiver
measures
the
aircraft
radio
altitude
(up
to
2,500
feet)
above
the
terrain. This
data
is
an
analog
output
that
is
converted
to
a
digital
signal
that
is
used
by
the
EFIS,
autopilot
(during
approach),
T
AS
and
FMS. The
receiver
is
located
in
the
avionics
compartment
and
the
test
switch
is
located
on
the
overhead
control
panel. The
system
is
powered
by
the
A
vionics
Bus
No. 1
and
is
protected
by
a
3-ampere
circuit
breaker
located
on
the
Copilot
outboard
subpanel
and
labeled
RAD
AL
T
. 20.3.6.2
Limitations
None. 20.3.6.3
Normal
Procedures
The
RA
operates
as
a
sensor
system
and
the
extracted
information
is
displayed
on
the
FMS
CDU
and
PFDs. 20.3.7
Radio
Sensor
System
(RSS)
20.3.7.1
Description
The
RSS
system
consists
of
two
display/control
units
and
the
installed
Communication,
Navigation
and
Identification
(CNI)
suite. The
display
and
control
portion
of
the
system
consists
of
a
CDU
and
a
Radio
T
uning
Unit
(R
TU). Both
units
provide
integrated
control
of
several
combinations
of
aircraft
communications
and
navigation
subsystems. The
integrated
control
includes
the
setting
of
radio
frequencies,
beacon
codes,
operational
modes
and
test
functions.

chunks.text:
The
aircraft
RSS
comes
equipped
with
one
VHF
communication
transceiver
,
two
VOR/LOC/GS/MKR
BCN
transceivers,
one
ADF
receiver
,
one
DME
transceiver
,
and
one
A
TC
Mode-S
T
ransponder
. The
Navigation
aspect
of
the
RSS
will
be
discussed
in
this
section
and
the
Communications
portion
will
described
in
Paragraph
20.4.3
RSS
VHF
COM. 1

2023

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Navigation
chunks.text:
20.3.7.1.1
VHF
Navigation
System
The
VHF
NA
V
System
is
a
remotely
located,
digital,
integrated
navigation
system
which
includes
separate
navigation
receivers. These
receivers
are
designed
to
receive
and
process
VHF
omnirange,
localizer
,
glideslope,
marker
beacon,
and
ADF
signals
(VOR1
only). The
VOR/LOC
receiver
processes
VOR
and
localizer
signals
in
the
frequency
range
of
108.00
MHz
to
1
17.95
MHz
in
50
kHz
increments
(160
VOR
channels,
40
localizer
channels). Power
to
operate
NA
V
1
and
NA
V
2
is
provided
by
the
A
vionics
Essential
Bus
and
the
A
vionics
Bus
No. 2
respectively
. Protection
for
each
system
is
provided
by
3-ampere
circuit
breakers
located
on
the
copilot
outboard
subpanel
and
marked
NA
V
1
and
NA
V
2. The
receivers
are
located
in
the
nose
avionics
bay
,
bottom
shelf. 20.3.7.1.2
Automatic
Direction
Finder
(ADF)
System
The
ADF
system
is
contained
in
the
remote
mounted
NA
V
1
receiver
and
has
a
bottom-mounted,
ADF
Antenna. Power
to
operate
the
ADF
is
provided
by
the
NA
V
1
system. The
receiver
is
integrated
with
the
NA
V
unit
and
is
located
in
the
Nose
A
vionics
Bay
,
bottom
shelf. 20.3.7.1.3
Distance
Measuring
Equipment
(DME)
System
The
DME
system
consists
of
a
remote
mounted
transceiver
and
a
bottom
mounted
blade
antenna.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Navigation
chunks.text:
The
system
is
an
all-digital,
triple
channel
scanning
DME
that
enables
one
channel
to
be
tuned
by
the
flight
crew
to
provide
distance
information
to
a
co-located
VOR
station. The
two
remaining
scanning
channels
are
automatically
tuned
by
the
FMS
to
provide
continuous
DME
information
and
assist
in
determining
the
aircraft
RNA
V
position. Power
to
operate
the
DME
is
provided
by
the
A
vionics
Bus
No. 1
through
a
2-ampere
circuit
breaker
,
marked
DME
located
on
the
Copilot
outboard
subpanel. The
receiver
is
located
in
the
Nose
A
vionics
Bay
,
bottom
shelf. 20.3.7.1.4
Mode
S
T
ransponder
The
Mode
S
T
ransponder
is
an
airborne
transponder
designed
to
perform
both
conventional
and
advanced
A
TC
functions. It
receives
coded
radar
signals
from
A
TC
ground
stations
and
other
aircraft
and
transmits
coded
replies. It
is
also
is
an
integral
part
of
the
installed
T
raf
fic
A
voidance
System
(T
AS). The
Mode
S
transponder
is
interfaced
with
the
R
TU
for
tuning
of
the
Mode
3A
codes,
the
ADC
for
Mode
C
inputs,
T
AS
computer
unit
and
the
upper
and
lower
Mode
S
antennas. Power
to
operate
the
Mode
S
is
provided
by
the
A
vionics
Bus
No. 2
through
a
3-ampere
circuit
breaker
marked
A
TC
and
located
on
the
Right
Hand
Subpanel. The
receiver
is
located
in
the
Nose
A
vionics
Bay
,
middle
shelf. a.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Navigation
chunks.text:
Remote
T
ransponder
Switch
The
ability
to
change
the
transponder
from
STBY
to
ON
is
provided
by
a
remote
mode
switch,
Figure
20.3-57
,
located
on
the
reversionary
panel
below
the
R
TU. The
position
of
this
switch
is
displayed
on

20.3.7.1.5
Mode
S
T
ransponder
with

Out
(TDR-94D)
The

is
a
Mode
S
transponder
with
ADS-B
Out
capabilities
controlled
through
the
R
TU
or
CDU. The
TDR-94D
receives
data
from
the
GPS,
the
input/output
concentrator
(IOC),
and
ADC. The
transmission
of
ADS-B
OUT
data
on
1090
extended
squitter
includes:
•
GPS
position,
altitude,
position
integrity
•
Ground
track,
heading,
ground
speed,
and
velocity
integrity
•
Air/Ground
status
•
Flight
ID,
Call
Sign,
ICAO
registration
number
•
Capability
and
Status
information
•
T
ransponder
squawk
code,
IDENT
,
and
emer
gency
status
20.3.7.1.5.1
TDR-94D
Operating
Procedures
1. Input
appropriate
squawk
via
the
R
TU
or
CDU
TUNE
page. 2. Entering
the
FLIGHT
ID
from
the
CDU:
a. Push
the
TUN
function
key
on
the
CDU
to
show
the
TUNE
page,
then
press
the
NEXT
function
key
to
show
TUNE
page
2/2. b. Enter
the
desired
code
into
the
scratchpad. The
code
can
be
any
combination
of
up
to
eight
characters
and
digits. c. Push
the
FLIGHT
ID
line
select
key
to
transfer
the
contents
of
the
scratchpad
to
the
FLIGHT
ID
field. 3. Entering
the
FIGHT
ID
from
the
R
TU:
a. From
the
R
TU
top
level
page,
push
the
A
TC
line
select
key
to
position
the
tune
window
around
the
first
character
of
the
FLIGHT
ID
code. b. Rotate
the
tuning
knobs
to
set
the
desired
FL
T
ID
code. c. T
o
swap
the
active
and
recall
FL
T
ID
code,
push
the
FL
T
ID
line
select
key
twice.

F
AIL]
annunciation

DEVICE
(XPDR)
F

A
Code

(

20.3-58
).

20.3.8
T
ACAN
System
The
T
ACAN
system
operates
in
three
basic
modes
to
provide
distance,
groundspeed,
time-to-station,
station
identifier
,
and
T
ACAN
azimuth
(bearing
or
radial)
information. The
three
basic
modes
of
operation
provided
by
the
T
ACAN
system
are
receive,
transmit/receive,
and
air
-to-air
transmit
receive. In
the
receive
mode
of
operation,
the
T
ACAN
system
provides
continuous
bearing
to
a
T
ACAN
ground
station
and
aural
identification
of
the
ground
beacon
to
which
the
system
is
channeled. In
the
transmit/receive
mode,
the
T
ACAN
system
provides
all
of
the
receive
mode
functions
listed
in
addition
to
the
following:
1. Distance
(slant
range)
of
the
aircraft
from
the
ground
station
2. T
ime-to-station
(time
for
the
aircraft
to
fly
to
the
ground
station)
3. Groundspeed
(inbound
or
outbound
aircraft
speed
in
relation
to
the
ground
station)
4. Generation
a
suppression
output
to
external
systems
during
the
time
of
its
own
transmission
In
the
air
-to-air
transmit/receive
mode,
the
T
ACAN
operates
as
both
interrogator
and
transponder
,
and
provides
the
following
functions:
1. Responds
up
to
five
aircraft
when
interrogated
2. Provides
slant
range
distance
to
the
nearest
complementary
aircraft
3. Provides
time
to
the
nearest
complementary
aircraft
4.

Shows
speed

T

The

located

radio

emer
gency
frequency

MHz,

.
All
other

commands
from
the
R
TU
or
CDU
are

when
this

is

The
switch

the
emer
gency
frequency
is

word

is

on

R
TU.
20.4.4
Headset
Noise
Cancellation
System
20.4.4.1
Description
The
electronic
noise
cancellation
system
uses
an
electronically
produced
signal
to
cancel
the
of
fending
noise.
Low
frequency
noise
is
sensed
and
an
opposing
signal
of
the
same
sound
level
is
generated.
The
result
is
cancellation
of
the
noise
before
it
reaches
the
headset.
The
headset
connectors,
Figure
20.4-7
,
for
the
pilot
and
copilot
are
located
on
the
outboard
portion
of
the
instrument
panel
and
the
observer's
connector
is
located
on
the
audio
panel
on
the
right
side
of
the
cabin.
The
pilot,
copilot
and
observer
systems
received
power
from
avionics
buses
No.
1,
2
and
3
respectively
and
are
protected
by
2
ampere
circuit
breakers,
located
on
the
center
pedestal
and
labeled
pilot
headset,
copilot
headset
and
observer
headset.

Normal
Procedures
1. Attach

cord
from

headset
to
the
noise
cancellation
panel. 2. Place
the
two
position
switch
in
the
ON
position. 3. Adjust
the
headset
as
necessary
to
unsure
a
proper
fit. 4. Adjust
the
volume
control
on
the
headset. 20.4.5
Emergency
Locator
T
ransmitter
(EL
T)
20.4.5.1
Description
The
Artex
C406N
series
EL
T
is
a
single
output
EL
T
that
incorporates
latitude/longitude
information
from
the
aircraft
navigation
system. All
3
emer
gency
frequencies
(121.5/243.0
and
406.028
MHz)
utilize
the
same
RF
output,
which
requires
only
1
coax
cable
to
connect
to
the
single
input
antenna. The
EL
T
unit
is
mounted
just
within
the
right
side
of
the
aft
fuselage
at
approximately
FS
340.00. The
associated
antenna
is
mounted
on
top
of
the
aft
fuselage
approximately
above
the
transmitter
,
(it
is
hidden
from
view
from
outside
the
aircraft). The
EL
T
automatically
activates
during
a
crash
and
transmits
the
standard
swept
tone
on
121.5
and
243.0
MHz. Every
50
seconds
for
520
milliseconds
(long
message
protocol)
the
406.028
MHz
transmitter
turns
on
and
transmits
an
encoded
digital
message
to
the
Cospas/Sarsat
satellite
system. The
EL
T
has
the
ability
to
communicate
with
the
aircraft's
navigation
system
and
receive
position
data
(longitude
and
latitude).

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Limitations
chunks.text:
The
EL
T
has
an
on-board
power
supply
backup
that
will
retain
position
data
for
greater
than
36
seconds
should
aircraft
power
be
lost. One
advantage
of
the
406.028
MHz
transmitter
is
that
it
will
produce
a
more
accurate
position,
typically
1
to
2
kilometers
as
compared
to
15
to
20
kilometers
for
121.5/243.0
MHz
transmitters. When
coupled
with
the
aircraft's
navigation
system
the
position
accuracy
improves
to
approximately
100
meters. The
C406-N
series
EL
T
is
housed
in
a
high
impact
fire
resistant
polycarbonate
plastic
case. Refer
to
Figure
20.4-8
. The
EL
T
unit
is
able
to
withstand
extremely
harsh
environments. Units
have
been
subjected
to
numerous
500g
shock
pulses,
1,000-pound
crush
weights
and
severe
penetrations
tests
and
continue
to
operate
normally
. Continued
operation
in
a
temperature
range
of
-20
through
+55
°C
is
assured. The
Battery
Pack
for
the
EL
T
consists
of
4
“D”
size
Lithium
Manganese
Dioxide
cells
connected
in
series. In
an
ef
fort
to
increase
the
safety
of
the
battery
pack,
a
number
of
features
were
designed
into
the
battery
pack. T
o
prevent
the
cells
from
being
char
ged,
diodes
are
connected
across
each
cell
and
fuses
are
connected
to
the
output. The
crash
force
activation
sensor
or
G-switch
is
designed
to
activate
with
a
change
of
velocity
of
4.5
ft/s
±0.5
ft/s
both
under
normal
conditions
and
while
being

to
30
G's
of

axis
forces.

There

chunks.text:
20.4.5.3
T
urn-On
Procedure
Since
the
system
is
always
in
the
arm
position
there
is
no
need
to
turn
it
on,
an
impact
will
accomplish
this. If
there
is
a
need
to
manually
activate
the
system,
select
the
remote
switch
to
ON
or
move
the
switch
on
the
EL
T
to
ON. 20.4.5.4
Normal
Operation
Procedure
The
EL
T
unit
is
considered
to
be
either
ACTIVE
or
in
ST
ANDBY
. ST
ANDBY
indicates
that
the
unit
receives
position
data
but
is
otherwise
inactive. T
aking
the
unit
from
the
ST
ANDBY
to
the
ACTIVE
state
requires
a
positive
switch
transition
from
one
of
three
sources:
Panel
mounted
switch,
Unit
mounted
switch,
or
the
G
Switch. Upon
entering
the
active
state,
the
unit
shall
meet
all
the
requirements
as
described
herein. T
o
exit
the
ACTIVE
state
and
enter
the
ST
ANDBY
state,
a
RESET
condition
must
be
entered. This
may
be
accomplished
in
one
of
two
ways:
1. The
front
panel
switch
may
be
toggled
from
the
ARMED/RESET
position
to
ON
and
then
back
to
ARMED/RESET
or
if
the
switch
is
already
in
the
ON
position,
it
must
be
placed
into
the
ARMED/RESET
position. 2. The
EL
T
unit
switch
may
be
moved
from
the
OFF
position
to
the
ON
position
and
then
back
to
the
OFF
position. The
EL
T
cannot
be
reset
from
the
opposite
switch
position
if
one
of
the
switches
remains
in
the
ON
position.

chunks.text:
20.4.5.5
Emergency
Operation
Procedure
Switch
should
always
be
in
the
ARM
position
unless
there
is
a
known
impending
crash
or
emer
gency
landing
away
from
a
suitable
airfield
and
time
permits
activation. In
this
case,
select
the
switch
to
ON. In
either
case,
the
406.028
MHz
transmitter
with
the
supplied
battery
will
operate
for
24
hours,
and
then
shutdown
automatically
. The
121.5/243.0
MHz
transmitter
will
continue
to
operate
until
the
unit
has
exhausted
the
battery
power
which
will
be
greater
than
50
hours. 20.4.5.6
Shutdown

T
o
reset

T
for

activation
move
remote
switch
above
copilot's
PFD
from

second. 1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: FLIGHT
chunks.text:
20.5
FLIGHT
SYSTEM
The
Flight
System
includes
the
FGS,
IAPS,
and
ancillary
equipment. 20.5.1
Flight
Guidance
System
(FGS)
20.5.1.1
Description
The
FGS
provides
dual
Flight
Guidance,
a
three
axis
autopilot
including
yaw
damper
,
and
pitch
trim. It
is
a
dual
system
made
up
of
two
Flight
Guidance
Computers,
a
Flight
Guidance
Panel,
and
four
servos. Controls
located
on
the
FGP
are
used
by
the
crew
to
set
the
desired
flight
guidance
mode,
and
to
engage
and
disengage
the
Autopilot
and
Y
aw
Damper
. Other
inputs
to
the
FGS
are
controlled
by
the
PFD
and
DCPs. Remote-mounted
switches
on
the
control
wheels,
throttle
levers,
and
other
cockpit
locations
also
have
FGS
functions. External
inputs
to
the
FGS
include
those
from
the
AHRS,
ADC,
navigation
receivers,
and
the
aircraft
pitch
trim
system. Lateral
and
vertical
modes
selected
by
the
pilot
are
used
by
the
FGC
to
generate
steering
commands. The
steering
commands
are
supplied
to
the
Flight
Directors
and
the
Autopilot. When
a
Flight
Director
is
in
view
on
the
PFD,
the
pilot
can
follow
the
steering
commands
supplied
by
the
FGC. When
the
Autopilot
is
engaged,
the
Autopilot
servos
maneuver
the
aircraft
using
the
steering
commands
supplied
by
the
FGC. The
FGS
receives
power
for
the
pitch,
roll,
yaw
and
pitch
trim
servos
from
A
vionics
Bus
No. 1.

pitch,

and

are

by

5
ampere
circuit

1

2. The

3

Pitch
T

aw

chunks.text:
1. Flight
Director
Button
(FD)
—
T
wo
FD
buttons
are
installed. The
left
side
(Pilot)
button
controls
the
left
side
(PFD)
FD;
the
right
side
(Copilot)
button
controls
the
right
side
(PFD)
FD. These
buttons
can
turn
a
FD
on
and
of
f. At
power
-up,
both
flight
directors
are
of
f. The
(selected)
flight
director
automatically
turns
on
when
the
autopilot
is
engaged,
or
when
a
vertical
or
lateral
mode
is

the
other
FD
button
to
alternately
turn
the
(of
fside)
FD
ON
and
OFF
. The
FD
button
of
a
coupled
FD
is
not
functional. 2. VS/Pitch
Wheel
—
T
urn
the
VS/Pitch
wheel
to
change
the
vertical
reference
value
used
by

speed
and
pitch
modes. This

is
not
functional
when
glideslope
is
captured. In
VS
mode,
turn
this
wheel
to
change
the
vertical
speed
reference
value. When
not
in

mode,
turn
the
wheel
to
input
a
pitch
“take
command”
function. The
pitch
mode
is
selected
and
any
active
vertical
mode
(except
GS
capture)
clears. T
urn
the
wheel
to
change
the
pitch
reference
value. Move
the
wheel
forward
to
command
pitchdown,
or
backward
to
command
pitch-up
attitude. 3. Mode
Buttons
a. VS
—
Push
the
VS
button
to
alternately
select
or
deselect
vertical
speed
mode. VS
and
the
vertical
speed
reference
value
annunciate
on
the
PFD. An
up
arrow
also
annunciates
for
positive
VS;
a
down
arrow
annunciates
for
negative
VS.

The
FGC
generates
commands
to

the
vertical
speed
(reference)

when

urn

V
—
Push
the
VNA

arm

navigation

Management
Computer
(FMC)

the
VNA
V
capture
point. After
capture
(VNA
V
modes

steering

FGC. VNA
V

waypoint

—
Push
the

(flight

and

an

or

reference
airspeed. The
mode

into

the

to

or

to

to

preselected

V
altitude

the

ability
(e.g. thrust

to

. The

when

V
modes. d. NA
V
—
Push
the
NA
V
button
to
alternately
select
or
deselect
navigation
mode. The
FGC/FMC
generates
lateral
commands
to
fly
the
active
navigation
course. The
navigation
source
is
selected
from
the
PFD
NA
V
SOURCE
menu.

The
(active
course)
NA
V
identifier

1,

select

FGC

to

preselected
Altitude

T

altitude

T

SYNC

Push

. The

aw

The
coupled
side
is
the

a
green
AP
←

to

(coupled
to

side)

the
autopilot
disconnect
to

the

if
the

damper
is

or

autopilot

a

autopilot

and

Button

a
(center)

(copilot
PFD). Clockwise

the

in

ge

Button
—

the
(center)

switch
to

from

mode,

to

—

urn

to

or

reference

Integrated
A
vionics
Processor
System
(IAPS)
The
IAPS
assembly
does
part
of
the
integration
function
required
to
interconnect
and
manage
the
various
avionics
subsystems
in
the
aircraft. The
IAPS
assembly
may
be
considered
as
part
of
the
aircraft
wiring
that
physically
houses
some
avionics
units. The
IAPS
Card
Cage
(ICC)
assembly
has
a
fully-wired
card-cage
that
holds
the
following
line
replaceable
modules:
1. One
environmental
controller
. The
ICC
provides
an
isolated
interface
between
the
resident
LRMs
(Line
Replaceable
Modules)
and
the
external
units. Each
half
of
the
ICC
motherboard
has
a
temperature
sensor
. The
IAPS
Environmental
Controller
monitors
the
temperature
sensors
and
operates
a
cooling
fan
and/or
heater
to
automatically
regulate
the
IAPS
environment. 2. T
wo
independent
power
supplies. The
power
supplies
provide
two
separate
power
sources
that
independently
power
the
left
FGC,
left
IOC,
right
FGC,
and
right
IOC. One
PWR
powers
the
left
side
LRMs
and
associated
portions
of
the
ICC.

chunks.text:
The
other
PWR
powers
the
right
side
LRMs
and
associated
portions
of
the
ICC. 3. T
wo
Independent
Input/Output
Concentrators
(IOC). The
IOCs
provide
a
data
management
function
by
acting
as
a
central
data
collection
and
distribution
point. 4. T
wo
configuration
strapping
units. These
concentrators
receive
data
bus
inputs
from
each
major
LRU
on
the
aircraft,
process
(sort)
the
data
words,
and
then
transmit
(only)
the
words
of
interest
to
each
receiving
LRU. The
Configuration
Strapping
Unit
provides
a
matrix
of
configuration
shunts
that
program
the
IAPS
specifically
for
operation
on
the
Raytheon
King
Air
aircraft. 5. T
wo
flight
guidance
computers
(part
of
the
Flight
Guidance
System). a. Flight
Management
Computer
(part
of
the
Flight
Management
System). b. Maintenance
Diagnostic
Computer
. The
MDC
receives
diagnostic
information
from
the
Rockwell-Collins
components
through
the
IOCs. The
MDC
processes
and
records
the
data
for
fault
messages
and
LRU
fault
history
. Maintenance
personnel
can
access
this
information
by
using
a
MDC
switch
located
in
the
avionics
compartment
which
converts
the
copilot's
PFD
to
a
MFD. In
this
configuration,
maintenance
information
can
either
be
viewed
real
time
or
download
it
to
a
lap
top
computer
. An
input
source
to
the
IAPS
is
derived
from
the
Data
Concentrator
Unit
which
digitizes
aircraft
discrete
and
analog
inputs.

T
raffic
Advisory
System
(T
AS)
T
AS
is
an

system

for

and

aircraft
near

own
aircraft. T
AS
includes
a
T
AS

,

and

and

other

by

their
transponders.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: AIRBORNE
chunks.text:
Aircraft
detected,
tracked,
and
displayed
by
T
AS
are
referred
to
as
Intruders. T
AS
analyzes
the
transponder
replies
to
determine
range,
bearing
and
relative
altitude,
if
the
Intruder
is
reporting
altitude. Should
the
T
AS
processor
determine
that
a
possible
collision
hazard
exists,
it
issues
visual
and
aural
advisories
to
the
crew
. The
visual
advisory
is
shown
by
symbols
on
the
traf
fic
display
. Complementing
the
traf
fic
display
,
T
AS
provides
appropriate
synthesized
voice
announcements
in
the
cockpit. T
AS
traf
fic,
when
selected,
can
be
displayed
on
both
the
MFD
and
the
PFDs. T
AS
is
unable
to
detect
any
Intruding
aircraft
without
an
operating
transponder
. T
AS
can
detect
and
track
aircraft
with
either
an
A
TCRBS
(operating
in
Mode
A
or
C)
or
Mode
S
transponders. The
traf
fic
display
,
Figure
20.6-2
shows
the
Intruding
aircraft's
position. T
AS
identifies
the
relative
threat
of
each
Intruder
by
using
various
symbols
and
colors. The
Intruder's
altitude,
relative
to
your
own
aircraft's
altitude,
is
annunciated
if
the
Intruder
is
reporting
altitude. A
trend
arrow
is
used
to
indicate
if
the
Intruder
is
climbing
or
descending
more
than
500
feet
per
minute. T
AS
modes
and
functions
are
controlled
by
switches
located
on
the
MFD. A
TC
procedures
and
the
“see
and
avoid
concept”
will
continue
to
be
the
primary
means
of
ensuring
aircraft
separation.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: EGPWS
chunks.text:
20.6.1.3.1
T
errain
Inhibit
The
EGPWS
has
a
T
errain
Inhibit
function
as
part
of
the
system
installation. The
purpose
of
this
function
is
to
allow
aircraft
to
operate
without
nuisance
or
unwanted
warnings
at
airports
that
are
not
in
the
system
database. When
engaged
by
the
pilot,
this
function
will
inhibit
all
visual
and
aural
alerts
and
warnings
associated
with
the
EGPWS. Also,
a
message
will
be
displayed
on
the
MFD
indicating
“W
arnings
Inhibited”. The
terrain
display
remains
operational. 20.6.1.3.2
T
errain
N/A
The
amber
T
errain
Not
A
vailable
annunciator
is
located
on
the
center
instrument
panel
below
the
FGP
. In
the
event
position
information
from
the
GPS
is
not
available,
or
other
problems
with
the
EGPWS
unit
occurs,
this
light
will
illuminate
and
the
terrain
display
will
be
removed
from
the
MFD. 20.6.1.3.3
Look-Ahead
Alerting
and
W
arning
Using
aircraft
position,
altitude
and
flight
path
information,
the
EGPWS
provides
an
envelope
of
protection
for
the
aircraft
that
is
independent
from
the
T
errain
A
wareness
Display
. This
Look-Ahead
function
compares
the
aircraft
flight
path
to
terrain
and
obstacle
database
information,
and
distance
to
known
runways.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: EGPWS
chunks.text:
When
the
“Look-Ahead”
function
detects
a
terrain
or
obstacle
threat
approximately
one
minute
ahead
of
the
aircraft,
the
voice
alert
“Caution
T
errain,
Caution
T
errain”
(or
“Caution
Obstacle,
Caution
Obstacle”)
is
given,
a
bright,
solid
yellow
“threat
area”
is
shown
on
the
T
errain
Display
and
the
TERRAIN
yellow
caution
annunciator
on
the
instrument
panel
will
illuminate. Should
the
aircraft
flight
path
continue
toward
the
threat
area,
the
alert
message
will
repeat
approximately
every
7
seconds. If
the
aircraft
flight
path
approaches
to
within
approximately
30
seconds
of
a
threat
area,
the
voice
message
“T
errain,
Pull
Up”
(or
“Obstacle-Obstacle,
Pull
Up”)
will
be
given
continuously
,
the
threat
area
on
the
T
errain
Display
will
be
shown
in
a
bright,
solid
red
color
and
the
PULL
UP
red
warning
annunciator
on
the
instrument
panel
will
illuminate. In
either
case,
when
the
pilot
reacts
and
changes
the
aircraft
flight
path
to
one
that
will
safely
avoid
the
detected
threat
area,
the
voice
alerts
will
cease
and
the
threat
area(s)
shown
on
the
T
errain
Display
will
be
removed. 20.6.1.3.4
Runway
Field
Clearance
Floor
(RFCF)
The
EGPWS
provides
additional
alerting
protection
for
situations
where
the
aircraft
descends
to
an
altitude
that
is
too
low
considering
the
aircraft's
distance
from
a
known
runway
. This
is
called
the
Runway
Field
Clearance
Floor
(RFCF).

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: EGPWS
chunks.text:
Note
This
alert
function
is
ONL
Y
active
when
the
aircraft
is
within
5
nm
of
a
known
runway
in
the
system
database. Using
the
aircraft
distance
to
a
known
runway
and
Geometric
Altitude,
the
system
establishes
a
“floor”
of
protection
below
the
aircraft. Penetration
of
this
floor
will
cause
the
TERRAIN
yellow
caution
annunciator
lamp
to
illuminate,
and
the
voice
alert
“T
oo
Low
,
T
oo
Low”
to
be
heard. If
aircraft
altitude
continues
to
descend,
the
voice
alert
will
be
heard
again,
and
at
an
increasing
frequency
. When
the
pilot
reacts
to
the
alert
and
climbs
back
above
the
RFCF
for
the
current
distance
from
the
known
runway
,
the
annunciator
lamp
will
extinguish
and
the
voice
alerts
will
cease. 20.6.1.3.5
Excessive
Rate
of
Descent
Alerting
and
W
arning
Using
vertical
velocity
information
provided
by
the
onboard
ADC,
the
EGPWS
will
provide
alerting
and
warning
to
the
pilot
when
the
aircraft
is
descending
toward
terrain
at
a
high
rate
for
its
relative
altitude
above
terrain. This
function
is
always
active. Initially
,
the
voice
alert
“Sink
Rate”
will
be
heard,
and
the
TERRAIN
yellow
caution
annunciator
lamp
will
illuminate. If
the
aircraft
continues
in
the
high
rate
of
descent,
the
“Sink
Rate-Sink
Rate”
voice
alert
will
be
repeated
at
an
increasing
frequency
. 1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: warning
chunks.text:
Should
the
aircraft
penetrate
the
warning
boundary
,
the
voice
alert
“Pull
Up”
will
be
heard
continuously
and
the
PULL
UP
red
warning
annunciator
lamp
will
illuminate. In
both
cases,
as
the
pilot
reacts
to
decrease
the
high
rate
of
descent
and
the
aircraft
flight
path
exits
the
alerting/warning
envelope,
the
annunciator
lamp
will
extinguish
and
the
voice
alerts
will
cease. Sometimes,
the
alerting
and
warning
functionality
for
excessive
rate
of
descent
may
be
overridden
by
the
terrain
“Look-Ahead”
functionality
. This
is
normal
as
the
“Look-Ahead”
function
has
a
higher
priority
in
the
system
alerting/warning
logic. 20.6.1.3.6
Inadvertent
Descent/Loss
of
Altitude
After
T
ake-Off
The
EGPWS
uses
known
runway
position
and
elevation
information
to
monitor
altitude
during
take-of
f
and
initial
climb. This
function
is
active
until
the
aircraft
reaches
an
altitude
of
approximately
700
feet
above
the
runway
elevation
used
for
take-of
f. Should
the
aircraft
experience
an
inadvertent
descent
or
loss
of
altitude
after
take-of
f,
the
TERRAIN
yellow
caution
annunciator
lamp
will
illuminate
and
the
verbal
alert
“Don't
Sink-Don't
Sink”
will
be
heard. The
voice
alerts
will
be
repeated
with
increasing
frequency
.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: warning
chunks.text:
As
the
pilot
adjusts
the
flight
path
of
the
aircraft
and
a
positive
rate
of
climb
is
re-established,
the
voice
alert
“Don't
Sink”
will
cease
and
the
yellow
caution
annunciator
lamp
will
extinguish. Note
It
is
important
for
the
pilot
not
to
over
-react
to
this
situation. While
it
is
important
to
react
quickly
and
positively
to
re-establish
a
positive
rate
of
climb,
the
pilot
should
remember
that
in
the
take-of
f/initial
climb
segment,
the
mar
gin
above
stall
speed
for
many
aircraft
is
fairly
small,
and
thus
must
be
respected. 20.6.1.3.7
Altitude
Callout
The
EGPWS
provides
an
altitude
callout
for
the
pilot,
to
indicate
a
position
approximately
500
feet
above
the
elevation
of
the
runway
being
approached. When
the
aircraft
is
within
5
nm
of
a
known
runway
,
the
altitude
callout
function
is
active,
and
is
then
triggered
when
Geometric
Altitude
shows
the
aircraft
500
feet
above
the
known
runway
elevation
(from
the
system
database). Example:
“Five-Hundred”. This
callout
is
not
accompanied
by
any
annunciator
lamp
indications
and
will
occur
only
once
per
approach. The
Altitude
Callout
is
reset
when
the
aircraft
climbs
more
than
700
feet
above
the
runway
elevation. 20.6.1.3.8

Symbol

plus

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: warning
chunks.text:
Geometric
Altitude
is
the
height
above
mean
sea
level
(MSL)
derived
from
the
GPS
receiver
,
filtered
by
the
vertical
figure
of
merits
from
the
same
GPS
and
complemented
by
short
term
variations
in
barometric
altitude. It
represents
the
aircraft's
calculated
true
height
above
MSL
and
serves
as
the
reference
altitude
for
color
-coding
of
the
terrain
display
and
the
altitude
input
to
the
look-ahead
algorithm. Because
it
is
primarily
comprised
of
GPS
altitude,
this
reference
altitude
will
often
dif
fer
from
cockpit
displayed
corrected
barometric
altitude. The
geometric
altitude
is
not
to
be
used
for
navigation. 8. Icon
Bar
—
Icons
representing
data
available
(black)
and
displayed
(color). 20.6.1.3.9
Limitations
1. Navigation
must
not
be
predicated
upon
the
use
of
the
T
errain
A
wareness
Display
. The
T
errain
A
wareness
Display
is
intended
to

a
situational
awareness
tool
only
,

not

the
accuracy
and/or
fidelity
on
which
to
solely
base
terrain
or
obstacle
avoidance
maneuvering
decisions. 2. The
EGPWS
must
have
an
operating
source
of
GPS
information,
with
enough
satellites
in
view
to
provide
GPS
data
within
the
accuracy
requirements
of
the
system. 3. W
ithout
the
optional
Outside
Air
T
emperature
(OA
T)
input
for
corrections,
“Geometric
Altitude”
may
have
errors
during
rapid
climbs
or
descents
in
non-ISA
conditions.

A

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Procedures
chunks.text:
b. Flight
Procedures
1. While
airborne,
other
display
pages
of
the
MFD
can
be
selected
with
confidence
since
the
EGPWS
will
always
be
running
in
the
background
as
indicated
by
the
gray
terrain
icon. If
the
EGPWS
issues
a
warning
or
caution,
a
pop
up
message
will
appear
and
the
display
will
automatically
change
to
the
T
errain
Page. Never
use
the
terrain
displayed
on
the
MFD
as
the
sole
reference
for
terrain
avoidance. 20.6.1.4
Map
T
opographic
and
Urban
Areas
Display
W
ith
power
applied
to
the
system
and
GPS
information
available,
selecting
the
MAP
function
on
the
MFD
will
activate
the
map
display
at
a
range
of
80
nm
around
the
aircraft
position,
Figure
20.6-4
. This
display
can
be
changed
from
T
OPO
ON
to
T
OPO
OFF
by
depressing
the
MAP
function
key
. In
the
T
OPO
ON
mode,
the
land
is
shaded
to
show
rising
ground
in
seven
elevations
levels
similar
to
those
seen
on
a
paper
chart. Built
up
or
urban
areas
are
shaded
light
gray
and
oceans
lakes
and
rivers
are
shown
in
blue. In
T
OPO
OFF
mode,
all
cartographic
data
is
automatically
removed. Both
modes
can
also
show
selectable
information
form
the
Jeppesen
Nav
Data
such
as
NA
V
AIDS
and/or
airports. In
the
upper
right
corner
of
the
MFD
is
a
joystick
that
can
be
used
to
move
a
pointer
around
the
display
. As
the
pointer
is
moved,
bearing
and
distance
information
from
the
aircraft
position
to
the
pointer
will
be
constantly
updated.

MAP
Setup
Pressing
the
“AUX”
Function
Select
button
on
the
MFD

the
“Auxiliary
mode
cover
page.”
Pressing
the
Power
Key
next
to
the
MAP
SETUP
Soft
Label
will
provide
access
to
the
four
pages
containing
map
setup
options.
On
any
of
the
four
pages,
use
the
joystick
to
change
the
settings
to
the
desired
values/parameters.
Use
the
RESET
V
ALUES
Power
Key
to
set
values
to
the
defaults.
Use
the
NEXT
Power
Key
to
cycle
between
the
pages.
When
finished,
use
the
SA
VE
&
EXIT
Power
Key
to
save
changes
and
return
to
the
“Auxiliary
mode
cover
page.”
20.6.2
W
eather
Radar
The
installed
T
urbulence
W
eather
Radar
(TWR)
System
is
a
2-channel,
solid-state,
X-band
W
eather
Radar
that
detects
precipitation-related
tar
gets
and
precipitation-related
turbulence
tar
gets
in
up
to
a
300
nautical
mile
range.
The
TWR
has
a
T
ar
get
feature
that
searches
for
heavy
rainfall
returns,
and
provides
an
alert
to
the
pilot
when
the
W
eather
Radar
overlay
is
not
in
view
.
The
TWR
also
has
a
map
mode
that
can
be
used
to
provide
terrain
information.
Both
the
weather
and
map
information
can
be
overlaid
on
either
or
both
PFDs
in
the
arc
or
PPOS
display
formats.
The
PFD(s)
contain
the
RDR
line
select
key
to
select
the
RDR
display
(on
or
of
f).
The
DCP(s)
provide
the
RDR
mode

Range
select

T

Suppression
(GCS). W
ith

PFDs
operational,

Radar
functions

Radars. Each

display
is

by
the
onside
DCP/PFD

of
the
antenna. 20.6.2.1
W
eather
System
Components
The
system
is
made
up
of
a
receiver/transmitter/antenna
(R
T
A),
two
PFDs
and
two
DCPs. Magnetic
heading
information
is
supplied
by
the
AHRS
and
data
collection
and
distribution
is
handled
by
the
IAPS. The
WTR
receives
power
from
the
A
vionics
Bus
No. 3
and
is
protected
by
a
7.5-ampere
circuit
breaker
located
on
the
copilot
outboard
subpanel
and
labeled
WX
RADAR. 20.6.2.2
W
eather
Radar
Modes
20.6.2.2.1
Standby
Mode
Selecting
STBY
on
either
side
causes
both
sides
to
go
to
STBY
. When
both
sides
are
in
STBY
and
one
side
selects
an
active
operational
mode
(TEST
,
WX,
WX+T
,
or
MAP),
both
sides
go
to
that
operational
mode
(i.e.,
if
STBY
is
active
and
the
copilot
selects
WX,
WX
becomes
the
current
radar
mode
on
both
sides). Independent
radar
modes
may
then
be
selected
by
each
pilot,
but
either
pilot
selecting
STBY
again
forces
both
sides
to
STBY
. At
system
power
-up
on
the
ground,
the
radar
powers
up
in
standby
mode. Standby
mode
is
automatically
selected
60
seconds
after
air/ground
transition
to
ground.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
20.6.2.2.2
Self-T
est
Mode
A
test
pattern
made
up
of
six
colored
arcs
in
a
rainbow-like
pattern
show
on
the
display(s)
when
test
mode
is
active. The
rainbow-like
pattern
is
made
up
of
the
colors
green,
yellow
,
red,
and
magenta. The
fifth
arc
changes
between
red
and
magenta
on
alternate
scans. Normal
radar
returns
do
not
show
when
test
mode
is
active. The
weather
radar
transmitter
is
not
active
in
test
mode. T
o
exit
from
the
test
mode,
another
mode
must
be
selected. 20.6.2.2.3
Map
Mode
MAP
mode
provides
the
most
detailed
ground
returns
and
should
be
used
while
purposely
mapping
the
terrain. In
MAP
mode,
signal
processing
and
tar
get
display
colors
are
changed
to
accentuate
ground
features. Ground
tar
gets
show
in
cyan,
green,
yellow
,
and
magenta
(least
reflective
to
most
reflective). MAP
mode
is
most
ef
fective
when
one
of
the
shorter
ranges
is
selected
and
the
antenna
is
tilted
down. 20.6.2.2.4
W
eather
Mode
Selecting
WX
mode
puts
the
radar
in
basic
weather
detection
mode. Detectable
precipitation
shows
as
one
of
four
colors:
green,
yellow
,
red,
or
magenta
(least
to
greatest
precipitation
rate). The
highest
precipitation
rates
show
in
magenta. 20.6.2.2.5
W
eather
+
T
urbulence
Mode
Detectable
precipitation
shows
as
one
of
four
colors:
green,
yellow
,
red,
or
magenta
(least
to
greatest
precipitation
rate). The
highest
precipitation
rates
and
turbulence
show
in
magenta.

chunks.text:
WX+T
mode
is
only
active
out
to
50
nm. When
a
display
range
greater
than
50
nm
is
selected,
WX
only
mode
is
automatically
selected. 20.6.2.2.6
T
urbulence
Only
Mode
By
removing
the
precipitation
tar
gets
from
the
display
,
the
areas
of

can
be
observed
alone. Like
the
WX+T
mode,
TURB
mode

only
active
out
to
50
nm. 20.6.2.2.7

arget

When

get
mode

the

searches
the
returns
from
a
±15°
sector
in

within

of
7
to

nm. When
a

get
is

between
7
and

nm,
a
yellow
TGT
message
shows
on
the
PFD. When
turbulence
is
detected
between
7
and
50
nm,
a
yellow
TRB
message
shows
on
the
PFD. As
soon
as
radar
is
selected

for
display
on

get
function

the

is

mode. 20.6.2.3
Antenna
Stabilization
Antenna
stabilization
(ST
AB)
is
used
to
eliminate
the
ef
fects
of
aircraft
pitch
and
roll
to
maintain
a
horizontal
scan. ST
AB
is
normally
on. Setting
ST
AB
to
of
f
is
primarily
useful
in
the
event
of
an
attitude
signal
failure. ST
AB
uses
inputs
from
the
attitude
heading
system
to
eliminate
the
ef
fects
of
aircraft
pitch
and
roll
to
maintain
the
selected
horizontal
scan. Setting
ST
AB
to
of
f
disconnects
the
inputs
from
the
attitude
heading
system. 20.6.2.4
Receiver
Gain
A
variety
of
gain
settings
allow
the
crew
to
increase
or
decrease
the
receiver
gain
to
analyze
radar
returns. Normally
,
receiver
gain
should
be
at
the
0
(NORM)
position. Six
other
positions,
including
three
of
increased
gain
for
the
utmost
in
sensitivity
,
are
available
for
analyzing
the
details
of
the
returns. The
higher
settings
are
useful
in
identifying
the
lightest
levels
of
precipitation
while
the
lower
settings
are
useful
for
more
in-depth
studies
of
the
most
intense
weather
tar
gets. Each
of
the
minus
settings
(–1,
–2,
and
–3)
reduces
the
sensitivity
of
the
radar
system
by
approximately
one
color
level.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
This
results
in
a
total
reduction
of
three
color
levels
when
operating
with
the
GAIN
set
to
the
–3
position. Therefore
if
a
tar
get
was
magenta
in
NORM,
the
same
tar
get
at
–3
will
be
almost
totally
removed
from
the
display
. Because
of
this
ef
fect,
the
weather
radar
system
should
not
be
operated
in
one
of
the
reduced
gain
settings
for
an
extended
period
of
time. 20.6.2.5
Sector
Scan
Sector
Scan
(SEC
SCAN)
reduces
the
antenna
scan
angle
from
the
normal
±60°
sweep
(120°
total)
to
a
±30°
sweep
(60°
total). By
reducing
the
antenna
scan
angle,
the
amount
of
time
needed
for
the
antenna
to
complete
its
sweep
is
also
reduced. This
ef
fectively
increases
the
weather
radar
update
rate. Selecting
sector
scan
on
one
display
will
not
select
it
on
the
of
f
side
display
. 20.6.2.6
Antenna
T
ilt
Operation
of
the
antenna
tilt
allows
the
operator
to
achieve
the
best
picture
of
storm
cell
size,
height,
and
relative
direction
of
movement. The
tilt
range
is
±15°. The
TIL
T
knob
is
functional
when
autotilt
is
active,
which
enables
the
pilot
to
manually
change
the
tilt/range
ratio
being
maintained. Proper
use
of
antenna
tilt
can
be
achieved
through
experience
and
regular
practice. T
ilt
can
be
adjusted
when
the
RADAR
menu
is
active,
or
any
time
the
radar
overlay
shows
on
the
onside
display
.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Figure
chunks.text:
20.6.2.7
Autotilt
Autotilt
automatically
adjusts
the
antenna
tilt
angle
to
attempt
to
keep
the
radar
in
the
same
region
of
space
when
the
aircraft
climbs
and
descends. Autotilt
is
designed
to
reduce
the
number
of
times
needed
to
adjust
the
TIL
T
control
whenever
the
aircraft
altitude
or
the
radar
range
setting
changes. Autotilt
may
be
selected
on
or
of
f
when
the
RADAR
menu
is
active,
or
anytime
the
radar
tilt
value
is
shown
on
the
onside
display
. 20.6.2.8
Ground
Clutter
Suppression
(GCS)
Ground
clutter
suppression
(GCS)
reduces
the
intensity
of
ground
returns
in
WX,
WX+T
,
and
TURB
modes,
which
assists
in
the
interpretation
of
rainfall
rates. Ground
clutter
suppression
reduces
the
intensity
of
ground
returns
in
WX,
WX+T
,
and
TURB
modes. Ground
clutter
suppression
removes
some
very
light
rainfall,
especially
rainfall
that
is
not
associated
with
convective
cells. This
type
of
unagitated
rain
(rain
with
no
associated
turbulence)
appears
ground-like
to
the
Doppler
processing. 20.6.2.9
Display
Functions
The
DCP
,
in
conjunction
with
the
RDR
mode
menu
on
the
PFD,
provides
control
of
the
W
eather
Radar
. The
weather
information
can
be
overlaid
on
either
or
both
PFDs
(
Figure
20.6-5
). The
DCP(s)
provide
mode
selection,
range
selection,
tilt
angle,
and
Ground
Clutter
Suppression
(GCS). W
ith
two
PFDs
operational,
the
W
eather
Radar
functions
like
two
independent
radars.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Procedures
chunks.text:
20.6.2.1
1.2
Flight
Procedures
During
flight
operations
each
pilot,
utilizing
the
controls
on
their
DCP
or
PFD,
can
individually
select
any
of
the
available
radar
modes
for
display
on
their
PFD
or
adjust
the
radar
tilt
and
gain. Figure
20.6-5
provides
an
example
of
a
radar
display
on
the
PFD. 20.6.3
Electronic
Standby
Instrument
System
(ESIS)
20.6.3.1
Description
ESIS
provides
a
visual
display
of
attitude
(pitch
and
roll),
and
slip/skid
information
computed
from
an
internal
three-axis
inertial
sensor
cluster
. The
ESIS
includes
an
internal
ADC
that
connects
to
the
aircraft
pitot-static
system
and
provides
baro
corrected
altitude,
air
speed,
Mach
number
,
and
vertical
speed
information. An
input
from
the
NA
V
1
provides
approach
information. The
system
is
not
configured
to
provide
heading
information. All
standby
information
is
displayed
on
a
3
inch
color
Active
Matrix
Liquid
Crystal
Display
(AMLCD). A
bezel
mounted
light
sensor
provides
automatic
display
dimming
capability
,
with
manual
of
fset
control
achieved
through
the
menu
mode. The
ESIS
has
its
own
battery
which,
under
normal
conditions
is
char
ged
by
the
aircraft
electrical
system. In
the
event
of
an
emer
gency
,
this
battery
can
be
completely
separate
from
the
aircraft
electrical
system
and
be
utilized
to
power
the
components
on
the
A
vionics
Essential
Bus
for
a
minimum
of
thirty
minutes.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: Procedures
chunks.text:
20.6.3.1.1
ESIS
Display
The
ESIS
is
a
self-contained
solid
state
instrument
that
has
a
3
inch
color
AMLCD
which
is
similar
to
the
PFD. Interfaced
with
the
aircraft
pitot
static
system,
the
ESIS
uses
its
internal
Air
Data
Computer
(ADC)
and
three-axis
inertial
sensor
cluster
to
provide
all
of
the
required
flight
information
with
the
exception
of
heading. Under
normal
conditions,
the
ESIS
display
receives
power
from
the
A
vionics
Essential
Bus
and
is
protected
by
a
3-ampere
circuit
breaker
located
on
the
copilot
outboard
subpanel
and
labeled
ESIS. The
instrument
has
a
bezel
mounted
light
sensor
that
provides
for
automatic
dimming
of
the
display
under
varying
light
conditions. Manual
control
is
also
available. Depressing
the
Menu
Access
Button
on
the
bottom
of
the
bezel
will
enable
the
flight
crew
to
toggle,
adjust
and
initiate
various
display
elements. The
display
is
located
in
the
top
middle
portion
of
the
instrument
panel. Figure
20.6-6
shows
a
typical
ESIS
display
. a. Screen
Features
—
Menu
Access
1. T
o
access
the
Menu,
press
the
Menu
Access
Button
located
below
the
Display
Screen. 2. A
menu
listing
will
appear
along
the
bottom
portion
of
the
Display
Screen
with
four
line
items
visible
at
a
time. Rotate
the
Adjustment
Knob,
located
below

The

(pilot
and

T
ransport
Provisions
The

go

provisions
for
five
passengers. However
,
the
T
-44C's

weight
and

as

in
each
aircraft's
DD
Form
365C
(known
as
Chart
C)
includes
only
the
weight
of
three
seats
at
stations
174,
184,
and
206. If
more
or
fewer
than
the
three
standard
seats
are
needed,
a
weight
and
moment
correction
factor
must
be
computed
and
entered
on
the

form
365F
(the
standard
weight
and

form). Each
seat
weighs
25

and,

removed,

for

and

will

seats
are
needed,
the
port
aft

will

(+25
lb
X
209
in

(–

21.2.2
Air
Cargo
21.2.2.1
Cabin
Provisions
The
passenger/car
go
compartment
may
be
readied
for
the
air
car
go
mission
(
Figure
21.2-2
)
by
removing
accommodations
which
are
specialized
to
other
missions
from
the
seat
tracks
and
walls
to
clear
a
storage
area. A
total
usable
floor
space
of
about
20
square
feet
is
thereby
readied
for
car
go
items
of
suitable
size
and
weight. This
storage
must
be
located
and
secured
to
the
seat
tracks
as
shown
by
Figure
21.3-1
. Usable
height
will
vary
from
about
40
inches
at
the
fuselage
walls
to
about
54
inches
at
the
aircraft
centerline. The
cabin
section
flooring
will
withstand
a
loading
of
200
pounds
per
square
foot
for
storage
supported
on
the
seat
tracks. Floor
areas
where
seat
tracks
are
not
present
(walkways
and
aft
baggage/utility
area)
will
only
support
100
pounds
per
square
foot
floor
loads. The
maximum
weight
allowable
in
the
aft
compartment
is
350
pounds
of
which
200
pounds
maximum
may
be
placed
on
the
floor
and
the
remaining
150
pounds
can
be
placed
on
the
shelf. 21.2-1

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CARGO
chunks.text:
21.3
CARGO
LOADING
The
pilot
in
command
should
ensure
proper
loading
of
the
aircraft
before
each
flight. At
the
time
of
delivery
,
the
aircraft
manufacturer
provides
the
necessary
weight
and
balance
data
to
compute
individual
loadings. All
subsequent
changes
in
aircraft
weight
and
balance
are
the
responsibility
of
the
Navy
. The
useful
load
weight
and
moment
tables
are
provided
to
compare
weight
and
balance
for
occupants,
baggage,
car
go,
and
usable
fuel
aircraft
loads. All
moments
are
divided
by
100
to
simplify
computation. Note
All
car
go
items
must
be
located
on
and
secured
to
the
seat
tracks. 21.3.1
Loading
Fundamentals
The
three
elements
of
proper
aircraft
loadings
are
weight,
balance,
and
restraint. Car
go
must
be
loaded
such
that
the
weight
of
the
car
go
is
within
the
carrying
capacity
of
the
aircraft,
the
balance
of
the
aircraft
is
within
the
operating
limits
specified,
and
the
car
go
is
prevented
from
shifting
during
flight. 21.3.1.1
W
eight
Each
aircraft
has
a
basic
weight. The
maximum
allowable
car
go
weight
may
be
determined
by
subtracting
the
basic
empty
weight,
the
crew
weight,
and
the
weight
of
fuel
required
for
the
mission
from
the
aircraft
maximum
gross
weight. The
weight
of
car
go
that
can
be
carried
also
depends
upon
the
location
of
car
go
within
the
aircraft
and
the
strength
of
seat
tracks
and
floor
structure.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CARGO
chunks.text:
21.3.1.2
Balance
Every
aircraft
has
a
point
about
which
it
will
balance. This
point
is
known
as
its
center
of
gravity
. Any
item
which
is
added
to
or
removed
from
the
aircraft
will
cause
a
change
in
the
balance
of
the
aircraft
and
cause
the
cg
of
the
aircraft
to
shift. If
the
cg
shifts
too
far
forward
or
too
far
aft,
the
aircraft
will
be
unsafe
in
flight. Car
go
must
be
loaded
so
that
the
cg
of
the
loaded
aircraft
is
within
the
range
specified. 21.3.1.3
Restraint
Car
go,
when
carried
in
an
aircraft,
is
subject
to
forces
resulting
from
flight
through
rough
air
,
acceleration,
rough
or
crash
landings,
etc. Unless
car
go
is
tied
down
or
restrained,
these
forces
will
cause
the
car
go
to
shift. Car
go
must
be
prevented
from
shifting
in
order
to
preserve
the
balance
of
the
aircraft
during
flight
and
prevent
injury
to
passengers
and
crew
by
moving
car
go. Unrestrained
car
go
may
shift
aft
during
normal
takeof
f
and
climb
resulting
in
a
tail-heavy
condition
which
could
lead
to
a
dangerous
takeof
f
stall. Careful
planning
shall
ensure
that
emer
gency
egress
routes
remain
intact
for
passengers
and
crew
. 21.3.2
Cargo
Load
Planning
Each
car
go
load
should
normally
be
planned
in
advance,
before
actually
being
loaded
into
the
aircraft. The
method
of
loading,
the
placement
of
car
go,
and
the
method
of
restraint
should
all
be
determined
in
advance.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CARGO
chunks.text:
The
following
rules
must
always
be
complied
with
when
planning
the
replacement
of
any
car
go
load:
1. Car
go
must
be
loaded
without
exceeding
the
strength
of
or
damaging
the
seat
tracks
or
floor
. 2. Car
go
weights
must
be
distributed
so
that
the
center
of
gravity
of
the
loaded
aircraft
is
within
the
limits
specified. 3. Car
go
must
be
arranged
to
permit
free
access
to
all
exits
and
emer
gency
exits. 4. The
car
go
must
be
arranged
to
permit
secure
tiedown
of
all
items. 21.3-1
1
SEPTEMBER
2023
CUI
NA
V
AIR
01-T44AAC-1
CUI

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CARGO
chunks.text:
5. Bulk
car
go
must
be
properly
arranged
to
prevent
damage
to
fragile
items. 6. Car
go
destination
should
be
considered
when
applicable. If
part
of
the
car
go
is
to
be
removed
at
an
intermediate
stop,
the
car
go
should
be
arranged
accordingly
. 21.3.2.1
Cargo
Placement
Planning
When
car
go
is
loaded
such
that
its
cg
falls
within
the
limits,
the
cg
of
the
loaded
aircraft
should
always
be
within
the
operating
limits
specified
in
the
NA
T
OPS
flight
manual. Prior
to
actual
loading,
the
location
of
each
individual
item
of
car
go
within
the
aircraft
should
be
planned
and
a
check
made
to
determine
if
the
arrangement
is
satisfactory
. The
planning
procedure
consists
of
two
steps. The
first
step
is
to
select
locations
for
items
within
the
aircraft
and
the
technique
by
which
the
cg
of
the
load
will
be
determined. If
the
cg
of
the
load
is
to
be
determined
by
the
balance
station
method,
specific
locations
must
be
assigned
to
each
item
of
car
go. The
second
step
is
to
determine
the
cg
of
the
car
go
load. The
station
method
should
be
used. Final
weight
and
balance
of
the
loaded
aircraft
must
always
be
determined
in
accordance
with
this
section. 21.3.2.2
Cargo
Center
of
Gravity
by
Stations
Car
go
center
of
gravity
by
stations
provides
a
method
of
computing
the
precise
center
of
gravity
of
a
load
and
should
be
used
when
the
load
consists
of
only
a
few
items
of
car
go.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CARGO
chunks.text:
In
order
to
use
this
method,
the
cg
of
each
item
of
car
go
must
be
known. The
cg
of
each
item
in
the
aircraft
will
coincide
with
a
fuselage
station
number
. The
cg
of
the
load
is
then
calculated
as
follows:
1. Set
up
a
table
and
record
the
weight
and
station
number
of
the
cg
of
each
item. 2. Calculate
the
moment
of
each
item. This
is
done
by
multiplying
the
weight
of
the
item
by
the
fuselage
station
number
of
its
cg. 3. Add
the
moments
to
obtain
the
total
load
moment. 4. Add
the
weights
to
obtain
the
total
load
weight. 5. Divide
the
total
load
moment
by
the
total
load
weight
to
obtain
the
cg
of
the
load. 21.3.3
Securing
Cargo
21.3.3.1
Load
Factors
Car
go
may
be
subjected
to
forces
resulting
from
flight
in
rough
air
,
acceleration,
rough
or
crash
landings,
etc. These
forces
will
act
more
strongly
in
some
directions
than
in
others
and
will
shift
the
car
go
unless
it
is
properly
restrained. Since
the
aircraft
and
car
go
both
move
forward
in
flight,
the
car
go
will
tend
to
keep
on
moving
if
the
aircraft
is
suddenly
slowed
or
stopped. This
forward
force
is
likely
to
be
the
strongest
force
that
will
act
on
the
car
go,
but
the
car
go
must
also
be
secured
against
the
forces
acting
on
it
laterally
,
vertically
,
or
in
an
aft
direction.

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CARGO
chunks.text:
The
amount
of
restraint
that
must
be
used
to
keep
the
car
go
from
moving
in
any
direction
equals
the
weight
of
the
item
times
the
load
factor
for
that
direction. Load
factors
are
expressed
in
units
of
gravity
or
“g”
forces. Following
are
the
load
factors
which
experience
has
shown
must
be
used
to
properly
secure
aircraft
car
go:
1. Forward
—
9.0
g's. 2. Up
—
3.0
g's. 3. Lateral
—
1.5
g's. 4. Down
—
5.0
g's. The
forward
load
factor
shown
above
means
that
car
go
must
be
restrained
from
forward
movement
by
a
force
equal
to
or
greater
than
nine
times
(9.0
g's)
its
normal
weight
to
be
adequately
secured. 1
SEPTEMBER
2023
21.3-2

chunks.title: A1 T44CA NFM 000
chunks.heading: CARGO
chunks.text:
21.3.3.2
Restraint
Devices
The
aircraft
is
equipped
with
full-length
seat
tracks
which
are
used
to
support
the
car
go
and
provide
attachment
points
for
the
car
go
tiedown
devices.
When
car
go
is
properly
secured
by
tiedown
devices,
it
will
be
restrained
from
moving
in
any
direction
within
the
aircraft.
21.3.3.3
Cargo
Restraining
Method
Car
go
is
restrained
by
passing
tiedown
devices
over
and
around
the
car
go
and
attaching
the
ends
of
the
tiedown
device
to
the
seat
tracks
as
shown
in
Figure
21.3-1
.
The
number
of
tiedown
devices
required
to
restrain
a
given
weight
of
car
go
may
vary
.
21.3.4
Passenger
Door
(
Figures
21.2-2
and
2.17-1
).
An
airstair
door
,
with
an
opening
26.75
inches
wide
by
51.5
inches
high,
is
provided
on
the
left
side
of
the
fuselage
for
crew
and
passenger
egress.
Refer
to
Chapter
2
for
detailed
coverage.
21.3.5
Loading
Procedure
The
loading
and
unloading
of
car
go
is
accomplished
through
the
main
cabin
entrance
(airstair)
door
.
Extreme
caution
should
be
exercised
to
prevent
damaging
the
wing
flaps,
doors,
floorboards,
seat
tracks,
upholstery
,
etc.
Personnel
should
observe
NO
STEP
areas.
After
completing
a
loading/unloading
operation,
check
the
aircraft
for
possible
danger
.
21.3-3
1
SEPTEMBER
2023

01-T44AAC-1
