 crew

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
Once on taxi centerline and after gaining taxi speed, slowly pull both “Clear right” (PM) 
 power levers into reverse and note symmetric acceleration on both “Clear left” (PF) 
 engine N1 gauges. Do not bring aircraft to a stop. Observing prop 
 RPM gauge indications does not satisfy this check. The intent is to “Needle left” (PF) 
 ensure symmetric “engine” spool-up not propeller spool-up. Utilize 
 minimum reverse required to verify proper operation and then Perform 3-way change 
 return power levers towards idle. Do not pull the powers levers to of controls. 
 max reverse to conduct this check. RS conducts brake check

Have the PM take the controls momentarily for a brake check. 
 Perform 3-way change 
 Direct the PM to “Continue the checklist.” Do not taxi between of controls. 
 runway/taxiway edge lighting while making final turn in the runup 
 areas. “Continue the 
 checklist” (PF) 
CAUTION

Minimize use of reverse at slow taxi speeds. Use of reverse range in 
 surface areas containing loose sand or small stones may cause 
 propeller blade erosion.

11. “TURN INDICATOR” Only one direction for turn needle is required. “CHECKED” (PF)

12. “BRAKES” “CHECKED” (PF) 
 “CHECKED” (PM)

“AFTER START CHECKLIST COMPLETE.” (PM)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-11 
"ENGINE RUN-UP CHECKLIST" (PF)

F104. ENGINE RUNUP CHECKLIST

The Engine Runup Checklist verifies operation of essential systems, which cannot be checked 
prior to start or at idle. The full Engine Runup Checklist is required on the initial takeoff of the 
day for any crew and when the pilot in command changes for subsequent flights during the day. 
During hot seat operations when engines are not secured and only the pilot in command changes, 
a full Engine Runup Checklist is not required. It is required on second or subsequent flights 
during any given day if significant maintenance was performed.

Pound sign (#) items constitute an abbreviated checklist that may be utilized by an aircrew 
making their second or subsequent takeoff during same day operations (except as noted in the 
previous paragraph). At a minimum, the abbreviated Engine Runup Checklist is required for 
every takeoff except when conducting touch-and-go, when executing a stop and go while 
remaining on the runway in use, or for an immediate taxi back to the approach end of the runway 
for takeoff when no engine has been secured in the interim.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
1. “PARKING BRAKE” LS ensures brake handle pulled out fully and brakes pumped firm. “SET” (LS) 
 PF will keep both feet on the brakes for the entire Runup Checklist. 
 PM will notify the PF if movement is detected and will immediately "Monitor for movement 
 depress the brakes if any movement is noted. during runup." (PF)

#2. “ESIS” Do not taxi until the ESIS has aligned. “ON” (PF) 
Note 
 Typical alignment time for the Standby Attitude Indicator is 
 approximately 2-3 minutes with a maximum expected alignment 
 time of 6 minutes.

#3. “ENGINE LS ensures both power levers are at IDLE and condition levers are at “CHECKED” (PF) 
 INSTRUMENTS” LOW IDLE. The main point of this check is to ensure N1 is at LOW 
 IDLE to commence Engine Runup if previously increased to support 
 a high generator load after starting engines.

WARNING

Whenever moving the condition levers to LOW IDLE, pinch the levers 
 together, and then move the levers very slowly. If a LOW IDLE stop is 
 weak, or a lever is moved rapidly, flameout may result. Should 
 flameout occur, do not move the condition lever back to LOW IDLE; 
 bring it fully into FUEL CUTOFF. The engine may be restarted using 
 the appropriate checklist.

NOTE

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
The indicator dial and pointer is the primary indication of each indicator. 
 As such and due to possible internal unit of measure limits, minor 
 differences between the dial and the digital indication may be seen.

LS checks all engine instruments for normal indications: 
 a. ITT – 685 ºC maximum 
 b. Torque – 100-200 lb.ft

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-12 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
 c. Prop RPM – 900-1100 RPM 
 d. N1 – 51-54% 
 e. Fuel flow – 100 lbs/hr (approximately) 
 f. Oil temp – -40-99 ºC 
 g. Oil press – 40-100 PSI

#4. “MANUAL FEATHER” LS performs the following checks: “CHECKED” (PF) 
 Power levers – IDLE, prop RPM stabilized. (Power must be at idle). 
 Pull prop levers aft through detent to FEATHER. Check that props 
will feather, then advance to FULL FORWARD. Prop rpm should 
return to 900 to 1,100 rpm.

NOTE

If propeller RPM does not read between 900 and 1,100 rpm with the 
 power levers at idle and the condition levers at low idle, perform a 
 Low Pitch Torque check (check flight item 5 in paragraph 10.6 of the 
 NATOPS) to ensure propeller flight idle stops are correctly adjusted.

5. “SUCTION AND LS ensures suction 4.3-5.9 inches Hg (5.1 ±0.8) and pneumatic “CHECKED” (LS) 
 PNEUMATIC PRESSURE” pressure 12-20 PSI (16 ±4). 
 a. Condition levers — HIGH IDLE 
 b. Verify N1 stabilizes between 70% - 73%

6. “OVERSPEED a. Prop levers – full forward “CHECKED” (LS) 
 GOVERNORS” b. Adjust power levers to ensure prop RPM less than 1900 RPM. 
 c. PROP GOV TEST switch – TEST (up position)

Maintain a firm grip on the switch. DO NOT release the switch while 
 the test is being performed. An abrupt surge in prop rpm may result.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
d. Slowly advance power levers until prop RPM stops increasing 
 (1900-2100 RPM). ITT and Torque should continue to increase 
 while prop RPM remains stabilized. A properly paced check 
 will not result in high torque. 
 e. Retract power levers to set 1900 prop RPM. 
 f. Release PROP GOV TEST switch.

7. “PRIMARY a. Check power levers to set 1900 prop RPM. “CHECKED” (LS) 
 GOVERNORS” b. Smoothly move both prop levers aft until reaching the feather 
 detent. This will be felt as increased resistance. Be careful not 
 to allow prop levers to enter into the FEATHER position. If 
 FEATHER is inadvertently selected, bring the props forward 
 immediately. 
 c. Ensure prop RPM stabilized between 1600-1800 RPM. 
 c. Slowly advance prop levers full forward. 
 d. Verify prop RPM returns to approximately 1900 RPM.

8. “AUTO-FEATHER/ LS performs the following checks: “Call my lights.” (PF) 
 AUTOIGNITION” a. Condition levers - LOW IDLE 
 b. Power levers – confirm greater than 460 lb.ft torque. 
 c. ENG AUTO IGN SWITCHES (2) – ON. Observe LH and RH 
 AUTO IGNITION ARMED annunciator lights (green) illuminated.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-13 
 d. AUTO-FEATHER switch – HOLD TO TEST (down position); 
 observe (2) green AUTO-FEATHER ARMED annunciator lights “Four lights” (PM) 
 illuminated. If lights do not come on, advance the power levers 
 slightly.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
e. Left power lever – Retract slowly. As torque reaches 410 ±50 “Swap” (PM) 
 lb.ft, check RH AUTO-FEATHER ARMED annunciator light “408” (PF) 
 out, LH AUTO IGNITION ARMED annunciator light out and (call out torque values for 
 LH IGN IND annunciator light (amber) illuminated. At 200 ±40 each checkpoint) (PF) 
 lb.ft check LH AUTO-FEATHER ARMED annunciator light 
 flashing. This indicates the prop is feathering. After prop RPM “Flashing” (PM) 
 decreases below 800 RPM, advance left power lever to 500 lb.ft “213” (PF) 
 torque (approximately). Note both the AUTO-FEATHER ARMED 
 annunciator lights and AUTO IGNITION ARMED lights are “All four” (PM) 
 illuminated (four green lights). f. Right power lever – Retract slowly. As torque reaches 410 ±50 “Swap” (PM) 
 lb.ft, check LH AUTO-FEATHER ARMED annunciator light “417” (PF) 
 out, RH AUTO IGNITION ARMED annunciator light out and (call out torque values for 
 RH IGN IND annunciator light illuminated. At 200 ±40 lb.ft each checkpoint) (PF) 
 check RH AUTO-FEATHER ARMED annunciator light flashing. “Flashing” (PM) 
 This indicates the prop is feathering. After prop RPM decreases “206” (PF) 
 Below 800 RPM, retract both power levers to idle. g. AUTO-FEATHER TEST switch – Release after noting right prop 
 feathering cycle terminated and right prop RPM increasing. h. ENG AUTO IGN switches (2) – Off.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
Ensure both IGN IND “CHECKED/OFF” 
 annunciator lights out. (LS)

“ENGINE RUNUP CHECKLIST COMPLETE.” (PM)

NOTE

After the Engine Runup Checklist is complete, the PF shall reset N1 
 via the condition levers as appropriate per NATOPS Figure 4.3-1.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-14 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
 "BEFORE TAKEOFF CHECKLIST" (PF)

F105. BEFORE TAKEOFF CHECKLIST

The Before Takeoff Checklist is designed to check the remaining systems and configure the 
aircraft for takeoff. The Before Takeoff Checklist is normally accomplished prior to taking the 
active runway for takeoff. Once cleared to "line up and wait" or for "takeoff," direct the PM to 
"continue" the Before Takeoff checklist. It is imperative that the crew does not begin their 
takeoff roll until cleared for takeoff by ATC. If taxiing back for another takeoff, complete the 
pound (#) items which constitute an abbreviated checklist that may be utilized by an aircrew 
making their second or subsequent takeoff during the same day. If the Secure checklist has been 
completed, a full Before Takeoff Checklist shall be completed.

When executing the abbreviated Before Takeoff Checklist, the correct response is “checked/set” 
for all applicable checklist items, with the understanding that not all items require a full check 
and response at this time. The “checked” portion of the response satisfies the requirement that 
the item was previously checked during the initial completion of the full Before Takeoff 
Checklist.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
1. “AUTOPILOT/YAW PF and PM perform the following checks: “CHECKED/ 
 DAMP” a. PF engages the autopilot and presses the AP/YD disconnect button DISENGAGED” (PF) 
 on the pilot’s control wheel. Observe that the autopilot disengages 
 and is accompanied by a warning horn and PFD indication. PF “CHECKED/ 
 then ensures flight controls operate freely. All that is required is DISENGAGED” (PM) 
 small movement of the control wheel and rudder pedals to ensure 
 there are no servos binding.

b. PF engages autopilot and directs the PM to press the AP/YD 
 disconnect button on the control wheel. Observe that the autopilot 
 disengages and is accompanied by a warning horn and PFD 
 indication. PM then ensures flight controls operate freely.

Verify AP is disengaged and select AP disconnect bar to the down 
 position. PF and PM press their respective FD button on the FGP 
 to remove command bars from PFD.

WARNING

Engagement of the yaw damp during takeoff or landing may result in 
 severe directional control problems.

CAUTION

Do not check the autopilot while taxiing. Do not attempt to move the 
 controls while the AP is engaged. Damage to the AP servos will result.

NOTE

Observe momentary flashing red AP text on top of PFD and 
 audible warning horn during the disconnect.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
2. “CONTROLS” Check as previously described, if not already checked. A second check “CHECKED” (PF) 
 is unnecessary. “CHECKED” (PM)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-15 
3. “PROPS” PF ensures props full forward. “FULL FORWARD” 
 (PF) 
4. “PROP SYNC” PF ensures Prop sync off. “OFF” (PF)

5. “AUTO-FEATHER” LS moves Auto-feather switch to armed. “ARMED” (LS)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
6. “ANTI-ICE/DEICE/ Full anti-ice/deice checks are practiced during cockpit procedures For pitot only check: 
 RADAR” trainers (CPTs) and must be memorized. “Pitot Heat 
 CHECKED” (PF) 
 If flight into icing conditions is anticipated and/or on the first leg 
 of a cross-country flight, full anti-ice/deice checks must be completed. “CHECKED” (PM) 
 Monitor load meter to ensure each system is operational and 
 minimum N1 is maintained for the load condition. When required, For full check respond: 
 conduct anti-ice/deice checks per NATOPS Chapter 19. If not “Give 30 second 
 required, PF and PM respond, “Pitot heat checked” since this was intervals” (PF) 
 previously checked during the Preflight Inspection NATOPS 7.2.1.2 
 “30 seconds” x4 (PM) 
Full Anti-Ice/Deice Check: 
 1. Propeller deicer — ON. (At DE-ICE CYCLE– 
 Commence timing. Assure PROP AMPS indicate 14 to 18 during SINGLE) 
 each of the 4 phases. The timer will switch phases approximately Wing de-ice boots: 
 every 30 seconds. Momentary needle deflection may be visible as “Inflate” (PF) 
 propeller heat cycles between phases. Secure the system after “Inflate” (PM) 
 amperage is checked during the fourth phase (2 minute minimum). Pneumatic pressure indicator: 
 Additional anti-ice and deice checks may be performed while the “Pressure” (PF) 
 propeller is being checked.

CAUTION

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
Monitor load meters during anti-icing equipment checks to prevent Tail de-ice boots: 
 Exceeding operating limits. Increase N1 if required in accordance “Inflate” (PF) 
 with Figure 4.3-1. “Inflate” (PM) 
 “Deflate” (PF) 
 2. Place the following anti-icing systems ON and monitor load for “Deflate” (PM) 
 proper indication: 
 a. LEFT & RIGHT PITOT — (checked during preflight) Gyro suction indicator: 
 b. Stall Heat — ON. “Suction” (PF) 
 c. LEFT & RIGHT ENG LIP BOOT — ON. − _No rise in Wing de-ice boots: 
 load should be evident during ground activation of engine lip “Deflate” (PF) 
 boots. A rise in load indicates a malfunction in squat switch “Deflate” (PM) 
 circuitry. (Repeat for MANUAL, 
 d. LEFT & RIGHT FUEL VENT — ON. releasing switch to deflate) 
 e. WSHLD ANTI-ICE — BOTH, Then PILOT. “CHECKED” (PF) 
 3. Upon completion of anti-ice checks, place all switches OFF. “CHECKED” (PM) 
 FUEL CONTROL HEAT remains ON. 4. Condition levers — HIGH IDLE. 5. DE-ICE CYCLE — SINGLE, Then MANUAL. - Check wing and tail boot automatically inflate, then deflate in 7 to 
 8 seconds when SINGLE is selected. - Check pneumatic pressure is 12 to 20 psi. Repeat the same check 
 in MANUAL, disregarding cycle time. 6. Condition levers — LOW IDLE. 7. Propeller deice — OFF (if not previously secured). Radar Test: Test the radar if utilization is anticipated in flight. a.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
Ensure both PFDs are in 120 format using R1 soft key. “Radar CHECKED/ 
 b. Ensure RLSK on PFD shows RDR as selected (Display is BLUE). STANDBY” (PF)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-16 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
 c. PF Selects Radar button on Display Control Panel (DCP), “Radar CHECKED/ 
 System should be in STBY. STANDBY” (PM) 
 d. Ensure STAB is selected ON. 
 e. Ensure GAIN is in NORM. 
 f. Ensure SECTOR SCAN is OFF. 
 g. Ensure TARGET is OFF. 
 h. Using the MENU ADVANCE knob, PF selects TST on 
 the display. 
 i. Observe test sweeps on both PFDs. Verify both patterns 
 display on each PFD (6-line test pattern and 5-line pattern). 
 Both seats verbalize “5” and “6”. 
 j. PF selects STBY using MENU ADVANCE knob.

7. “PRESSURIZATION” PM sets: “SET” (PM) 
 a. Rate control knob to midpoint of travel (approximately one 
 o’clock position). 
 b. Cabin controller to field elevation plus the correction factor per 
 NATOPS Figure 2.18-5.

Ensure Press mode selector switch is in "Press" for initial takeoff of the 
 day. Moving to "Dump" after takeoff is permitted, as long as the aircraft 
 is not pressurizing with the bleed air valves closed. If the aircraft 
 pressurizes, one of the bleed air valves is open and the flight should not 
 be continued.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
8. “BLEED AIR VALVES” RS sets bleed air valves as required. “OPEN” or “CLOSED” 
 (as required) (RS) 
#9. “TRIM” (normally 0, 0, 2 up) 
 PF and PM perform the following check: “CHECKED/SET” (PF) 
a. Split switch (on control wheel) – Individually move each half of “CHECKED/SET” (PM) 
split switch and note the elevator trim wheel does not move. Do 
not rush the check. 
b. Split switch – Move both halves of split switch and note smooth 
movement of the trim wheel. Perform check in both directions. 
While trim wheel is moving during the second half of the check, 
depress and hold the red AP/YD button (to the second detent) on 
the control wheel to disengage the electric trim. Note the trim 
wheel stops and a red TRIM annunciator illuminates on the PFD. 
Release the AP/YD button, and note trim re-engages before 
releasing the split switch. 
c. PF normally sets the following: 
Aileron – 0, Rudder – 0, Elevator – 2 up

Checking of electric trim is only required for the first takeoff of the 
 day. When utilizing the abbreviated before takeoff checklist, it is not 
 required to re-check electric trim.

NOTE

At high gross weights and aft center of gravity (cg), consideration 
 should be given to utilizing less nose-up trim for takeoff.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
#10. “FLAPS” Check as previously described, if not already checked. Visually “CHECKED/UP” (PF) 
 confirm the flap handle, flap position indicator, and flaps are all in “CHECKED/UP” (PM) 
 the UP position.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-17 
#11. “ALTIMETERS” PF and PM perform the following checks: “PFD set 30.12 reading 
 30 feet, ESIS set 30.12 
 a. Barometric Altimeter (BA): reading 30 feet. 
 Set the current altimeter setting on both PFDs and ESIS. RADALT checks.” 
 Ensure no comparators or warning flags visible on PFD. “CHECKED/SET” (PF) 
 Ensure the barometric altimeter indicates within ±75 feet 
 of airport elevation and compare LS, RS and ESIS altimeter 
 readings. See the airport diagram for specific field elevations. “Set 30.12, reading 
 b. Set BARO/RA minimums as required. 30 feet, RADALT checks” 
 c. Radar Altimeter (RA): 
 PF depress radio altimeter test button to verify proper indications. “CHECKED/SET” (PM) 
 Both altimeters should show a rise of 50 feet and the green RAD 
 ALT display should show 50 ±5 feet. 
d. Set and select V SPEEDS (VT-102, VR-91, VREF-110. Select VT, 
VR, and VREF for display.)

A typical response would be: (PF) “PFD set 30.12 reading 30 feet, 
 ESIS set 30.12 reading 30 feet. RADALT checks.” (PM) “Set 30.12, 
 reading 30 feet, RADALT checks” and reports any discrepancies.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
#12. “FMS” PF will tell PM to “Hold the checklist,” and contact clearance delivery “Hold the checklist” 
 (or Seagull Radar if necessary) in accordance with the Typical Briefs (PF) 
 and Voice Procedures Appendix to copy the IFR clearance if appropriate. “You have the controls. Before going heads down to load FMS data, the controls should be I will be heads down.” 
 passed to the PM. (PF) 
 Perform 3-way change 
 Typical response would be “You have the controls. I will be of controls. heads down.” 
 For IFR clearance, call 
 Using the IFR clearance information, or typical VFR configuration, clearance delivery. For 
 set up FMS for flight as follows: VFR, coordinate with 
a. Status – Verify FMS database is current and ensure UTC time Seagull on BTN 19 as 
and date correct. needed. (PM) 
b. Pos Init – MANDATORY, Use RSK 6 on STATUS page. Set FMS position to GPS position using RSK 4. Load Route or flight 
c. FPLN – For RI events, enter departure and destination airports plan as desired. (PM) 
on FPLN page. For Familiarization events, when loading a ROUTE for 
a working area, a full flight plan will load. There is no need to “Verify” (PM) 
enter a departure or destination when loading a ROUTE. Once the FPLN or ROUTE is loaded, verify the waypoints are correct 
on the LEGS page. Then call for the PF to verify the waypoints. Once PF verifies, Press EXEC button.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
Select LEGS and 
inhibit the waypoints from sequencing using RSK 1. In order to activate the fuel functions within the FMS, Select Check 120 Map mode, 
 PERF INIT (RSK 6) at the bottom right of the FPLN page. verify correct routing, 
d. Select the Manual Fuel entry (LSK 4) and enter current total “Execute” (PF) 
fuel load in lbs. (typical training load 2400 lbs.). e. Check RAIM if required – IDX button then GPS CTL. Enter 
destination airfield, ETA, and verify RAIM availability. Perform 3-way change 
of controls.

Refer to current CNAF M-3710.7 for predictive RAIM check 
 requirements. “CHECKED/SET” (PF) 
 “CHECKED/SET” (PM)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-18 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
#13. “FLIGHT PF and PM check flight instruments for proper indications: “CHECKED/SET” (PF) 
 INSTRUMENTS” 
a. Compare flight information on both Primary Flight Displays “CHECKED/SET” (PM) 
(PFDs) and the Electronic Standby Instrument System (ESIS). Note any comparators or warning flags. b. V/UHF radio: Check squelch on and verify function switch 
TR+G. Set desired display intensity by pressing ENT then 8 for dim or 9 
for bright. Select desired MAN or PRESET frequency. Set desired 
frequency. c. VHF radio: Set desired display intensity on RTU with rheostat 
knob. Depress EMER 121.5 button and ensure button is 
illuminated with frequency displayed in RTU active window. Reset the EMER 121.5 button to return RTU to normal 
functionality. Set appropriate frequency in preset and active. d. NAV1 / NAV2 / ADF: Set appropriate frequencies in preset and 
active using RTU. Tune and identify NAVAIDs. Repeat for 
NAV2 after depressing ½ hard key on RTU. Set appropriate 
frequency in ADF window on RTU. e. Set XPNDR in accordance with assigned squawk or SOPs. Set 
ADS-B call-sign on RTU ATC or FMS TUN page 2/2. f.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
TACAN: On TACAN control head, tune appropriate frequency, 
verify ON/OFF switch is ON, A/A or A/G switch is in A/G 
position, and X/Y band selector switch is flipped to appropriate 
band. g. PFD: LS and RS select desired NAV sources for CDI, PRESET, 
bearing source 1 and bearing source 2 on PFDs. Set CDI source as 
appropriate. Set desired PFD display format. h. FGP: set departure speed using SPEED knob, desired altitude 
using ALT select knob, and heading as desired using HDG knob. Set ESIS course as appropriate.

NOTE

V1, VR, and V2 are automatically removed from the airspeed tape 
 at 150 KIAS

#14. “FUEL QUANTITY” Compare actual fuel load with preflight fuel calculations requirements “__lbs, __lbs required” 
 in accordance with SOP and CNAF M-3710.7 minima. (PF)

NOTE

With full fuel tanks left and right, quantity indicators with TOTAL 
 selected should indicate within 100 pounds of each other. With 
 NACELLE selected, the maximum difference should be 50 pounds. 
 In either case, quantity shall be outside the yellow arcs and the 
 discrepancy must be noted on the appropriate maintenance form for 
 correction prior to the next flight.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
#15. “CREW” Brief the PM in accordance with the Typical Briefs and Voice “Back me up on the 
 Procedures Appendix, as appropriate. At a minimum, brief the PM power quadrant and 
 On the takeoff, instrument departure procedures, and intended type monitor all engine and 
 of recovery if an emergency is encountered after takeoff. flight instruments. Call 
 out any malfunctions.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-19 
 May include any other information deemed helpful for the flight, All malfunctions will be 
 particularly TOLD data. Other items may include: handled in accordance 
 with NATOPS. Call 
 N– NOTAMs/Notes rotate at 91 knots and 
 O– Obstacles note the time of takeoff. 
 T– Terrain/Trouble Ts 
 A– Airfield/Airspace This will be a course 
 M– Mins/eMergencies rules departure to 
 S– Speeds/TOLD (working area). In the 
 WX– Weather/BASH event of an emergency 
 return, I’ll maintain 
 VMC, and request 
 downwind.” 
 -Brief TOLD 
 “Any questions?” (PF)

“Briefed” (PF)

Check nacelle fuel quantities to ensure it is “out of the yellow” “Hold the checklist. 
 cautionary range. Check me out of the 
 yellow. Request further 
 taxi.” (PF)

“You’re out of the yellow.” 
 (PM) 
 PM Calls ground for further taxi. “Navy Corpus Ground, 
 PF repeats clearance internally. (call-sign), request further 
 taxi.” (PM) 
 Ground delivers clearance, 
 PM repeats clearance to 
 ATC. 
 PF confirms and 
 announces clearance to the 
 crew

“Clear left.” (PF) 
 “Clear right.” (PM)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
Taxi to the Hold Short: Approaching the hold-short, switch to channel 4 and request takeoff “Navy Corpus Tower, 
 clearance. If there is sufficient time prior to being cleared onto the (call-sign), runway ##, 
 runway, the PF is encouraged to check total and nacelle fuel quantities #1(or #2), holding short, 
 prior to takeoff clearance to minimize takeoff delays once cleared. VFR to (location). Ready 
 for departure” (PM) 
 PF repeats clearance internally. 
 Tower gives takeoff clearance.

After cleared to line up and wait or for takeoff: “(call-sign), cleared for 
 Visually clear the downwind, base, final, and the runway for traffic. takeoff, runway ##” (PM) 
 Then direct the PM to “continue” the Before Takeoff Checklist PF confirms and 
 while crossing the hold short line and taxiing into position for announces clearance to the 
 takeoff. crew.

“Clear left and above” 
 (PF)

“Clear right and above” 
 (PM) 
 “Continue the checklist.” 
 (PF)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-20 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
 After cleared onto the active runway:

#16. “LIGHTS” LS places the following switches on: “SET” (LS) 
 a. LEFT and RIGHT LANDING 
 b. TAXI 
 c. NAV (night or when visibility is less than 3 statute miles) 
 d. ICE (night only) 
 e. BEACON (already on) 
 f. STROBE (before taking the active runway for departure)

#17. “ANTI-ICE/DEICE” LS turns on anti-ice/deice equipment per NATOPS Chapter 19. “SET” (LS) 
 Normally, only the left and right pitot heat is necessary.

CAUTION

Pitot heat should not be left ON for extended ground operations, as 
 prolonged ground operation without cooling airflow may damage the 
 heating element. 
#18. “TRANSPONDER” RS switches XPDR switch below RTU from STBY to ON. “SET” (RS)

#19. “AUTOIGNITION” PM sets both RH and LH ENG AUTO IGN switches to ARM “ARMED” (PM)

#20. “CONDITION LEVERS” PF places both condition levers full forward and verifies 70- “HIGH IDLE” (PF) 
 73% N1 with power levers at idle. 
 “BEFORE TAKEOFF CHECKLIST COMPLETE.” (PM)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
When lined up and Check left wing/nacelle for fuel caps in place, panels secure, “Checked left” (LS) 
 ready for takeoff: and no fluid leaks. Check PFD and ESIS headings aligned 
 with the runway, no abnormal flags, and engine instruments/props “Checked right” (RS) 
 stabilized. Confirm there are no malfunctions and ready for takeoff, 
 report “checked left.” The RS will do the same for the right 
 wing/instruments and report “checked right.” Release the brakes 
 and place both feet on the lower portion of the rudder pedals. 
 Commence takeoff roll per NATOPS.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
After power is stabilized above 70-percent N1, smoothly apply maximum allowable power. Monitor ITT and 
torque. At rotation speed (91 KIAS), set takeoff attitude by raising the nose 7 to 10° noseup pitch. Check the 
Vertical Speed Indicator (VSI) and altimeter for a positive rate of climb, then call "landing gear up.” The pilot 
monitoring should confirm a positive rate of climb and call "landing gear up" before the RS pilot selects the landing 
gear handle to the up position. Once the landing gear indicates up, the PM will announce “landing gear up” and PF 
will retard prop levers simultaneously to 1900 rpm. Accelerate to an appropriate enroute climb speed. Maximum 
allowable power must be applied prior to releasing brakes to obtain the normal takeoff distances in Part XI. 
Crosscheck climb information on ESIS with that displayed on both PFDs.

CAUTION 
 Rapid advancement of the power levers may result in propeller surging 
 and asymmetric power application which could result in directional 
 control problems.

“CLIMB CHECKLIST” (PF) 
or 
“ABBREVIATED CLIMB CHECKLIST” (PF)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-21 
F106. CLIMB CHECKLIST 
The Climb Checklist is designed to check and set systems after takeoff, when safely established 
in a climb. PF calls for the appropriate Climb Checklist above 1000 feet AGL when cockpit 
duties permit. The abbreviated checklist noted by a pound sign (#) may be utilized when doing 
multiple approaches in the same general area. If PF calls “Abbreviated Climb Checklist,” PM 
completes items 1 through 4. If PF calls “Climb Checklist,” PM completes the entire checklist. 
Neither checklist is required if entering directly into the home field bounce pattern or proceeding 
directly to and from Cabaniss or CRP via the NUECES transition.

#1. “LANDING GEAR” PM ensures landing gear handle up, light in landing gear handle “UP” (PM) 
 extinguished, and three green lights out.

#2. “FLAPS” PM ensures flap handle UP and flap gauge indicating UP. A “UP” (PM) 
 visual check of flap position is not required unless experiencing 
 a malfunction.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
#3. “INSTRUMENTS/ LS looks outside and checks the left wing and nacelle for fluid “CHECKED” (PF) 
 NACELLES” leaks, loose panels, and any other discrepancies. LS then checks “CHECKED” (PM) 
 engine and flight instruments, and fuel quantities (return quantity 
 switch to TOTAL). RS also looks outside and checks the right wing 
 and nacelle for fluid leaks, loose panels, and any other discrepancies. 
 RS then checks engine and flight instruments for discrepancies. If 
 there are no discrepancies, the appropriate checklist response is 
 “checked” for both pilots.

Utilize ice lights for wing checks at night.

#4. “LIGHTS” LS turns landing, taxi, and ice lights OFF. “SET” (LS)

(PM reports “Abbreviated Climb Checklist complete” or continues with full checklist.)

5. “AUTO-FEATHER” LS selects AUTO-FEATHER switch OFF, unless low altitude “OFF” (LS) 
 operations requiring auto-feather are planned. (as required)

6. “PROP SYNC” PF sets 1900 RPM on left propeller RPM, then fine-tunes the right “ON” (PF) 
 propeller to minimize audible modulation, then selects prop sync ON (as required) 
 if desired. Prop sync is not typically used for high-work.

7. “PRESSURIZATION” Once airborne, pressurization setting is at the discretion of the PIC. “CHECKED” (PM)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
Pressurized, PM checks: 
 - Depending on ambient altitude and system efficiency, the 
 differential (short needle) should increase from zero as the cabin 
 pressurizes. The long needle will indicate cabin altitude. If desired 
 cabin pressure indications are not correct, call for the Loss of 
 Pressurization checklist.

Unpressurized, PM checks: 
 - Differential (short needle) should remain at zero while the cabin 
 altitude climbs with the aircraft (long needle). Cabin altitude should 
 indicate approximately the same as the barometric altimeter. The cabin 
 rate of climb should indicate the approximate aircraft rate of climb. The 
 PRESS switch may now be moved to DUMP at PIC discretion. 
“CLIMB CHECKLIST COMPLETE” (PM)

“CRUISE CHECKLIST” (PF)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-22 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
F107. CRUISE CHECKLIST

Perform the Cruise Checklist if operating above 10,000 feet or if the leg is planned to exceed one 
hour. Initiate after level-off at cruise altitude. Generally, cruise power requirements are 
dependent upon the prevailing circumstances, length, and type of mission being flown. Refer to 
the cruise charts in Chapter 28 of the NATOPS Manual for flight planning.

Fuel logs should be kept hourly during extended cruise operations.

1. “INSTRUMENTS/ LS looks outside and checks the left wing and nacelle for fluid “CHECKED” (PF) 
 NACELLES” leaks, loose panels, and any other discrepancies. LS then checks “CHECKED” (PM) 
 engine and flight instruments, and fuel quantities (return quantity 
 switch to TOTAL). RS also looks outside and checks the right wing 
 and nacelle for fluid leaks, loose panels, and any other discrepancies. 
 RS then checks engine and flight instruments for discrepancies. If 
 there are no discrepancies, the appropriate checklist response is 
 “checked” for both pilots.

Utilize ice lights for wing checks at night.

2. “ALTIMETERS” If below FL 180, ensure current altimeter setting (within 100 NM) is “SET” (PF) 
 set. If FL 180 or above, set STD by depressing the BARO knob center. “SET” (PM)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
3. “PRESSURIZATION/ PM checks cabin altitude and differential. Verify proper indications “CHECKED” (PM) 
 OXYGEN” and cabin altitude no higher than 10,000 feet. Ensure oxygen pressure 
 within limits (1,000 psi local, 1,500 psi cross country).

“CRUISE CHECKLIST COMPLETE” (PM)

“DESCENT CHECKLIST” (PF)

F108. DESCENT CHECKLIST

The Descent Checklist must be completed anytime the Cruise Checklist has been completed. 
Commence the Descent Checklist upon descending through FL 180 or upon initial descent from 
cruise altitude if lower.

Descent from cruising altitude may be made at any airspeed and power combination within the 
aircraft operating envelope. Maximum rate of descent may be obtained in either the clean or dirty 
configuration. However, the descent gradient of a dirty descent is considerably greater than that 
of a clean descent at a given power setting.

Note 
 Approximately 75 percent N1 (single engine 85 percent N1) is required 
 to maintain the pressurization schedule during descent.

1. “ALTIMETERS” Set regional altimeter setting (within 100 NM). If still above FL 180, “___.___ SET” (PF)
 you may leave STD set until descending through FL 180, then set “___.___ SET” (PM)
 regional altimeter. If proceeding to an uncontrolled field, set the ASOS 
 altimeter setting within 25 NM of the field.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
#2. “PRESSURIZATION” PM sets pressurization controller to the destination elevation plus 500 “SET” (PM) 
 feet (corrected for reported altimeter setting). Refer to NATOPS 
 Figure 2.18-5.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-23 
“DESCENT CHECKLIST COMPLETE” (PM)

“APPROACH CHECKLIST” (PF)

Or

“ABBREVIATED APPROACH CHECKLIST.” (PF)

F109. APPROACH CHECKLIST

The Approach Checklist should be completed prior to landing except in the touch-and-go pattern. 
The abbreviated checklist noted by the pound sign (#) may be utilized when doing multiple 
approaches in the same general area and is intended only to be utilized in conjunction with the 
abbreviated Climb Checklist. If a full Climb Checklist is executed, then a full Approach 
Checklist is required. If the PF calls for the “Approach Checklist,” PM commences the 
checklist at step 1 and completes the entire checklist. If the PF calls for the “Abbreviated 
Approach Checklist,” PM completes Steps 4 through 9.

1. “PRESSURIZATION” PM sets pressurization controller to the destination field elevation plus “SET” (PM) 
 500 feet (corrected for reported altimeter setting), or verifies the 
 correct setting if previously performed. Refer to NATOPS Figure 
 2-18-5.

2. “AUTO-FEATHER” LS selects AUTO-FEATHER switch to ARMED. “ARMED” (LS)

3. “PROP SYNC” PF selects prop sync OFF. “OFF” (PF)

(PM commences Abbreviated Approach Checklist at Step 4 if appropriate.)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
#4. “FMS” PF directs PM to set appropriate destination and airways in the active “SET” (PF) 
 FPLN page. PM also loads the current approach for the destination “SET” (PM) 
 Airport.

#5. “RADIOS/NAVAIDs” PF directs PM to set all applicable radios and NAVAIDs as required “SET” (PF) 
 for arrival and approach. Utilize the RTU, CDU TUNE page, and “SET” (PM) 
 TACAN to ensure all applicable NAVAIDs are set.

#6. “PFDs/FGP/ESIS” a. Set PFD display as follows: PF and PM set primary nav source, “SET” (PF) 
 preset nav source, #1 and #2 bearing pointers as required for “SET” (PM) 
 the approach. 
 b. Set FGP as follows: PF sets Course in the left CDI and 
 directs the PM to set HDG, ALT, and Speed as required by the 
 type of approach or to enhance situational awareness. 
 c. Set ESIS final approach course if applicable.

#7. “ALTIMETERS” PF and PM set destination altimeter setting in PFDs and ESIS. “___.___ SET” (PF) 
 “___.___ SET” (PM)

#8. “MINIMUMS” Sets BARO MIN to appropriate MDA, DH or DA and RA MIN to “____ SET” (PF) 
 appropriate HAT or HAA. PF shall display Barometric Altimeter “____ SET” (PM) 
 mins and the PM shall display Radar Altimeter minimums. During 
 VFR pattern work, PF shall set the Barometric Altimeter minimums 
 to 500’ above field elevation. PM shall set the Radar Altimeter 
 minimums to 500’.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-24 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
 The BARO MINS will not display in the Altitude Select window on 
 the PFD unless CPL arrow is selected to the side with BARO MINS 
 selected.

#9. “CREW” PF briefs PM on the approach and landing in accordance with the “BRIEFED” (PF) 
 NATOPS, FTI, and Typical Briefs and Voice Procedures Appendix. 
 At the completion of the brief, PF asks “Any questions?” PF answers 
 questions and then responds “Briefed.” PF ensures crew and 
 passengers are ready for landing.

For VFR arrival, brief course rules or visual arrival. Brief Touch and 
 Go prior to landing.

WARNING 
 Overriding the landing gear warning horn in the traffic pattern or during 
 final segment of an instrument approach could result in a landing gear-up 
 landing.

Note 
 Cancellation of the wheels warning horn shall be at the direction of the 
 Pilot flying; however, shall not be overridden in the traffic pattern or 
 during the final segment of an instrument approach.

“APPROACH CHECKLIST COMPLETE” or “ABBREVIATED APPROACH CHECKLIST COMPLETE” (PM)

“LANDING CHECKLIST” (PF)

F110. LANDING CHECKLIST

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
The Landing Checklist shall be completed prior to every landing. The Landing Checklist is 
never held. If the checklist is interrupted for any reason, restart the checklist from step 1. It 
must be completed no later than the 90 position or 1 mile on a straight-in.

Verify the aircraft speed is within limits before changing configuration.

If APPROACH flaps are required, as determined by the maneuver being conducted, PF ensures 
speed 174 KIAS or less prior to calling for APPROACH flaps by stating “Speed checks, flaps 
approach.” PM ensures 174 KIAS or less and places the flap handle to the APPROACH 
position.

PF verifies 155 KIAS or less and calls “Speed checks, landing gear down, Landing 
Checklist” when calling for the landing gear handle to be placed down. PM ensures 155 KIAS 
or less and states “Speed checks, landing gear down.” RS ensures 155 KIAS or less and places 
the landing gear handle down. PM commences the Landing Checklist.

On subsequent passes in the pattern, when the landing gear remains down, it is acceptable to call 
for the “Landing Checklist”. It is not required to call for “landing gear down” prior to calling 
for the Landing Checklist.

If the landing gear is retracted or the Landing Checklist is interrupted for any reason, the entire 
checklist must be completed again prior to landing.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-25 
1. “FLAPS” PF checks that the flap handle and flap indicator agree and reports “Up/Approach/Down” 
 position. A visual check of flap position is not required unless (as required) (PF) 
 experiencing a malfunction. 
 “Up/Approach/Down” 
 (PM)

2. “LANDING GEAR” PF waits for all three indicators to turn green and the light in the “DOWN/LOCKED” 
 landing gear handle to go out, then reports “Down/locked.” PM (PF) 
 verifies three green lights and landing gear handle down with no “DOWN/LOCKED” 
 red light, then responds “Down/locked.” (PM)

3. “LIGHTS” LS turns on the landing and taxi lights. “SET” (LS)

WARNING 
 Overriding the landing gear warning horn in the traffic pattern or 
 during final segment of an instrument approach could result in a 
 gear-up landing.

CAUTION 
 Engagement of the yaw damp during takeoff or landing may result 
 in severe directional control problems.

Before landing, ensure the prop levers are placed to the full forward 
 (HIGH RPM) position. 
Note 
 Under certain meteorological conditions, landing lights may induce 
 vertigo.

“LANDING CHECKLIST COMPLETE” (PM)

“AFTER LANDING CHECKLIST” (PF)

F111. AFTER LANDING CHECKLIST

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
Once clear of the active runway, PM will contact ground for “taxi to my line” per Typical Briefs and 
Voice Procedures Appendix and PF will call for “After Landing Checklist.” This checklist may be 
accomplished during taxi. The checklist shall be completed after each full-stop landing, except when the 
aircraft remains on the runway and a subsequent takeoff is anticipated. The strobe light should be 
energized when crossing all active runways.

1. “CONDITION LEVERS” PF slowly places condition levers to LOW IDLE. Whenever moving “LOW IDLE” (PF). 
 condition levers to LOW IDLE pinch the levers together to prevent 
 inadvertent FUEL CUTOFF. Observe N1 between 51-54% prior to 
 responding.

CAUTION 
 If low idle stop is worn and/or condition levers are moved rapidly 
 back to low idle, a flameout may occur. Returning the condition 
 lever to low idle immediately or adding power with the power levers 
 can result in an overtemp or explosion. If flameout occurs, execute 
 Emergency Shutdown On Deck Checklist, Paragraph 13.2.

2. “TRANSPONDER” RS selects transponder to STBY. “STANDBY” (RS) 
 (as required)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-26 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
Note 
 Busier airports may incorporate ASDE-X and require transponders 
 to remain on for taxi. Consideration should be given to checking 
 appropriate publications for transponder requirements prior to taxiing.

3. “FLAPS” PM selects flaps UP and/or verifies flaps are UP by checking flap “UP” (PM) 
 indicator. A visual check of flap position is not required unless 
 experiencing a malfunction.

4. “AUTO-IGNITION” PM secures or verifies auto-ignition is off. “OFF” (PM)

5. “PRESSURIZATION” PM checks cabin altitude indicating field elevation (approximately “CHECKED/ 
 same as barometric altimeter) and differential indicating zero. This DEPRESSURIZED” 
 ensures the cabin is not pressurized. If cabin is still pressurized, (PM) 
 select DUMP.

6. “BLEED AIR VALVES” RS closes bleed air valves. If PF desires for the valves to remain “CLOSED” (RS) 
 open, PF briefs PM to leave the valves open. This would only be (as required) 
 applicable if taxiing back for takeoff and the cabin door is not to be 
 opened.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
7. “LIGHTS” LS places the following switches OFF: “SET” (LS) 
 a. LEFT and RIGHT LANDING (as required) 
 b. TAXI (leave on if low visibility or night) 
 c. NAV (leave on if low visibility or night) 
 d. ICE (turn on in line area to illuminate wingtips at night) 
 e. STROBE (clear all active runways prior to securing strobes)

8. “ANTI-ICE/DEICE” LS secures all anti-ice/deice equipment. Leave left and right fuel “OFF” (LS) 
 control heat on if further flight is intended.

“AFTER LANDING CHECKLIST COMPLETE” (PM)

“SECURE CHECKLIST” (PF)

F112. SECURE CHECKLIST

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
The Secure Checklist is initiated when the PF calls for the "Secure Checklist”. Steps #1 
through 6 may be completed as time permits after clearing the active runway provided there is 
intent to secure both engines upon completion of the taxi. Steps #7 through 20 are accomplished 
in the parking spot when the PF-calls "continue the checklist”. At a hot spot or quarterdeck, 
when only one engine needs to be secured to facilitate loading/unloading of personnel or cargo 
and a restart is intended after a short period, the abbreviated Secure Checklist, identified by 
pound signs (#), may be utilized. During periods when one engine is secured, the operating 
engine should be run at high idle to support generator load requirements. When utilizing the 
abbreviated Secure Checklist, Steps #11 and #12 apply only to the engine being secured.

#1. “TRANSFER PUMPS” LS selects both transfer pumps OFF. “OFF” (LS)

2. “CROSSFEED” LS selects crossfeed CLOSED. “CLOSED” (LS) 
3. “AUTO-FEATHER” LS selects Auto-feather OFF. “OFF” (LS)

#4. “FUEL CONTROL HEAT” LS selects both fuel control heat switches OFF. “OFF” (LS)

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-27 
5. “TRIM” PM manually sets elevator, aileron, and rudder trim “SET zero” (PM) 
 wheels to zero.

6. “ESIS” PM selects ESIS power button OFF. “OFF” (PM)

PM Checks in with Base “Safe on deck” and contacts Pegasus “Hold the checklist." 
 base for a parking spot. (PF)

When reaching the parking spot and lineman gives the “Stop” signal, “Continue the 
 PF will tell the PM to “continue the checklist.” checklist.” (PF)

#7. “PARKING BRAKE” LS pulls brake handle and pumps brake pedals until firm. “SET” (LS)

8. “AUX BATT/ PF selects AUX BATT and AVIONICS MASTER switches “OFF” (PF) 
 AVIONICS MASTER” to OFF.

#9. “CABIN TEMPERATURE RS selects cabin temperature mode OFF. “OFF” (RS) 
 MODE”

#10. “VENT BLOWER” RS selects vent blower AUTO. “AUTO” (RS) 
#11. “PROP(S)” PF ensures both power levers at flight idle then pulls both prop levers “FEATHER” (PF) 
 into FEATHER and ensures prop RPM immediately starts to decrease. 
 There is no need to wait for the Props to fully feather before continuing 
 the checklist.

#12. “CONDITION LEVERS” PF ensures ITT has been below 650 °C for at least 1 minute. PF “FUEL CUTOFF” (PF) 
 places both hands on condition levers and selects FUEL CUTOFF. 
 PF notes ITT decrease and monitors engine instruments for a normal 
 shutdown.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
13. “BOOST PUMPS” LS selects both boost pumps OFF. “OFF” (LS)

14. “FUEL QUANTITY LS notes normal indication on left and right fuel quantity “CHECKED” (LS) 
 (CURRENT LIMITERS)” indicators. If either indicator reads zero, a current limiter 
 is blown.

15. “OXYGEN” Push Oxygen Push/Pull knob and release pressure by placing diluter “CLOSED” (PM) 
 control lever on the mask regulator in the “EMER” position until O2 
 hose indicator is red. Then set diluter control lever back to “100%.”

16. “LIGHTS” Turn off all interior and exterior lights except NAV (if required) “OFF” (LS) 
 and BEACON. Turn NAV (if required) and BEACON off once 
 prop rotation has stopped. Return all rheostats to the full 
 counter-clockwise position. Turn OFF both utility lights and return them 
 to the overhead receptacle.

17. “GANGBAR” “OFF” (LS)

18. “CONTROL LOCK” LS installs control lock. Ensure chain is not tangled, then: “INSTALLED” (LS) 
a. Place guard around engine prop controls, open side facing aft. (as required) 
b. Pull control wheel aft until hole is visible in tube. Push control 
wheel forward approximately 2 inches until hole in the tube and 
hole in column are lined up. Slide pin into top hole. Route 
chain around left side of column. 
c. Adjust rudder pedals fully aft. Displace left pedal slightly 
Forward and slide metal rod into cylinder on right pedal.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-28 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
Push rod into cylinder enough to allow clearance to move 
Left pedal back to original position. Slide rod into cylinder 
on left pedal.

19. “WHEELS” “CHOCKED” (LS)

20. “PARKING BRAKE” Release the parking brake upon receiving confirmation that the “RELEASED” (LS) 
 wheels are chocked. Consideration should be given to keeping (as required) 
 the parking brake set if parked on an icy or inclined ramp and/or 
 prop wash/jet blast are present.

“SECURE CHECKLIST COMPLETE” (PM)

F113. EMERGENCIES

In the simulator and the aircraft, emphasis is placed on proper handling of emergencies. Selected 
emergency procedures (bordered by a black cross-hatched margin) can be found on the reverse 
side of the normal checklist. Asterisked items must be committed to memory. Daggered items 
require concurrence of both pilots before taking the required action.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
The need for concurrence before switch or control lever actuation is paramount since incorrect 
action will most likely jeopardize safety to a greater degree. The PF must announce intended 
action, and then pause for the PM to concur before manipulating a switch or control. Only point 
to the switch or lever. Do not grab or “hook” it. 
Failure to follow this sequence may result in fuel cutoff or prop feathering of the operating 
engine. Timely and correct procedure execution, such as identifying “left” or “right” firewall 
valves, is essential for the safety of the aircraft, crew, and passengers. Practice engine shutdown 
procedures in the simulator, moving all related switches and valves as required.

Before securing an engine or immediately after an unexpected power loss, the “power up, rudder 
up, clean up” technique may be utilized.

Power up

Utilize available power as required to maintain airspeed or prevent loss of airspeed.

Rudder up

Utilize rudder as required to stop the heading change and maintain balanced flight. Trim out 
control pressures as time permits.

Clean up

Raise landing gear and flaps as required. 
When performing the Emergency Shutdown Checklist, do not configure or call for the landing 
checklist until the required memory items have been completed.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F 
T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” F-29 
Accomplish Execution of the Emergency Engine Shutdown Checklist in the following manner 
(the word simulate will be used only during SSE training in the aircraft):

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
PF PM 
Brief intentions. “This will be an emergency shutdown of the left/right engine.” Identify the engine to be shut down and place 
finger on appropriate power lever. PM visually confirms correct power lever 
“Left/right Power lever – Idle, concur?” 
Slowly retard power lever to idle. “Concur” 
Identify prop to feather and point to appropriate prop lever. PM visually confirms correct prop lever and 
guards against inadvertent movement 
“Left/right Propeller – Feather, concur?” “Concur” or “Simulate” 
Identify condition lever to close and point to appropriate condition lever. PM visually confirms correct 
condition lever and guards against inadvertent movement. IP may add power to the “feathered” engine to 
simulate decrease in drag. “Left/right Condition lever – Fuel Cutoff concur?” “Concur” or “Simulate” 
NOTE: If Prop Fails to Feather, at this point in the checklist, execute the Alternate Prop Feathering Checklist. If the prop is feathered, or upon completing the Alternate Prop Feather Checklist, pull the prop levers to 
1900 RPM and continue the Emergency Shutdown Checklist in case of a confirmed/suspected fire or fuel 
leak. If there is no confirmed/suspected fire or fuel leak, continue with step 7 of the checklist. PF will continue with the checklist if fire or fuel leak related.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
See Chapter One for specific circumstances 
when the checklist does not need to be continued for a fire or fuel leak. The procedures for executing the 
remaining memory items are described below: 
Identify appropriate firewall valve by verifying corresponding power lever at idle. Because the firewall 
valves are aligned fore and aft, it is easy to make a mistake. Do not rush. Point at the appropriate firewall 
valve. PM visually confirms correct valve. “Left/right firewall valve closed, concur?” “Concur” or “Simulate” 
PF, PM decide if the fire extinguisher is required and point to the appropriate extinguisher. PF, PM visually 
confirm correct extinguisher. “Left fire extinguisher discharge, concur?” 
or 
“Left fire extinguisher not required, concur?” 
“Concur” or “Simulate,” 
or 
“Concur” 
Or 
If the shutdown was caused by a fire light or a fuel leak on the side opposite the PF, the PF will direct the PM 
of the action to take. The PM will then point to the fire extinguisher and state the appropriate action. “Discharge right fire extinguisher.” 
“Concur” 
or 
“Right fire extinguisher not required, concur?” 
“Right fire extinguisher discharge, concur?” 
or 
“Concur” 
PF identifies left/right bleed air valve and directs PM to close bleed air valve or performs the action if flying 
as RS after receiving concurrence from PM. Concur.” PM closes bleed air valve or simulates closing.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX F
chunks.text:
“Close left/right bleed air valve” “Left/right bleed air valve closed, concur?” 
“Concur”

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX F MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
F-30 T-44C COCKPIT PROCEDURES “HOLLYWOOD CHECKLIST” 
Decide if the rest of the checklist is required. 
“Continue the checklist” PM continues as directed. 
If an engine fails or requires shutdown, complete the Emergency Shutdown Checklist, turn toward the nearest 
suitable field, and declare an emergency. If it is determined a restart of the affected engine may be 
accomplished in case of an unrelated emergency, then you may complete the Starter Assisted Air-start 
Checklist up to and including “Generator Off.” Complete the Approach and Landing Checklists per the FTI.

Figure F-1 Emergency Engine Shutdown Checklist

When handling a fire warning light that has no secondary indications, SMAs shall provide the 
instructor with sound reasoning and their risk assessment if they elect to leave the engine 
running. NATOPS states, “Even if no secondary indications are observed, consideration should 
be given to shutdown of the affected engine.”

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: THREAT AND ERROR MANAGEMENT (CRM/ORM) G-1
chunks.text:
THREAT AND ERROR MANAGEMENT (CRM/ORM) G-1 
APPENDIX G 
THREAT AND ERROR MANAGEMENT (CRM/ORM)

G100. INTRODUCTION

The most essential learning behaviors in the Multi-Engine Pilot Training System are engine out 
training, instrument flying, and the use of Threat and Error Management (TEM). This Appendix 
is designed to assist in the development of sound Crew Resource Management (CRM) 
techniques that will maximize safety and efficiency in follow on aircraft. Keep in mind that 
CRM is only one important aspect of Threat and Error Management.

NATOPS chapter 22 must be thoroughly understood and utilized in order to safely accomplish 
required training events. This Appendix will assist in the application of procedures outlined in 
NATOPS.

G101. CRM WITH AUTOMATION

Due to the complexity of modern aircraft avionics systems, CRM is an essential tool to eliminate 
task saturation, minimize heads down time by both pilots, and increase situational awareness. 
The below procedures will help maximize safe operation of automation.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: THREAT AND ERROR MANAGEMENT (CRM/ORM) G-1
chunks.text:
In the T-44C, there are multiple methods to fly the same procedure. This can create confusion 
between pilots when it is time to fly a departure, route, STAR, or approach. It is imperative to be 
on the same page about the level of automation to be used during all phases of flight. Therefore, 
the following techniques will enhance the use of automation through CRM and standardize the 
usage of the flight director during advanced multi-engine training.

1. The mantra for flight director and autopilot usage is select and verify. Ensure that the 
proper flight mode is selected then verified on the Flight Guidance System Display to confirm 
that the anticipated mode of operation is either activated or in sequence for activation.

2. Other than the go-around mode, the direction for Flight Director inputs by the Pilot Flying 
and the manipulation of the Flight Guidance Panel by the Pilot Monitoring shall not be 
conducted until the Pilot Flying establishes a positive rate of climb, the landing gear is raised, 
props are brought to 1900 RPM, and the aircraft is climbing through 400 feet AGL and clear of 
traffic.

3. Be concise about what is desired for the flight director to display. A technique is to use the 
term “select” for mode selection (HDG, NAV, APPR, VNAV, FLC, VS etc.). Use the term “set” 
for headings, altitudes, speeds, and vertical speeds.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: THREAT AND ERROR MANAGEMENT (CRM/ORM) G-1
chunks.text:
4. Pilots must remain vigilant about aircraft control including airspeed, courses, and altitudes. 
The biggest dangers of automation are complacency and trusting systems too much. Flight 
directors are garbage in = garbage out systems, meaning if false information is selected it can be 
a distraction or dangerous to flight operation. If the flight director or autopilot is detracting from 
safety of flight, turn them off and fly manually.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX G MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX G MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
G-2 THREAT AND ERROR MANAGEMENT (CRM/ORM) 
5. During flight director use, there may be times with the PM is unable to select desired 
modes in a timely fashion because of task saturation. Do not delay the execution of a clearance 
because of slow actuation of the Flight Guidance Panel.

6. Ensure the PM is briefed about the expectations for mode selection before an approach or 
go-around. Briefing the expected modes will ensure timely and accurate FGP operation and 
consequently add to CRM and safety.

CAUTION

PF and PM shall never both be heads down in the cockpit at the 
same time.

Flight Guidance Panel Operation. Each pilot will normally operate the corresponding CDI 
selector knob during all phases of flight and ground operation and at all levels of automation 
usage. During ground operations, the PF will operate the FGP. During flight with the autopilot 
off, the PF will delegate FGP control to the PM. With the autopilot on, the PF will operate the 
FGP. Inputs made to the FGP will be verbalized to the crew.

Anytime the FGP captures a new active mode (green) the crew should be alerted. The PF should 
verbalize to the crew active (green) and armed (white) modes displayed on the PFD.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX G MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
Flight Director Assisted approaches. For any approaches where the flight director is planned 
on being used, the PF should brief the modes of planned operation during the approach brief or 
any time before the approach is commenced.

Example: TACAN 13R NGP. PF states, “This will be a FD assisted approach using NAV and 
VS 800 on final.”

Approach FGP PFD 
LATERAL VERTICAL LATERAL VERTICAL 
VOR, TACAN, 
LNAV LOCALIZER 
(MDA) 
NAV FLC, VS VOR, TACAN, 
LOC, FMS 
FLC, VS 
ILS (DH) APPR N/A APP LOC 
1/LOC 2 
GS 
LNAV/VNAV (DA) APPR VNAV APPR FMS VGP 
NOTE: FGP modes should be selected prior to FAF when “cleared approach.” PFD modes 
are expected from FAF to MAP or RWY.

Figure G-1 T-44C Flight Director Matrix

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX G
chunks.text:
MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX G 
THREAT AND ERROR MANAGEMENT (CRM/ORM) G-3 
FMS OPERATION. In order to minimize incorrect inputs to the FMS and pilot “heads down” 
time, all crews shall follow the following procedures during FMS operation.

1. When the aircraft is moving, whether on the deck or in-flight, FMS data entry will be 
accomplished by the PM.

2. When on the ground, parked, with the parking brake set, either pilot may enter data into the 
FMS.

3. PF shall fly the aircraft and maintain a dedicated heads-up lookout. If the PF wishes to be 
heads-down for an extended period of time, aircraft control shall be transferred to the PM 
who shall remain heads-up.

4. If the PM must divert attention away from normal clearing and monitoring duties for an 
extended period of time, they shall state, “heads-down.” Verbal acknowledgment from 
the PF is necessary to prevent both pilots from being heads-down at the same time.

5. Both pilots shall not be heads-down at the same time. Any crewmember that observes both 
pilots heads-down at the same time shall alert the PF without delay.

6. Crewmembers shall verbalize when they are “heads-up” after completing the heads-down 
task. The PF shall acknowledge this call and brief any status changes.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION APPENDIX G
chunks.text:
7. After data entry, points should be verified by the instructor prior to pressing the execute 
button. This duty may be delegated to the SMA if the IP feels it necessary.

8. Either pilot will verbalize the need to enter a discontinuity. The PM will enter the 
discontinuity and advise the PF when the entry is complete.

9. Either pilot will verbalize the need to close up a discontinuity. The PM will receive 
concurrence from the PF prior to closing up the discontinuity.

10. Either pilot will verbalize the need to enter or edit a waypoint. The PM will enter the 
waypoint and execute it after receiving concurrence from the PF.

11. The PM should monitor flight progress via the LEGS page, especially in the Terminal 
environment.

12. Either pilot should verbalize the fact that an annunciator has illuminated. The PM looks up 
amplifying information, if necessary. The pilots should discuss the situation to determine 
what actions may be required.

13. In response to the FMS Approach Mode active (CDI accuracy ±.3 nautical mile and “GPS 
APPR” on PFD,) PF will verbalize “Approach Mode Active” and the other pilot will 
acknowledge the call.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX G MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX G MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
G-4 THREAT AND ERROR MANAGEMENT (CRM/ORM) 
14. After a missed approach, the PF will request approach reselection. The PM will 
acknowledge and comply, then inform the PF when approach reselection is complete.

G102. COMMUNICATION

The most important behavior of CRM is communication. Communication can happen in many 
forms; non-verbal, verbal, within the cockpit, over the radio, and through specific maneuvers 
between aircraft or individuals on the ground. Precise and timely communication is critical 
during non-proceduralized phases of flight. For instance, during a circling approach the PF 
needs to communicate his intensions on how the maneuver will be performed so the PM 
understands what is expected and can properly back up the PF. Effective communication will 
enhance situational awareness, safety, and mission effectiveness.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX G MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
Mandatory Callouts. The mandatory callouts listed in NATOPS are designed to minimize error 
and enhance situational awareness. The callouts, however, will not address every possible 
instance in which communication will be needed, they provide only a framework for good 
communication and risk management. Additionally, there will be times in which safety of flight 
overrides the importance of callouts. Remember to aviate, navigate, then communicate. An 
improper rudder input or power correction is far more dangerous than forgetting to announce 
1000 ft to level.

Missed Callouts. During advanced instrument rides, students can expect instructors to 
intentionally “miss” required callouts. This will be used to verify that students are not 
completely relying on the callouts for situational awareness and basic air work. When noticing 
the PM has missed a callout, the PF should question the PM of the missed call-in order to ensure 
both pilots agree with the position of the aircraft in the respective phase of flight. For example, 
if the PM does not call “1000 to level,” the PF should question the PM on the missed call to 
ensure that the PM agrees that you are in fact 1000 ft to level off altitude.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX G MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
Deviations. As deviations from planned parameters are noticed, the PM’s call should be 
commensurate to the level or extent of the deviation. For instance, if the PF is 10 knots slow, the 
PM should state “Airspeed.” The PF will state “Correcting.” If the PF continues to operate 
outside of parameters, the PM should add extra verbiage to the next call and/or direct a required 
action for the PF, “Airspeed, 10 knots slow” or “Airspeed 10 knots slow, add power.” The 
amount of direction and extra verbiage stated by the PM should be commensurate to the extent of 
the deviation. Finally, be familiar with the two-challenge rule in NATOPS. Keep in mind that 
the two-challenge rule is designed for breakdowns in communication and safety of flight issues.

Instrument approaches. Callouts are most critical during terminal phases of flight. The 
following figures are designed to be used as an aid for making required calls during instrument 
approaches. The approach for the below examples is the ILS/LOC 13 at CRP.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MINIMUM CONTROLLABLE AIRSPEED (VMCA) H-1
chunks.text:
MINIMUM CONTROLLABLE AIRSPEED (VMCA) H-1 
APPENDIX H 
MINIMUM CONTROLLABLE AIRSPEED (VMCA)

H100. INTRODUCTION

Vmca is the minimum speed at which directional control can be maintained with an engine 
inoperative. This speed is established by the manufacturer under the following criteria specified 
by the Certifying Authority (FAA).

1. Takeoff power set on the operating engine

2. Standard Day (Temperature 15 degrees C and Pressure 29.92" at Sea Level)

3. Maximum takeoff weight

4. Critical engine windmilling (left engine in the T-44)

5. Flaps at takeoff setting

6. Landing gear Up

7. 5 degrees AOB into the operating engine

8. Maximum allowable aft cg.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MINIMUM CONTROLLABLE AIRSPEED (VMCA) H-1
chunks.text:
86 KIAS is the published Vmca speed for the T-44 under the above conditions; however, engine 
failures don't only occur under this exact set of conditions and therefore, the actual Vmca in any 
particular situation may be either more or less than the published value. With regard to the eight 
variables, you should notice that except for the angle of bank, gross weight, and standard day 
conditions, all of the remaining items are the worst case (they increase Vmca) or are related to 
the takeoff scenario. 86 KIAS is considered a “worst case scenario” and staying above this 
airspeed should allow controllability in a single engine (SE) scenario, it does not guarantee climb 
performance or even safe stall margin. The following is an explanation of some of the factors 
and how they affect Vmca.

Banking into the good engine three to five degrees lowers Vmca by vectoring lift to counter yaw 
(effectively increasing the horizontal component of lift) and also by reducing sideslip. Reducing 
the sideslip yields greater rudder effectiveness, making possible better control of yaw at slower 
airspeeds. Conversely leveling the wings or banking away from the good engine will increase 
Vmca and should be done with caution in a SE scenario.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: MINIMUM CONTROLLABLE AIRSPEED (VMCA) H-1
chunks.text:
Maximum weight also decreases Vmca. As you may recall from basic aerodynamics, the lift an 
aircraft generates must equal the weight. Thus, a lightly loaded aircraft will generate less total 
lift than a heavier loaded aircraft. Also recall that the total lift is the sum of the vertical and 
horizontal lift components. Since lightly loaded aircraft generate less lift, there is less horizontal 
lift (when 3-5 degrees of bank is applied) to control the yaw. The lightly loaded aircraft would 
therefore require more airflow over the rudder to control the yaw which necessitates a higher 
Vmca speed.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX H MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
APPENDIX H MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION 
H-2 MINIMUM CONTROLLABLE AIRSPEED (VMCA) 
Maximum power produces the greatest yaw and roll toward the dead engine. In addition, 
conditions such as denser air, lower altitudes, and lower temperatures that increase engine 
performance will increase Vmca. The good news is pilots have direct control over this 
condition. Reducing power on the good engine reduces asymmetric thrust and lowers Vmca; 
however, keep in mind how reducing power will affect climb performance and stall margin. The 
pilot must be sure to maintain adequate margin above both stall and Vmca speed at all times 
when single engine.

A rearward or aft C.G. reduces the lever arm between the C.G. and the rudder. Recall that an 
airplane rotates about its C.G. along all three axes (in all three planes). The shorter the rudder 
arm, the more rudder that is required to counteract yaw, so rudder effectiveness is at a minimum, 
which necessitates higher airspeeds in order to increase the airflow over the rudder to maintain 
control and therefore the higher Vmca.

chunks.title: FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION T-44C
chunks.heading: APPENDIX H MULTI-ENGINE FLIGHT TRAINING INSTRUCTION
chunks.text:
The flaps in the takeoff position and the landing gear up are stipulated because they are 
indicative to the takeoff scenario. The landing gear and the landing gear doors extended tend to 
act like rudders and act to decrease Vmca. Vmca will increase as we raise the landing gear. The 
flaps add drag and help resist the yawing moments set up by the operating engine.

All multi-engine pilots must have a thorough knowledge of Vmca and how it is affected by the 
current conditions. Having this knowledge will allow a pilot to recognize when an aircraft is 
approaching Vmca and diagnose the best course of action to facilitate a successful recovery. In 
order to successfully recover from Vmca, airspeed must be increased or the parameters must be 
changed, e.g., less asymmetric power, propeller feathered, etc.

See FAA_P-8740-66 (faasafety.gov) “Flying Light Twins Safely” and the Aerodynamics 
Workbook for more information on Vmca.

Normal oil pressure is 85 to 100 PSIG at power settings above 27,000 rpm (72%) N1, oil pressure below 85 PSIG is undesirable, and may be used only
for completion of a flight, and then at a reduced power setting. Low oil pressure should be corrected prior to next flight. During ground operations, oil pressures
below 40 PSIG require engine shutdown; during flight, oil pressure below 40 PSIG is unsafe and requires either engine shutdown or use of minimum power 
until a landing can be made. DECREASE VMO 4 KIAS FOR EVERY 1,000 FT ABOVE 15,500 FT COOLING PERIOD AFTER FIRST STARTER CYCLE: 60

4. This value is time limited to two seconds. If ITT is likely to exceed 925 °C, discontinue start. 5. For every 10 °C below −30 °C ambient temperature, reduce maximum allowable N1 by 2.2 %. 6. High ITT may be decreased by reducing accessory load and/or increasing N1 speed. 7. High generator loads at low N1 speeds may cause the ITT acceleration temperature limit to be exceeded. Observe the generator load limits. 8. This power rating is intended for emergency use at the discretion of the pilot. 9. Torque limits between 1,900 and 2,200 rpm vary linearly between 1,315 and 1,520 ft-lb. 10.
